summary: Chelan County PUD enacted a moratorium on power requests of 1 MW or more in December 2014, prompted by influx of bitcoin miners drawn to cheap hydropower. Led to creation of a High Density Load rate in 2016. A second moratorium was imposed March 2018 when applications surged again. 28+ unauthorized operations identified; 19 shut down with $5K-10K enforcement fees.
objective: Pause crypto mining in Chelan County PUD pending regulations

summary: East Wenatchee Council passed a 6-month interim zoning ordinance restricting cryptocurrency operations to Central Business and General Commercial districts only, and prohibiting use of cargo containers for crypto mining. Part of a broader regional wave: Wenatchee, Leavenworth, Entiat, Waterville, and Chelan also passed emergency controls.
objective: Ban crypto mining from residential zones in East Wenatchee

summary: Franklin County Commission denied rezoning for Red Dog Technologies' proposed Bitcoin mining data center near Tullahoma. Residents said noise would be unbearable and damage property values. The denial was one of the early examples of local opposition to crypto mining data centers in Tennessee.
objective: Deny Red Dog Technologies rezoning for data center in Franklin County

summary: Groton Planning and Zoning Commission placed a 1-year moratorium on data centers larger than 5,000 sq ft in June 2022. Previously, data centers were classified as "telecommunications facilities." Replaced by permanent data center zoning regulations adopted July 2023 (Section 5.1-6.F). Groton now has an active data center Host Municipality Fee Agreement.
objective: Impose 1-year data centers moratorium in Town of Groton

summary: Clay County unanimously approved a permanent ban on commercial cryptocurrency mining facilities in August 2022. Prompted by noise, electricity, and waste problems from crypto mines in neighboring Cherokee County. Residential/personal crypto mining is exempted.
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Clay County

summary: Henderson County passed a 60-day moratorium on cryptocurrency mining on May 1, 2023, citing noise, energy, and e-waste concerns. The moratorium was replaced by permanent Land Development Code amendments establishing zoning requirements for crypto mining. State Sen. Tim Moffitt also sponsored SB 774 granting Henderson, Polk, and Rutherford counties authority to regulate crypto mining.
objective: Impose 60-day crypto mining moratorium in Henderson County

summary: Buncombe County (Asheville area) unanimously passed a 1-year moratorium on cryptocurrency mining on May 2, 2023. Extended in May 2024 for another year through May 2025. The county developed permanent regulations to replace the moratorium. Commissioner Terri Wells proposed the moratorium after hearing complaints from Cherokee County residents.
objective: Impose 1-year crypto mining moratorium in Buncombe County

summary: Harrison passed a moratorium on data centers and crypto mining on May 25, 2023 after hundreds of residents opposed a Green Digital LLC crypto facility (a subsidiary with Chinese Communist Party ownership ties). The moratorium was actively removed on Nov 28, 2023 (~6 months in) after Arkansas Act 851 ("Right to Mine" law) preempted local regulation of crypto mining.
objective: Impose 6 month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in City of Harrison (Green Digital)

summary: Roundhouse Digital Infrastructure planned a $100M, 10MW data center at the Port of Cascade Locks in the Columbia River Gorge, about 40 miles east of Portland. The facility would have more than doubled the area's power draw. Residents raised concerns about electricity supply impacts, the developer's lack of experience, and unclear funding. Opposition was a key issue in local elections that saw voters recall two officials who backed the project, with two other data center advocates losing re-election bids. Following the recall, the new port authority board canceled the project in July 2023. Friends of the Columbia Gorge was a key opposition group.
objective: Recall officials who approved Roundhouse Digital data center

opposition_groups: Concerned Citizens of Waterford and East Lyme (CCWEL)
summary: CT Siting Council denied Dominion Energy's petition to modify Millstone Power Station boundaries for NE Edge's 1.5M sq ft hyperscale data center (300MW, ~15% of reactor output). Concerned Citizens of Waterford and East Lyme (CCWEL) organized opposition.
objective: Deny NE Edge 300MW data center at Millstone Power Station

summary: Santa Clara Planning Commission denied a 72MW data center permit at 2805 Bowers Ave (4-2 vote). Chair noted the city has 50+ data centers consuming 60% of its energy and asked 'when is it too many?' GI Partners appealed; City Council approved the project Aug 29, 2024.
objective: Uphold denial of GI Partners data center on appeal

summary: Applied Digital proposed a 200MW, $1.5B data center adjacent to a NextEra substation. Supervisors drafted a 1,200ft setback ordinance but put it on hold after the third reading was not completed. The county has no comprehensive zoning plan.
objective: Impose 1200-foot data center setbacks in Adair County

summary: Tract withdrew its $14B, 1,000-acre 'Project Range' datacenter proposal in western Maricopa County in May 2024 after the adjacent cities of Goodyear and Buckeye formally opposed the rezoning, citing incompatibility with surrounding residential neighborhoods. Building heights and noise were key concerns raised by city staff.
objective: Block Tract data center through opposition in Maricopa County

opposition_groups: Stop Duneland and Valpo/Wheeler Data Centers
summary: Provident Realty Advisors proposed a $1.3B data center on the 200-acre former Brassie Golf Course site along Pearson Road. The plan called for eight buildings at 150,000 sq ft each with backup generators. At a Memorial Day meeting, 60-75 residents gathered at Dogwood Park voicing concerns about noise, air quality, water, wetlands, wildlife, property values, and mental health impacts. The developer proposed landscaping buffers and noise suppressors. The Town Council declared it could 'never support this project' at its current scale, and Provident abandoned the proposal on June 10, 2024. The developer subsequently moved to Burns Harbor with a similar proposal.
objective: Community pressure forces Provident Realty Advisors to withdraw in Town of Chesterton

summary: DeKalb County Commission adopted 5,000-foot distance requirements for cryptocurrency mining, quarries, landfills, and other nuisance uses under the Tennessee County Powers Act. Prompted by a crypto mining operation near Smithville and inquiry about a nuclear-powered data center.
objective: Impose 5000-foot data center setbacks in DeKalb County

summary: Clinton County enacted a moratorium on wind project applications on June 10, 2024, extended multiple times through September 2025. Prompted by NextEra Energy/Clinton County Wind LLC proposal for 80-90 turbines. Replaced by new wind ordinance adopted December 15, 2025 (2-1 vote). Landowners filed lawsuit in Feb 2026 challenging the new ordinance.
objective: Pause wind energy projects in Clinton County pending regulations

summary: On September 3, 2024, the Atlanta City Council voted to ban data centers within a half-mile of MARTA rail stops and along the 22-mile Beltline trail loop. Councilmembers Jason Dozier and Matt Westmoreland sponsored the legislation to preserve mixed-use development, housing, trails, and green space in prime neighborhoods like Downtown and Grove Park. Mayor Andre Dickens supported the ban, stating data centers limit jobs, strain utilities, and negatively impact communities. Existing data centers operated by QTS and DataBank in the Beltline Overlay district are grandfathered. The council later rejected a separate data center proposal in December 2024.
objective: Ban data centers near MARTA stops and Beltline in Atlanta

company: Starwood Capital Group (SCG Global Holdings LLC)
summary: Fairfax County Board of Supervisors voted 8-2 on September 10, 2024 to adopt a comprehensive Data Center Zoning Ordinance Amendment. Key provisions: data centers must be built at least 200 feet from residential areas (500 feet for ground-mounted equipment like generators); minimum 1-mile distance from Metro stations; all cooling/ventilating equipment must be fully enclosed; mandatory noise studies before site plan approval; new architectural requirements for building design; 80,000 sq ft size limits in some districts; Special Exception permits required for most new data centers. Site plans accepted before July 16, 2024 are grandfathered under prior rules.
objective: Impose 200-foot data center setbacks in Fairfax County

summary: Morton County adopted an indefinite moratorium on data centers, pending development of siting and performance standards. Separate from a carbon capture moratorium also adopted. Inspired by noise issues from a data center in Williams County that prompted a lawsuit. Preemptive action; no specific data center proposed.
objective: Impose indefinite data centers moratorium in Morton County

summary: Jackson County has enacted multiple data mining facility moratoriums: first on Sept 17, 2024 (Resolution #1066), rescinded Nov 5, 2024, re-enacted March 25, 2025 (Resolution #1105), renewed Sept 9, 2025 (Resolution #1142). The Zoning Commission is working on a permanent Data Processing Center ordinance. Multiple companies were eyeing sites near substations.
objective: Pause data centers in Jackson County pending regulations

summary: Thorndale passed a formal resolution opposing a proposed 15MW bitcoin mining facility by Data Factory Austin on County Road 437 in Milam County. Dozens of residents expressed concerns about noise, water supply, and energy consumption (15MW equals about 10,500 homes' daily electricity). Residents referenced noise complaints from Data Factory's existing 10MW mine near Rockdale, where noise peaked at 99 decibels — louder than a subway. The city signed the resolution to show solidarity with citizens and sent it to local and state leaders to prevent construction.
objective: Pass resolution opposing Data Factory Austin bitcoin mine

summary: Peculiar originally added a 'data center' definition to its light industrial zoning, enabling Diode Ventures' proposed $1.5B Harper Road Technology Park — a 500-acre campus with multiple buildings next to homes and businesses. A Facebook group called 'Don't Dump Data in Peculiar' grew to nearly 1,000 members (about 1/6 of the city's population). After hundreds of residents called on local leaders to reverse course, the Planning Commission Board announced on October 3, 2024 an ordinance removing data centers from the zoning code, effectively blocking the project. The mayor claimed aldermen were pressured by a 'boisterous and ill-informed group.'
objective: Restrict Diode Ventures 500-acre data center in City of Peculiar

summary: Jones County enacted a moratorium on data center development circa October 2024. The moratorium was replaced by a permanent Cryptocurrency and Data Center Ordinance restricting such facilities to agricultural, commercial, and industrial zoning districts via Special Permitted Use application.
objective: Pause data centers and crypto mining in Jones County pending regulations

summary: US Development filed to rezone 434 acres from agricultural to industrial near Yeehaw Junction for a 7+ million sq ft data center campus. Adjacent to the 27,000-acre DeLuca Preserve, crucial habitat for the endangered Florida grasshopper sparrow.
objective: Block US Development rezoning of 434 acres of farmland

company: Undisclosed (crypto mining facility proposed on Eller Road)
opposition_groups: Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining
Gilmer County Citizens Against AI Data Centers
summary: Gilmer County in the North Georgia mountains became the only Georgia county to enact a full ban on cryptocurrency mining. The ban followed organized opposition led by Cyndie Roberson, cofounder of 'Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining,' who brought hundreds of residents to a planning commission meeting. A Change.org petition ('Stop Gilmer County from allowing a Crypto Mining facility in our county') gathered signatures. Opponents cited extreme noise, massive electricity and water drain, and insufficient job creation. The county unanimously passed crypto and land use ordinances on first reading.
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Gilmer County

summary: A $3-4 billion, 2.7M sq ft hyperscale data center campus proposed at 199 Servistar Industrial Way. State legislature passed a 20-year sales tax exemption. By Dec 2025, developers appear to have abandoned the project. Conservation Law Foundation raised concerns about insufficient environmental safeguards.
objective: Developers abandon $3-4B Westfield data center campus

summary: The No Desert Data Center Coalition organized fierce opposition to Beale Infrastructure's 750MW Project Blue datacenter in Marana. Despite collecting over 2,800 petition signatures in just four days and filing a lawsuit alleging Open Meeting Act violations, the Marana Town Council unanimously approved the rezoning in January 2026. The coalition has expanded to oppose multiple Southern Arizona datacenter projects and has traveled nationally to share their organizing playbook.
objective: Block Beale Infrastructure rezoning of 600 acres in Town of Marana

company: Applied Digital (reported interest; no formal project filed)
summary: Over 200 people packed the Betty Hagel Memorial Civic Center in Center, ND as Oliver County leaders debated a proposed moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining. The Oliver County Commission voted 3-0 to adopt a moratorium on December 12, 2024, suspending development of all data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities. The Planning & Zoning Board had previously passed the moratorium unanimously. The purpose is to allow the county adequate time to conduct research and develop standards to guide future development. Nearby Mercer County also voted 3-2 to suspend data center development for one year, as concerns spread across western North Dakota.
objective: Halt data centers and crypto mining in Oliver County after Applied Digital proposal

summary: Requires AI data center owners and operators to submit quarterly reports to the Commissioner of Energy and Environmental Protection on energy and water consumption. Authorizes commissioner to adopt regulations concerning water and energy efficiency standards for data centers.
objective: Require quarterly energy and water reports in Connecticut

summary: Black Mountain Power LLC is pursuing a $10B data center on 530+ acres in southeast Fort Worth. Multiple zoning cases with 312 acres approved, additional parcels postponed by City Council citing infrastructure concerns. Four council members called for a data center ordinance. In February 2026, City Council tabled a vote on 42 additional acres after Council member Jeanette Martinez said she was 'not comfortable moving forward' due to a lack of information about infrastructure impacts. The campus would surround Weston Gardens, a landmark whose roots date to the 1930s. Residents cite concerns about water consumption (with Fort Worth already under water restrictions), noise, light, and air pollution.
objective: Restrict Black Mountain Power 530-acre data center in Fort Worth

summary: Originally introduced to create a state siting process for large electricity users (50+ MW). Significantly amended — siting process stripped and replaced with mandate for interim legislative study on impact of large energy consumers on the state's electrical grid. Passed House 89-3, Senate 46-0. Signed April 28, 2025.
objective: Mandate study on large energy consumer grid impacts (HB 1579)

summary: OpenAI's Stargate project — announced by President Trump as a $500B AI infrastructure initiative — is building its first campus on 875 acres in Abilene. The facility will draw up to 1.2 GW of power across eight 500,000 sq ft 'AI factory' halls. A $500M on-site Lancium natural gas power plant will pollute the surrounding community, putting residents at increased risk of asthma and respiratory disease. The project has sparked a housing crisis — thousands of construction workers flooded the city, driving up rents and forcing vulnerable people onto the streets. Despite $100B+ in investment, only 357 permanent jobs are promised (avg salary $57,600). Crusoe received an 85% property tax abatement on billions of infrastructure, causing Abilene to forgo enormous potential revenue. Former school board member Samuel Garcia was among the few vocal opponents questioning water and power impacts.
objective: Oppose Lancium / Crusoe Energy data center over water in Abilene

summary: Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter appealed the Planning Commission's approval of Webb Data Center's 82,000 sq ft facility, arguing the city established zoning guidelines without public input and citing open meeting law violations. On January 22, 2025, the Reno City Council denied the Sierra Club's appeal after multiple contentious meetings, effectively upholding the data center approval. The Webb Data Center will be the first data center within Reno city limits, located in the North Valleys adjacent to an Amazon warehouse. The Reno Planning Commission subsequently recommended a temporary pause on filing and issuing permits for future data centers.
objective: Uphold denial of Webb Data Center data center on appeal

opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Planning
summary: Multiple Minnesota data center bills are in play. SF 608 (94th Legislature, 2025-2026), co-sponsored by Sens. Lieske, Maye Quade, Wesenberg, Gruenhagen, and McEwen (companion bill HF 1109), would prohibit 'enterprise data centers' (800,000+ sq ft) in certain districts. Sen. Jen McEwen (DFL-Duluth) announced she will introduce a separate bill imposing a two-year moratorium on new data center permits while the state studies long-term effects. Sen. Erin Maye Quade (DFL-Apple Valley) plans legislation to prohibit NDAs for data centers and end tax exemptions for big tech. Previously, HF 16 (2025 special session, sponsored by Rep. Greg Davids and Sen. Ann Rest) passed and was signed into law (effective June 15, 2025), establishing initial data center regulations. On February 18-20, 2026, a large rally at the state Capitol drew hundreds of opponents calling for a statewide moratorium.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via SF 608

summary: STREAM Data Centers proposed a $6.3B, 2.2 million sq ft data center complex at the WNY STAMP site in Alabama, NY — adjacent to Tonawanda Seneca Nation treaty-protected territory and complex wetlands with old-growth forest. The Tonawanda Seneca Nation and Sierra Club filed litigation citing environmental and treaty concerns. GCEDC construction caused 500-700 gallon hydraulic drilling fluid spills into Refuge wetlands. In late 2025, the Genesee County Economic Development Center rescinded its approvals, and opponents declared victory. Up to $801M in tax subsidies had been proposed.
objective: Challenge STREAM Data Centers for treaty rights violations

summary: Hitchcock County passed a 1-year moratorium on data center and cryptocurrency mining development via resolution in February 2025. The moratorium was later replaced by permanent regulations.
objective: Impose 1-year data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Hitchcock County

summary: Reno Planning Commission voted 4-2 to recommend a temporary moratorium on new data center permits. The City Council rejected the moratorium despite broad public support at the meeting.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Reno (rejected)

summary: Ada County initiated a Data Center Zoning Ordinance Amendment to develop standards regulating data centers, protecting neighboring property owners and the environment. Prompted by Meta's Kuna campus and the broader Treasure Valley data center boom.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Ada County

summary: Oppidan proposed a seven-building technology campus with five data centers on ~134 acres (1,050,000 sq ft). Planning Commission tabled due to site-related issues and public concerns. Hearing to resume March 18, 2026.
objective: Rally against Oppidan / Connect Data Centers data center in Apple Valley

summary: Clayton County enacted Resolution #8-2025, a moratorium on rezoning requests and conditional use permits for digital asset mining/data centers on February 25, 2025. Extended December 2, 2025 through December 31, 2026. Allamakee-Clayton Electric presented info about potential data mining projects. Concerns: noise, water, property values, fire protection.
objective: Pause data centers and crypto mining in Clayton County pending regulations

company: BlackChamber Group / Fairfield Residential (Innovation Gateway project)
summary: In March 2025, the Loudoun County Board of Supervisors voted 7-2 to approve a comprehensive plan amendment and zoning ordinance amendment eliminating by-right data center development. All new data center applications now require staff-level review, a public hearing, and Planning Commission recommendation. Previously, data centers could be approved with only staff-level review in some zones. Loudoun Climate Project and the Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition (which includes the Piedmont Environmental Council, Sierra Club, and National Parks Conservation Association) pushed for these restrictions.
objective: End by-right data center approvals in Loudoun County

summary: A 341,273 sq ft data center proposed on 143-acre former Princeton Nurseries site along Route 1. Residents raised concerns about electricity consumption, cooling noise, property values, and few permanent jobs.
objective: Oppose data center over noise in South Brunswick

summary: Kootenai County commissioners unanimously approved a 182-day moratorium on data center building permits in March 2025. After the moratorium expired in late August 2025, commissioners adopted strict conditional use permit requirements making it as difficult as possible to build a data center, including prohibiting siting over the Rathdrum Prairie Aquifer, while working toward a potential permanent ban.
objective: Convert Kootenai County moratorium into permanent ban

summary: Grant County PUD imposed load-growth limits after data center demand threatened distribution capacity. Data centers account for 45% of Grant PUD's average load (~800 MW). New high-capacity transmission line from Wanapum Dam to Quincy projected for 2029 completion. Quincy's 53 data centers pay 75% of city property tax revenue.
objective: Cap data center electrical load in Grant County PUD

summary: Nassau County towns imposed moratoriums on battery energy storage systems (BESS). All three towns (Hempstead, North Hempstead, Oyster Bay) have their own bans. County Executive Bruce Blakeman led advocacy. Safety and fire concerns are the primary drivers. State-level bill A8328 proposed to prohibit BESS on Long Island entirely. Note: This is a BESS moratorium, not data center.
objective: Propose battery energy storage systems moratorium in Nassau County

opposition_groups: Faulkner County League of Women Voters
summary: Conway City Council approved MOU with Forgelight Ventures (likely Google) for a $1B, 300,000 sq ft data center generating only 50 jobs. Developer identity hidden behind shell companies. Faulkner County League of Women Voters organized town hall. Concerns about 30-year tax abatements and secrecy.
objective: Mobilize opposition to Forgelight Ventures data center in Conway

summary: Nautilus cancelled its $300M, 60MW data center at the former paper mill (announced 2021). Could not secure the AI customer needed because the facility couldn't produce enough power. The mill site closed permanently in 2008.
objective: Nautilus cancels Millinocket data center over insufficient power

summary: HB 1002 ('Rate Payer Protection Act') prohibits charging residential ratepayers for data center electricity costs (100+ MW threshold) and creates a Special Commission for Data Center Planning. HB 638 ('Equitable Escalation of Electricity Demand Act') requires data centers needing dispatchable power to secure it independently rather than drawing from the existing grid.
objective: Prohibit residential ratepayer subsidies for data centers (HB 1002)

summary: WV House Bill 2014 (2025) created a certified microgrid program and set policies for data centers, stripping local government jurisdiction over 'high-impact' data centers (90+ MW) and diverting property tax revenue. In 2026, bills to restore local control (HB 5590, HB 5620) and require water usage disclosure were introduced but blocked. HB 4983 passed 78-16, bundling rulemaking to limit transparency around data center projects.
objective: Mandate data center water disclosure via HB 5590

summary: Provident Realty Advisors — after being rejected in Chesterton — proposed a seven-building, 102-acre data center campus at the corner of S. Babcock and Haglund Roads on the Worthington Steel property. Residents packed multiple plan commission meetings to oppose the project, citing concerns about building heights (120 feet), inadequate setbacks (50 feet), noise, and impacts on Indiana Dunes National Park. By February 2025, Provident announced it would revise its proposal, but as of May 2025, the company had not filed a rezoning application and the project appeared to have stalled. The data center wave in Indiana communities has slowed significantly due to community pushback.
objective: Block Provident Realty Advisors rezoning of 102 acres in Town of Burns Harbor

summary: Developers proposed converting 1,767 acres approved for ~5,000 homes into industrial/data center use, reducing housing to ~1,350 units. Planning Commission rejected; City Council approved 4-3; Regional Planning denied; Governing Board reversed. Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter led opposition. NBC News covered water concerns.
objective: Oppose StoneGate data center in Reno

summary: Amazon planned a multibillion-dollar data center near the Xcel Sherco coal-fired power plant in Becker, proposing 250 diesel backup generators. In March 2025, the Minnesota PUC ruled Amazon must obtain a Certificate of Need, placing the burden on Amazon to demonstrate necessity and evaluate sustainable alternatives. Environmental advocate Sarah Mooradian of CURE MN argued that even generators not connected to the grid have huge impacts. In May 2025, Amazon abandoned the project, citing uncertain development timelines for permitting. Minnesota also eliminated a sales tax exemption on data center electricity.
objective: Require certificate of need for Amazon data center generators

summary: On June 10, 2025, the Henrico County Board of Supervisors adopted stricter data center requirements, creating a 3,100-acre White Oak Technology Park overlay district and adding noise, lighting, and setback rules. All new hyperscale data centers now require provisional use permits with public hearings. In February 2026, the Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested rights claim for a data center on nearly 200 acres along Darbytown Road in Varina. A key factor: CEO David Wagner did not purchase the site until November 2025, a month after filing his claim in October 2025. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.
objective: Create data center overlay zone in Henrico County

summary: Surge Development withdrew its application to rezone 775 acres of farmland for a data center near Tuttle Orchards, a century-old farm. Over 70 people signed up to speak at a public hearing.
objective: Block Surge Development data center through opposition in Hancock County

summary: Eagle Mountain approved five data centers plus Google and QTS campuses. Meta's 4.5M sq ft campus withdrew 35M+ gallons of water in 2024 under a 2018 confidentiality agreement preventing disclosure. When the city proposed an Alternative Energy Overlay Zone for small modular nuclear reactors, residents pushed back. The Council tabled the plan in Jan. 2026.
objective: Community pressure forces Meta, Google, QTS to withdraw in Eagle Mountain

summary: Little Rock approved annexation and rezoning under the code name 'Project Boar.' AVAIO Digital Partners (Stamford, CT) later announced the 'Leo' campus — a 760-acre site with $6B first phase investment, up to 1 GW capacity, over $21B total buildout, and 500+ construction jobs. The Coalition of Little Rock Neighborhoods organized a March 14 meeting to oppose both the AVAIO and Google $1B data center proposals in the area.
objective: Block Google rezoning of 760 acres in Little Rock

summary: Cielo Digital Infrastructure is investing $2.1 billion in an 86-acre campus off Ford Road in Gaffney, engineered for up to 300MW of IT load. Four facilities of approximately 400,000 sq ft each (1.6M total) with a dedicated on-site electrical substation. The project will create 30 new jobs. Initial operations expected online by year-end 2028. A regional petition opposing Upstate SC data centers has gathered approximately 4,000 signatures.
objective: Restrict Cielo Digital Infrastructure 86-acre data center in Cherokee County

company: University of Michigan / Los Alamos National Laboratory / DTE Energy
project_name: Hydro Park Data Center / AI Supercomputer
summary: A $1.2 billion data center project on South Hydro Park by the University of Michigan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, and DTE Energy would include a 100MW facility for classified national security AI work and a 10MW UMich research facility on 144.5 acres of critical wetland near Ford Lake Dam. The opposition group 'Stop the Data Center' organized assemblies and protests. Ypsilanti Township pushed back, urging relocation.
objective: Rally against University of Michigan data center in Ypsilanti Township

summary: Oakland University's Board of Trustees approved plans for a data center on Parking Lot 35, located behind Hillcrest Residence Hall near the Native American Heritage Site, biological preserve, and nature trail. Student Congress led opposition efforts through an Action Network petition. Students and community members cite concerns about water usage, chemical pollution, and impacts on nearby dorm residents.
objective: Petition to block Oakland University data center in Oakland University

summary: Clark Township enacted a 1-year moratorium on data center development in June 2025, making it one of the earliest communities in Michigan to take preemptive action, months before the broader wave that grew to at least 27 communities. The statewide backlash culminated in bipartisan House Bills 5594-5596 proposing a construction halt through April 2027.
objective: Impose 1-year data centers moratorium in Clark Township

summary: Change.org petition with 67 signatures opposing data center construction in Commercial Point and Scioto Township. The Commercial Point Data Campus is a proposed 152-acre facility marketed by JLL, featuring 1.75 million square feet of space with an initial 75,000 sq ft expanding to 220,000 sq ft. It includes a 1.3 GW natural gas plant and solar array, with 450 MW of grid-connected power planned by 2029. Not affiliated with nearby AWS facilities. Pickaway County Commissioners voted unanimously to deny a 344-acre annexation petition in April 2025. Teays Valley Against Overgrowth (TVAO), founded by Cory Wasmus, is the primary opposition group, citing traffic, noise, and environmental impacts from fossil fuel reliance.
objective: Deny K-NOVA 450MW data center permit in Commercial Point /

summary: La Grange passed a 150-day moratorium on data centers and private utilities on June 2, 2025, later extended by another 150 days. Response to "Project Lincoln," a proposed $6 billion hyperscale data center campus by Western Hospitality Partners on 267 acres of farmland. Massive community backlash led WHP to relocate and downsize. A deputy judge/executive was fired amid corruption allegations related to the deal.
objective: Impose 150-day data centers moratorium in City of La Grange (Western Hospitality Partners)

summary: Millard County Commission unanimously rezoned 1,100+ acres from agricultural to heavy industrial for Creekstone Energy's 10GW data center campus despite significant public opposition. The county is under 100% drought conditions per NOAA. Residents argue the rezone permanently shifts the area's rural character and threatens wildlife. Large data centers in the area could use up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Creekstone and Joule Power claim closed-loop cooling and water recycling systems will achieve 90% water reduction, but residents remain skeptical.
objective: Block Creekstone Energy rezoning of 1100 acres of farmland

summary: Stax proposes a 50MW Bitcoin mining operation at Franklin Bluffs on the Dalton Highway, with plans to scale to 1GW. Would burn natural gas. Environmentalists and academics slam the proposal for industrializing the sensitive Arctic.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Stax Capital Partners in North Slope Borough

summary: Johnson City Commission unanimously approved a 1-year moratorium on data centers and bitcoin mines in the I-2 (heavy industrial) zoning district on June 5, 2025. Prompted by BrightRidge (local electric utility) purchasing land for a data center after being pushed out of Washington County. BrightRidge also separately imposed its own moratorium on May 13.
objective: Impose 1-year data centers and crypto mining moratorium in City of Johnson City (BrightRidge)

summary: Oregon Legislature adopted the POWER Act (HB 3546) requiring Oregon PUC to create a new industrial customer class for users over 20 MW. CUB accused PGE of circumventing the law — PGE built $174M in substations for data centers but proposed assigning 47% of costs to residential ratepayers. Data centers are 6% of Oregon consumption, projected 20% by 2030. Oregon subsidizes data centers ~$450M/year in property tax breaks.
objective: Expand data center tax exemptions via HB 3546

summary: Comprehensive data center regulation. Defines 'qualified large-scale data centers' (25,000+ sq ft, $250M+ investment). Eliminates electricity sales tax exemption. Extends IT equipment exemption from 20 to 35 years. Imposes $2M-$5M annual fees based on peak electrical demand. Requires prevailing wages. Passed House 85-43, Senate 40-26.
objective: Eliminate data center electricity tax exemption statewide (HF 16)

opposition_groups: Residents Against the Etheridge Lakes Data Center
summary: Chesapeake City Council unanimously rejected a proposal to rezone 22.6 acres of agricultural land for a 350,000 sq ft data center. The Planning Commission had voted 6-1 against it. Over 650 emails and a 450-signature petition opposed the project. The council chamber erupted in a standing ovation after the unanimous rejection.
objective: Deny Doug Fuller rezoning of 22.6 acres of farmland

summary: SB 6, signed by Governor Abbott on June 20, 2025, imposes major data center regulations: facilities with 75+ MW must shoulder grid connection costs, maintain remote disconnect capability, disclose on-site backup generation capable of meeting 50% of site demand to ERCOT, and demonstrate financial commitment. The PUCT is implementing through multiple rulemaking dockets throughout 2026.
objective: Require data centers to bear grid costs and enable load shedding

summary: Clarke County Board of Supervisors approved an indefinite moratorium on data center development on June 23, 2025. Supervisor Randy Dunbar cited lack of existing zoning ordinances. Concerns included water consumption. The county already had wind and solar moratoriums. Applied Digital had proposed a 200MW facility in adjacent Adair County.
objective: Impose indefinite data centers and solar moratorium in Clarke County

summary: Warrenton Town Council voted to fully ban data centers after significant community opposition. GI Partners had purchased a $39M, 42-acre plot for a 220,000 sq ft data center. Over 500 people attended a Town Council meeting with nearly 130 speakers opposing the project, including Oscar winner Robert Duvall. Citizens for Fauquier County (CFFC) filed multiple lawsuits, including challenges over the special use permit and withheld emails.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Town of Warrenton

summary: Google confirmed it is behind a data center project on 550 acres along West State Road 42 near Monrovia. Despite resident protests, the Morgan County Plan Commission gave a favorable recommendation for rezoning. County commissioners approved additional land. Protect Morgan County group is raising funds for legal action, and residents filed a lawsuit claiming commissioners disregarded the county's comprehensive plan and that the rezoning was arbitrary. Construction began in February 2026. The Citizens Action Coalition (CAC) is also actively fighting the project.
objective: Challenge Google rezoning in Monrovia in court

opposition_groups: Stop the Data Center Construction in Perry Township
summary: Province Group (Newport Beach, CA) proposed a 7-building data center campus on the 215-acre Champion Farm property shared between Perry Village and Perry Township. Residents organized a referendum petition drive led by Curtis Curd, gathering 211 signatures (nearly 3x the 76 required). Perry Village Council repealed the data center zoning ordinance on July 31, 2025 and Province Group withdrew its zoning application. However, the fight is not over as Province Group could still pursue a conditional use permit under existing zoning.
objective: Require use permit for Province Group data center in Perry Township

summary: Western Hospitality Partners proposed 'Project Lincoln: OC Data Center,' a $6B, 600MW facility initially planned for 267 acres at 3557 North Highway 53. Due to fierce opposition from 'We Are Oldham County,' the company tried switching to a smaller 30-acre drive-in movie theater site. Oldham County passed a 150-day moratorium 4-2, and Western Hospitality Partners subsequently withdrew its application entirely.
objective: Impose 150-day data centers moratorium in Oldham County (Western Hospitality Partners)

project_name: AWS Data Center at 8050 Camino Arroyo
summary: Amazon Web Services received approval on July 3, 2025 for a 218,000 sq ft data center requiring 49 MW from PG&E and 25 emergency generators on a 56-acre site. Two Change.org petitions were created - one to stop the project and another to ban/regulate data centers in Gilroy. The mayor said the city cannot legally stop the project.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Gilroy

opposition_groups: Arizona Chapter of 7x24 Exchange (industry opposition to restrictions)
summary: Mesa City Council adopted new zoning rules restricting data centers to General and High Industrial zones (4.1% of city land), requiring special use permits. Fifteen data centers had been constructed, approved, or proposed in Mesa within six years.
objective: Require use permit for CyrusOne data center in Mesa

opposition_groups: New Jersey League of Conservation Voters
summary: New Jersey has introduced multiple data center bills. S4318 (Sen. Burzichelli, Sen. Greenstein) requires the BPU to study data center effects on electricity costs and was signed into law by Gov. Murphy. S4293 (Sen. Ruiz, Sen. Burgess) requires data centers to report water and energy usage to the BPU; conditionally vetoed by Gov. Murphy in October 2025. A5462/S4307 (Asm. Dave Bailey Jr.) would require electric utilities to develop special tariff rules for data centers using 100+ MW monthly to protect non-data-center customers from cost increases; pocket-vetoed by Gov. Murphy as one of his last acts and refiled in the 2026 session. In February 2026, committees in both chambers approved a bill requiring large power users (100+ MW/month) to pay at least 85% of their service costs.
objective: Study data center electricity cost impacts in New Jersey (S4318)

summary: Quantica plans a 5,000-acre 'Big Sky Digital Infrastructure' AI campus near Broadview (pop. ~130) requiring up to 1GW — more than NorthWestern Energy's entire residential customer base. Local resident Cari Olson has been rallying opposition statewide. Concerns: water, noise, tax incentives.
objective: Rally against Quantica Infrastructure data center in Broadview

summary: St. Charles became the first city in the nation to enact a citywide one-year moratorium on data center construction after residents mobilized against 'Project Cumulus,' a secretive 440-acre hyperscale facility later linked to Google. Concerns centered on threats to the city's water source and environmental damage. The developer CGR withdrew the project amid overwhelming public opposition.
objective: Enact first citywide data center construction ban in St. Charles

summary: Thor Equities proposed a $1 billion data center on 810 acres (building on about 20%) near Milan. Despite the Planning Commission recommending denial, the Board of Trustees unanimously approved rezoning of 522 acres in July 2025. PACT (Protect Augusta Charter Township) gathered 957 signatures in two weeks (needed 561) to force a public referendum. The special election is expected May 2026 at earliest. PACT is confident residents will vote the project down. Opponents fear noise, light pollution, destruction of rural character, higher utility bills, and brownouts. Thor committed to abiding by the township's noise ordinance.
objective: Force public referendum on Thor Equities data center in Augusta Charter

summary: Oppidan halted its data center project citing lengthy permitting for diesel generators. MCEA (Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy) sued the city in August 2025, alleging officials hid information and approved a faulty AUAR (Alternative Urban Areawide Review) environmental study. MCEA argues the review failed to adequately analyze water withdrawals from the Mount Simon-Hinckley Aquifer that supplies drinking water to ~1 million Minnesotans. The proposed development could consume 500,000 to 30 million gallons of water/day. Even though Oppidan backed away, the lawsuit remains necessary because the approved AUAR could allow future data center proposals. Briefing expected April 2026.
objective: Sue for environmental review of Oppidan project

summary: Maryland-based developer Natelli Investments LLC filed plans for the 'New Hill Digital Campus,' a 300MW data center complex on 190 acres off Old U.S. Highway 1 near Apex, requiring annexation and rezoning. The facility would consume up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day during peak summer heat. Over 4,500 people signed a Change.org petition opposing the project. Residents formed the Protect Wake County Coalition to coordinate opposition. Concerns include noise, air pollution from backup generators, water use, strain on the power grid, loss of farmland, and impact on nearby schools. The neighboring Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers and crypto mining in response to the regional proposal wave.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Wake County (Natelli Investments)

opposition_groups: NAACP Center for Environmental and Climate Justice
summary: Logistics Land Investment proposed Project Marvel, a $14.5B, 4.5 million sq ft data center requiring clearing 700 acres of forest in Bessemer — a city that is approximately 70% Black with a legacy of pollution from steel mills, coal plants, and coke ovens. The NAACP (Alabama State Conference, Metro Birmingham Branch, and Center for Environmental and Climate Justice) formally opposed the project, demanding transparency, disclosure of NDAs, expected employment and tax impacts, energy/water usage details, and guarantees against utility cost increases. The project would require 2 million gallons of water daily. Despite stiff opposition and 7,000+ petition signatures, Bessemer changed its laws to accommodate data centers in October 2025, passing the project forward.
objective: Oppose Logistics Land Investment data center in Bessemer

summary: Developer withdrew $1B rezoning application after 8 months of resident organizing. Planning Commission had backed project in May. Opposition cited industrialization of rural land.
objective: Block Diode Ventures data center through opposition in Charles City County

summary: Applied Digital's $7B Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale hosts a 250MW data center leased to CoreWeave for 15 years. Construction is underway despite community resistance. At a packed city meeting, residents wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts and carrying 'My Land, My Choice' signs confronted elected officials about lack of representation. Renee Hutchinson, who retired to Ellendale before the facility was announced, cited grid stability concerns. Leon Westerhausen opposed the project, warning it would affect future land development. The facility requested annexation into the city of Ellendale in late 2024. North Dakota lawmakers are now considering legislation to address rifts between infrastructure developers and landowners.
objective: Ban NDAs concealing Applied Digital development in Ellendale

summary: Elon Musk's xAI built 'Colossus,' the world's largest AI supercomputer, in Boxtown — a 90% Black neighborhood in southwest Memphis first settled by formerly enslaved people in 1863. The facility operates 33 methane-powered gas turbines (permitted for only 15) without appropriate Clean Air Act permits, increasing Memphis's smog-forming NOx emissions by 30-60%. University of Tennessee researchers found peak nitrogen dioxide levels increased 79% near the facility. Over 2,000 public comments were submitted to the Shelby County Health Department opposing the turbines. The SELC filed a 60-day notice of intent to sue on behalf of the NAACP for Clean Air Act violations. Despite protests, the Shelby County Health Department approved xAI's air quality permit. Community groups — including Memphis Community Against Pollution, Protect Our Aquifer, and Young Gifted & Green — formed a coalition opposing the project. State Rep. Justin J. Pearson has been a vocal opponent. xAI leased 552 acres in Frank C. Pidgeon Industrial Park and plans to expand to 300MW+ consuming 5.7 million gallons of water per day. The facility's MLGW electricity bills averaged $4.5 million/month in 2025.
objective: Sue xAI for unpermitted gas turbines in Memphis

summary: Microsoft filed plans for two data centers ($350M each) near Castroville while Rowan Digital Infrastructure's Project Cinco is developing a separate $900M hyperscale campus on 440 acres with 300MW capacity. Medina County is in an 'exceptional drought' and the projects would draw water from the Edwards Aquifer. Rowan announced investment in an Edwards Aquifer Authority conservation program to achieve 250% offset of water usage. Eight data centers total are planned for the county. San Antonio-area data centers already consumed 463 million gallons of water in 2023-2024 while residents face Stage 3 watering restrictions. Texas data centers are estimated to use 49 billion gallons of water in 2025, projected to soar to 399 billion gallons annually by 2030.
objective: Oppose Microsoft data center over Edwards Aquifer drought impacts

summary: Denver-based Tract proposed the $30B Mooresville Technology Park on 400 acres of undeveloped land owned by Teresa Earnhardt, widow of NASCAR champion Dale Earnhardt. The project drew intense community backlash. Dale Earnhardt's eldest son Kerry and his wife Rene posted a YouTube video detailing their opposition, saying their father would be 'furious' to see his name associated with the project. About 200 people crowded a Board of Commissioners meeting wearing red T-shirts to signal opposition, with all 10 speakers raising concerns about water use in a drought-prone region and skepticism about job promises. Tract withdrew the rezoning request on August 13, 2025.
objective: Block Tract data center through opposition in Mooresville

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Ozaukee County
No DCs in Ozaukee County WI
summary: Recall effort against Mayor Ted Neitzke failed, falling several hundred signatures short of the 25% threshold by the Feb. 16 deadline. However, Great Lakes Neighbors United's separate lawsuit challenging $458M in TIF financing (TID) continues, with a motion hearing scheduled for March 13. The group also submitted a referendum petition requiring voter approval for future TIDs over $10M. Business associations counter-sued over the referendum ordinance. The $15B data center campus (Vantage Data Centers/OpenAI/Oracle/Stargate) construction is underway. The recall petition against Mayor Ted Neitzke launched by Great Lakes Neighbors United failed in February 2026, falling several hundred signatures short of the 1,600 required. The group collected approximately 1,200 signatures. The $15 billion Stargate/OpenAI/Oracle data center construction has begun despite the opposition effort.
objective: Recall officials who approved data center in Port Washington

summary: Copia Power Devco LLC (backed by Carlyle) received 4-1 approval from Lyon County commissioners for a Master Plan Amendment to build the Monarch Data Center - 4.6 million sq ft across 14 buildings on 505 acres in Mason Valley, Yerington. The 1,000MW facility would require enough electricity to power roughly 500,000 homes. Multiple Change.org petitions oppose the project, citing water scarcity in the drought-prone region, corruption allegations against officials, and impacts on the agricultural community. Commissioner Hockaday cast the lone dissenting vote.
objective: Oppose Copia Power Devco data center in Yerington

summary: Meta's $10B+, 1 GW data center campus on 1,500 acres in Lebanon's LEAP District. Uses ~5M gallons/day. Wabash Valley Power Alliance increasing transmission spending by 1,300% ($627M) primarily for Meta, with costs likely passed to ratepayers. 200+ people rallied in Feb 2026.
objective: Rally against Meta data center in Lebanon

summary: Meta's largest AI data center ($27B 'Hyperion'). LPSC approved three Entergy gas plants (2,200 MW total). Impacts: 600% spike in vehicle crashes, Holly Ridge Elementary shut playground due to truck safety, low-income families evicted for Meta worker housing, ~23M gallons/day water usage, $550M transmission line costs passed to 1.1M Entergy customers.
objective: Oppose transmission lines for Meta data center

summary: MCEA sued Lakeville and developer Olam Holdings, alleging the city deliberately concealed that a 1.36M sq ft, 152-acre development is likely a data center, describing it generically as 'light industrial.' Environmental review accused of being inadequate.
objective: Sue for environmental review of Olam Holdings 1 project

summary: Tucson City Council voted 7-0 to reject Project Blue, a 290-acre, 600MW data center linked to AWS. Community opposition centered on water use and energy costs. Amazon later pulled out entirely.
objective: Deny Amazon 600MW data center permit in City of Tucson

opposition_groups: Clean Air Coalition of Western New York
summary: North Tonawanda Common Council unanimously approved a two-year moratorium on all crypto mining and data center developments in July 2024, expiring approximately July 2026. The ban covers new crypto mining plants, expansion of existing operations, and new data centers. The existing Digihost (now Digi Power X) facility continues to generate noise complaints. With updated noise monitoring equipment, North Tonawanda police issued several citations to Digi Power X for allegedly breaking the city's noise ordinance. Digi Power X filed a motion to dismiss those noise citations, with the case in city court. Residents also raised concerns about the facility's plans to use nuclear power. The city is working to update its zoning codes during the moratorium period.
objective: Halt data centers and crypto mining in North Tonawanda after Digihost (existing operator) proposal

summary: Wrangell's borough assembly authorized a nonbinding agreement with Greensparc to explore a small-scale data center using hydroelectric power. Community members raised concerns about noise, light, and water discharge. A Cordova resident reported Greensparc's existing facility there has been 'underwhelming and underperforming.'
objective: Oppose Greensparc data center over water in Wrangell

summary: NorthPoint Development (Kansas City) proposed a 664-acre, 14-building hyperscale data center with 1,008MW peak power demand and 3 million sq ft in the small riverside community of fewer than 2,000 people. The projected electricity consumption of 8.8 million megawatt-hours per year is more than half the output of the Gaston Plant (1,880MW). Over 100 residents packed a council meeting to oppose. A Change.org petition gathered 500+ signatures. No formal application has been filed but residents are organizing preemptively.
objective: Petition to block NorthPoint Development data center in Wilsonville

summary: Applied Digital's $3B Polaris Forge 2 data center (280MW) proposed near the 900-person town of Harwood on approximately 900 acres sparked a confrontational community meeting on August 25, 2025. Residents peppered the company VP with questions for 90 minutes, raising concerns about noise, utility rate increases, sprawl, and lack of transparency (NDAs required for council members). Harwood approved the project in September and construction began shortly after. The project triggered an annexation battle with the City of Fargo, which sought to annex the site for tax revenue, though Fargo later withdrew its annexation attempts.
objective: Community pressure forces Applied Digital to withdraw in Harwood

summary: GigaLand LLC sought to rezone 202 acres for an 800MW, 2.2M sq ft data center campus with seven 80-foot buildings. The Planning Commission voted 4-1 to recommend denial. Developers withdrew on August 25, 2025, calling it 'a direct result of the strong community opposition.'
objective: Deny GigaLand rezoning of 202 acres in Fauquier County

opposition_groups: Empowerment Congress of Dona Ana County
summary: BorderPlex Digital Assets/STACK Infrastructure's $165B Project Jupiter (Oracle/OpenAI Stargate) on ~1,400 acres near Santa Teresa. Construction started but awaiting two air quality permits from NMED due by April 22, 2026. Over 1,500 public comments were submitted opposing the air permit, which could authorize up to 14 million tons of CO2 per year. A secretive pro-project PR campaign has emerged. State SB235 oversight bill died in the House.
objective: Challenge BorderPlex Digital Assets air quality permits in court

company: TA Realty / Digital Realty (recently approved projects)
summary: Clayton County Board of Commissioners approved a moratorium on new data center applications through the end of 2025, citing concerns about rapid growth after TA Realty and Digital Realty projects were approved for a combined 324MW. The moratorium covers special land use permits, rezonings, and building permits for new or expanding data centers.
objective: Halt data centers in Clayton County after TA Realty proposal

opposition_groups: Troup County Anti-Data Center Coalition (Gage Bailey, founder)
summary: Troup County enacted a 90-day moratorium on data center applications, later extended, after Fertile Ground Investments pursued a 600MW facility on 437 acres near Hogansville. Residents raised concerns about property values, water consumption, energy costs, and utility rate increases.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Troup County (Fertile Ground Investments)

opposition_groups: No Data Center in Landover coalition
Party for Socialism and Liberation DC
Brandywine TB Southern Region Neighborhood Coalition
summary: At least 300 protesters rallied against the $5 billion Brightseat Tech Park, a 4.1 million sq ft, 820 MW data center campus proposed by Lerner Enterprises on the 87-acre former Landover Mall site. A Change.org petition gathered over 22,500 signatures. The project was approved in October 2024 before most neighbors were aware. Activists cite environmental racism concerns in the predominantly Black community. County Executive Braveboy signed an executive order pausing all data center permitting on Sept. 15. A 20-member task force produced a 460-page report with 14 recommendations. The County Council extended the data center moratorium through end of 2026.
objective: Halt data centers in Prince George's after Lerner Enterprises proposal

summary: LaGrange City Council passed a 180-day moratorium on new data center applications amid community backlash over 'Project Pegasus,' an $8 billion Thor Equities/Form8tion campus on 271 acres projected to consume 400MW of power. The moratorium does not affect Project Pegasus, which was already approved. Residents cited concerns over water and energy use.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in City of LaGrange (Thor Equities / Form8tion)

summary: Pike County enacted a moratorium on data center development as part of a wave of Georgia county ordinances. Commissioner James Jenkins said the moratorium would create a better understanding of how to accommodate data centers in a county that lacks building codes and zoning ordinances designed for these facilities.
objective: Halt data centers in Pike County after Amazon/AWS proposal

summary: Lamar County enacted a moratorium on data center development after Amazon/AWS purchased 985 acres for $270 million, the largest real estate transaction in county history. Commissioners said the moratorium would allow them to update industrial and commercial zoning ordinances not designed for facilities of this scale.
objective: Halt data centers in Lamar County after Amazon/AWS proposal

summary: Compass Data Centers proposed a $1B+ campus on 333 acres of farmland on Stamey Farm Road in western Iredell County. Plans include five buildings of approximately 270,000 sq ft each (1.35M total) with 40 backup generators per building. A Facebook group with 900+ members organized opposition citing air and groundwater pollution and property value impacts. Despite opposition, the Statesville City Council unanimously approved the project in September 2025.
objective: Organize coalition against Compass Data Centers data center in Statesville

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Cumberland County PA
summary: Hampden Township Commissioners unanimously rejected a proposed zoning ordinance amendment that would have designated office-park zones for data centers. Some 300 residents signed a petition, and commissioners heard more than two hours of public comment from dozens of people, most opposing the change. Food & Water Watch's eastern Pennsylvania organizer Virginia Marcille-Kerslake mobilized opposition through a door-to-door campaign with flyers and QR codes. Residents raised concerns about air, light, and noise impacts. The rejection was part of a broader wave of Pennsylvania communities proactively restricting data centers.
objective: Deny Undisclosed data center permit in Hampden Township

company: Contour / TeraWatt / THO Investments (Armory Innovation District consortium)
opposition_groups: Eco-Socialist Green Party of Eastern Missouri
Democratic Socialists of America St. Louis
Party for Socialism and Liberation St. Louis
summary: A $600M data center proposed at the Armory in St. Louis spurred a petition with 13,000+ signatures calling for a year-long moratorium. Over 200 residents attended a community-led town hall. The Board of Aldermen moratorium attempt was defeated when more than half voted against introduction. However, Mayor Spencer's Executive Order 92 now requires conditional use permits for all data centers. The Planning Commission drafted comprehensive rules limiting large data centers to industrial zones, capping at 500K sq ft, requiring 300ft residential buffers. The Armory project, rebranded as the $3B 'Armory Innovation District' (120MW), had a CUP hearing scheduled for March 19, 2026.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after Contour proposal

summary: A data center proposed at a former power station site in Carbon County's Packer and Banks townships has been delayed by officials. Opposition groups Save Carbon County and Greenfire Coalition have spoken out, with two petitions gathering around 900 signatures total. Concerns focus on loss of green spaces and community impacts.
objective: 900-signature petition opposing NorthPoint Development data center

summary: Griffin City Council unanimously passed a 180-day moratorium on data centers and other technological facilities. The moratorium was enacted so city planning staff can study current codes and ordinances for potential data center regulations. Officials noted the Unified Development Code currently does not specifically allow 'Technological Facilities' within the city, either by right or special use permit.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Griffin

opposition_groups: Rankin County Residents Against Data Center (ad hoc)
summary: AVAIO Digital's $6 billion Taurus data center campus under construction at East Metropolitan Center. Nearly 1,000 residents signed petition demanding guarantees on utility bills and pollution. 60 permanent jobs promised. Occupancy targeted first half 2027.
objective: Petition to block AVAIO Digital data center in Brandon

summary: TransGas proposes a data center at Harless Industrial Park in Holden, powered by a new natural gas plant (Adams Fork Energy project). Residents outspoken about air quality, water, and health concerns.
objective: Oppose TransGas Development Systems data center over water in Logan County

summary: Jerome Township trustees unanimously approved a 9-month moratorium after residents complained of persistent noise from existing Amazon/AWS data centers. Up to four new projects were paused. Trustees plan to reclassify data centers from 'permitted' to 'conditional' use. Amazon cited multi-million-dollar investments in noise reduction engineering.
objective: Impose 9-month data centers moratorium in Jerome Township (Amazon/AWS)

summary: RadiusDC broke ground on a 12MW, 100,000 sq ft data center on 12 acres off Brick Church Pike in the Trinity Hills neighborhood of North Nashville, across from an elementary school. Residents raised environmental justice concerns about siting in a predominantly Black neighborhood. The complex houses 27 backup diesel generators and 16 cooling towers.
objective: Oppose RadiusDC data center as environmental injustice

company: Poe Companies / PowerHouse Data Centers (approved project)
summary: One 525MW data center project in southwest Louisville has been cleared to proceed, built by Poe Companies and PowerHouse Data Centers (starting at 100MW, expanding to 400MW). District 17 Council Member Markus Winkler proposed a six-month moratorium on September 11, 2025, which was tabled indefinitely. A survey showed 81.5% of respondents chose 'Nowhere in Jefferson County' for data center locations. City planners continue drafting regulations.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after Poe Companies proposal

opposition_groups: Blakely No Data Center Campaign (Bob Cicci & Katie Klein-Cicci, organizers)
summary: Developers proposed a data center campus offering up to 1.5GW of capacity on 209 acres in Blakely Borough — requiring power equivalent to the entire output of the nearby Lackawanna Energy Center gas-fired power plant. The first phase called for four two-story buildings at 150,000 sq ft per floor. About 100 residents packed the Borough Building with 60 more outside and 210+ watching via Zoom for an August 13, 2025 Q&A session. A grassroots petition gathered roughly 800 signatures opposing the project. The developers withdrew their zoning application on September 12, 2025, days before a scheduled public hearing. The borough subsequently adopted an ordinance applying nearly three dozen restrictions on future data center developments.
objective: Block Kriger / Alpesh 'Al' Patel data center through opposition in Blakely

opposition_groups: College Station Residents Against Data Center
summary: College Station residents mounted strong opposition to Priority Power's proposed 600MW datacenter on 200 acres of Midtown Drive. More than 75 individuals registered to speak in person and a petition with over 5,000 signatures was submitted. Concerns included noise pollution, procedural issues, environmental impacts, and utility demands. The city rejected the land sale and is now proactively considering guardrails for future datacenter proposals.
objective: Deny Priority Power 600MW data center permit in College Station

summary: The Tarboro Town Council rejected a Special Use Permit for Energy Storage Solutions' proposed 300MW, eight-phase data center on a 52-acre site near Anaconda and McNair roads by a 7-1 vote. The developer filed an appeal to Edgecombe Superior Court in November 2025, alleging the town committed 'errors of law.' A separate project proposed for the Kingsboro Business Park continues. Edgecombe County commissioners also voted down another ESS project. Residents cited concerns about traffic, water use, 24/7 noise, and proximity to the animal shelter.
opposition_groups: Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability
objective: Uphold denial of Energy Storage Solutions data center on appeal

summary: Social Circle approved a 90-day moratorium on new data center applications after being overwhelmed with seven applications. Despite the moratorium, the council approved a controversial rezoning for 151 acres, drawing resident opposition. Part of a statewide wave of Georgia moratoriums.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Social Circle (Multiple)

summary: Google withdrew its $1B 'Project Flo' data center proposal minutes before the City-County Council vote on September 22, 2025, after months of community pushback. Hundreds attended to protest the 468-acre farmland conversion. Residents cited water/electricity consumption, environmental risks, and limited permanent jobs.
objective: Community pressure forces Google to withdraw in Franklin Township

summary: Predominantly Black communities in South Fulton and Union City are fighting a wave of at least 20 planned data centers in southern Fulton County. On Sept. 22, dozens of residents gathered at the Etris Community Center to discuss concerns. Community organizer Wanda Mosley of Black Voters Matter Fund has been rallying residents who struggle to find out about proposed zoning changes. South Fulton approved a data center ordinance in June 2025 setting noise and distance criteria, but then approved a new data center in August despite ongoing community opposition. Advocates highlight environmental racism concerns as the area has long been burdened with industrial development.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in City of South Fulton

summary: Menomonie signed an NDA with Balloonist LLC in February 2024, over a year before announcing the $1.6B data center proposal in July 2025. Over the following six months, residents coordinated a local opposition campaign. Mayor Randy Knaack announced on Sept. 22 that the city was no longer interested in pursuing the project. On January 5, 2026, the City Council revised a zoning ordinance to bar Balloonist from moving ahead. The data center would have used approximately 75,000 gallons of water per day and brought 50-75 full-time jobs.
objective: Oppose Balloonist data center in Menomonie

summary: TeraWulf signed an 80-year lease for 183 acres at a former coal plant on Cayuga Lake to develop up to 400 MW of data center capacity. Hundreds of residents submitted public comments opposing the project, and the town held a public hearing on a moratorium to pause large-scale development while rewriting the zoning code.
objective: Legal challenge to TeraWulf data center in Lansing

opposition_groups: Taylor community residents (led by Carrie D'Anna)
Halt the BPP Project in Taylor
summary: Five Taylor residents sued to stop a 135,000 sq ft data center with an on-site electrical substation on land deeded in 1999 as parkland. Judge Ryan Larson initially issued a temporary block but dismissed the case on Oct. 8, 2025, ruling residents lacked standing. Construction is proceeding.
objective: Legal challenge to Blueprint Data Centers data center in Taylor

summary: Fidelis New Energy proposed a data center complex in Mason County, but residents are frustrated by the lack of answers about impacts on water, environment, and public health. State law HB 2014 stripped local control over 'high-impact' data centers and diverts property tax revenue away from local bodies. Residents from Mason, Tucker, Mingo, and Logan counties visited the Capitol in Feb 2026 to demand lawmakers restore local control.
objective: Cap data center water consumption in WV

summary: Requires PSC-regulated utilities to establish a separate rate class for large energy-use facilities (20+ MW) primarily engaged in data processing. Ensures data centers pay fair share of grid costs and prevents rate increases for residential customers. Advanced out of committee January 2026.
objective: Regulate data center development in Delaware

summary: Shutesbury Special Town Meeting approved a 9-month moratorium bylaw on data centers on September 30, 2025. Planning Board voted 6-1 to bring it forward after a potential company expressed interest. Concerns: water, electricity, noise, exhaust. Planning Board developing permanent zoning, public hearing scheduled March 9, 2026.
objective: Impose 9-month data centers moratorium in Shutesbury

opposition_groups: White County IN Against Data Centers
summary: White County's Board of Commissioners passed a moratorium on data center development on October 20, 2025, after residents packed public meetings in opposition. The moratorium was prompted by a proposed project near Wolcott. Residents voiced concerns about water availability and process transparency. White County joined Marshall (February) and Putnam (November) as Indiana's three counties with data center moratoriums, which conflict with a state government actively courting tech projects through a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases.
objective: Halt data centers in White County after Undisclosed proposal

summary: The Yorkville City Council unanimously approved Project Cardinal on Nov. 10, 2025 -- a 1,037-acre data center campus with 14 two-story warehouses, developed by Pioneer Development LLC. However, a resident filed a lawsuit arguing the project would cause sound pollution and decreased property values. An online petition opposing both Project Cardinal and a separate Project Steel in Yorkville gathered over 1,300 signatures. The lawsuit appeared headed for settlement after a Dec. 30 hearing. NOTE: This appears to be Yorkville, IL (Kendall County), not Yorkville, WI.
objective: Legal challenge to Pioneer Development data center in Village of Yorkville

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Jones County
No Data Center in Jones County GA
summary: EagleRock Partners withdrew rezoning for 600+ acres after organized opposition under 'No Data Centers in Jones County.' Commissioners voted 4-1 for a 90-day moratorium.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Jones County (EagleRock Partners)

opposition_groups: Concerned Citizens of Twiggs County (Rob Fricks, attorney)
Protect Twiggs County -- No Data Center
summary: Twiggs County commissioners unanimously approved Eagle Rock Partners' $5B datacenter rezoning despite hundreds of residents packing the meeting in opposition. The crowd erupted in boos after the vote. Landowners filed a lawsuit alleging improper zoning notices (wrong property, incorrect hearing date) and failure to conduct a Development of Regional Impact study. Construction cannot begin until 2028 due to power infrastructure delays.
objective: Challenge EagleRock Partners rezoning in Twiggs County in court

project_name: Columbia North Industrial Rezoning (potential data center)
summary: A landowner sought to rezone 63 acres of farmland near U.S. 63 for a potential data center. The Planning & Zoning Commission voted 5-2 to recommend the change, but the City Council rejected it 3-3 on Nov. 19, 2025. Residents opposed the loss of farmland and questioned why the project couldn't use already-industrial land.
objective: Block Undisclosed rezoning of 63 acres of farmland

summary: City Council voted 5-2 to sell 500 acres of recreational land to Huntley LLC for a $10B, 1,000 MW data center — less than one mile from Horseshoe Bend. 'Page Residents for Voter Rights' collected 405 signatures to force a referendum, but it was disqualified on a technicality (numbering on pages). A recall effort targeting Mayor Kidman and Councillor Roundtree is ongoing.
objective: Recall officials who approved Huntley data center

summary: Over 30 Carroll County residents filed a lawsuit in Superior Court challenging Villa Rica's annexation and rezoning of 200+ acres for a data center. Door-to-door petitions gathered 500 signatures with approximately 95% of respondents opposing the project. Concerns include 1.1 million gallons/day water use and proximity to residential areas.
objective: Challenge Embry Development Company rezoning in Villa Rica in court

summary: Over 100 residents from New York and New Jersey objected to DataBank's proposal for a 146,000 sq-ft data center adjacent to Lake Tappan reservoir. The Bi-State Datacenter Crisis Coalition organized opposition citing threats to wetlands, wildlife, water quality, and fire risks from lithium-ion battery storage.
objective: Organize coalition against DataBank data center in Orangetown

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Preble County Planning Commission unanimously (11-0) denied rezoning of 300 acres of farmland for a data center. The developer withdrew his application in October after residents organized a referendum campaign. Former Republican state lawmaker Gene Krebs helped lead opposition.
objective: Deny Patrick Scanlon (developer) rezoning of 300 acres of farmland

summary: Prologis purchased 141 acres for its 'Project Mila' datacenter in Trenton. Residents Barry and Lorie Blankenship formed W.A.T.E.R. (Woodsdale and Trenton Environmental Resistance), which had 248 Facebook members. The group filed multiple public records requests seeking information about NDAs, environmental analyses, and health studies. Residents expressed concerns about higher energy/water bills, noise and light pollution, and feeling left in the dark.
objective: Organize coalition against Prologis data center in Butler County

summary: A Fortune 20 tech company (identity hidden behind NDAs) proposed a $1 billion, 1,200-megawatt data center on up to 5,000 acres of farmland on Big Pond Pike near Maysville. The project could bring 400 full-time positions plus 1,500 construction jobs with $80,000 average salary. The massive power requirement nearly equals the output of Eastern Kentucky Power Cooperative's Spurlock power plant. Town leaders signed NDAs barring public disclosure, sparking controversy. Rules governing the project moved toward a final vote in February 2026.
objective: Oppose Undisclosed data center over secrecy in Mason County

summary: Project Kestrel proposes six hyperscale datacenters totaling 1.8 million sq ft north of Kansas City International Airport with massive property and sales tax subsidies. Platte County Presiding Commissioner Scott Fricker publicly opposes the project, stating it will drive up water and utility rates for households and small businesses while giving subsidies to the world's richest corporations. Residents lack clarity on who is behind the project, who pays for power upgrades, and why there was no public input.
objective: Study data center impacts on water resources in MO

project_name: Lower Mt. Bethel Data Center Campus
summary: Peron Development and J.G. Petrucci Company are proposing a data center campus at the intersection of Gravel Hill Road and Martins Creek Belvidere Highway in Lower Mt. Bethel Township, Northampton County. The site is across from the John M. Mauser Nature Education Trail System along the scenic Delaware River. A petition gathered ~4,600 signatures opposing the project.
objective: 4600-signature petition opposing Peron Development data center

opposition_groups: Joplin Citizens Against Wildwood Ranch Data Center Site
summary: The Joplin City Council approved annexation and rezoning of Wildwood Ranch for a data center. The Joplin Sustainable Technology Alliance launched a referendum effort but fell short, collecting only about half of the ~8,000 signatures needed by the March 7 deadline. On March 17, 2026, the Council certified the petitions as insufficient. The annexation and rezoning of the 600-acre Wildwood Ranch site stands. The site, owned by local developer Jimmer Pinjuv, offers 200+ MW capacity on the southwest side of the city.
objective: Halt data centers in Joplin after Jimmer Pinjuv / Wildwood Ranch proposal

summary: Despite a petition with 2,500+ signatures and a 4,000-member opposition group, Hobart reached a deal with Amazon Web Services for a data center on 500+ acres. AWS plans to invest $15 billion in 2.4 GW of data center capacity across Northwest Indiana. The No Data Centers Hobart Indiana group has also launched a dedicated opposition website with legal and community action resources.
objective: Petition to block Amazon/AWS data center in Hobart

summary: RadiusDC is planning to build two 100,000 sq ft data centers in Plainfield using 24 MW of electricity and about 700 gallons of water/day. The Plainfield Plan Commission unanimously approved the primary plat petition on Jan. 5, 2026, after more than a dozen concerned residents opposed the project during public comment. A Change.org petition opposes the project citing lack of transparency.
objective: Petition to block RadiusDC data center in Plainfield

company: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC / BHGH Properties LLC
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Lordstown was one of the first Ohio municipalities to institute a moratorium on data centers. In October 2025, developer Bristolville 25 Developer LLC submitted site plans for a 133-acre, $3.6B data center in Trumbull and Mahoning counties. The council passed a ban on data centers in November, but later rescinded it for a 180-day moratorium after legal advice. Bristolville 25 filed suit in the Ohio Supreme Court seeking a writ of mandamus to force officials to process the application. The court dismissed the case against the Village Solicitor on Dec. 24, 2025, but denied dismissal against the village. The developer filed 3,000+ pages of evidence in February 2026. The developer Bristolville 25 submitted evidence in its Ohio Supreme Court lawsuit against the Village of Lordstown in February 2026. The developer is seeking a writ of mandamus to force the village to process its site plan application for a $3.6B, 133-acre data center near the Ohio Turnpike. The village previously passed a data center ban in November 2025 but rescinded it in favor of a 180-day moratorium after receiving updated legal advice. The lawsuit remains ongoing.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Lordstown (Bristolville 25 Developer)

company: Ohio Industrial Realty Group (potential tenant: Amazon)
opposition_groups: Save the IX Center Campaign (Cassia Core, organizer)
summary: A petition led by resident Cassia Core gathered nearly 2,000 signatures opposing the conversion of the historic Cleveland IX Center into an AI data center.
