summary: Harvey County Commission unanimously approved a moratorium on data center construction in unincorporated areas through the end of 2028 (roughly three years). Commissioner Don Schroeder explained the moratorium gives the planning department and zoning board time to research and design regulations, as the county currently has no zoning rules for data centers, crypto mining, or related facilities. Concerns centered on water use from the Equus Beds Aquifer. Nearby Andale and Colwich residents pushed back against proposed projects, citing the importance of preserving their farming and ranching community character.
objective: Pause data centers and crypto mining in Harvey County pending regulations

project_name: SB 886 / SB 887 - Ratepayer Protection & ELDP Data Center Certification
summary: SB 886 requires PUC to establish a special tariff to protect ratepayers from data center transmission costs. SB 887 allows data centers to be eligible for Environmental Leadership Development Project certification with additional requirements. These follow failed 2025 efforts where Big Tech blocked data center regulation, leaving only a study requirement. Bills are pending hearings in the Senate.
objective: Shield ratepayers from data center costs via SB 886

summary: Saginaw City Council unanimously approved a resolution enacting a 6-month blanket prohibition of data centers on January 13, 2026. Council members aim to finalize a comprehensive data center policy within that timeframe to ensure responsible development that benefits the community long term.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in City of Saginaw

summary: Mandates data centers use closed-loop cooling systems with zero net water withdrawal and zero wastewater discharge. Prohibits data centers from extracting groundwater or using municipal water for cooling.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs in SC

summary: HB 120 prohibits construction of new data centers and prohibits state or local approvals for construction unless/until the General Assembly enacts legislation related to co-location with new or existing power generation (including natural gas, nuclear, or small modular reactors). Maryland lawmakers previously overrode Gov. Moore's veto of a data center impact study bill. The moratorium is tied to energy co-location requirements rather than a general pause.
objective: Pause data center permits for study via HB 120

company: Project Jupiter (Dona Ana County data center development)
summary: SB 235 (Microgrid Oversight Act) requires the Public Regulation Commission to develop rules for any microgrid producing more than 20 MW, grants regulators oversight over approval and operation of microgrids, and requires proof of ability to meet state renewable energy standards for microgrids coming online after May 2026. Annual reports required after 2027 detailing power generation, water usage, and compliance. Largely driven by concerns about Project Jupiter in Dona Ana County, which proposed self-reliant 'microgrids' to power multi-billion dollar data centers. Passed NM Senate 22-20.
objective: Require PRC oversight of 20MW+ microgrids serving data centers

summary: Armada Township enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development starting January 14, 2026. The moratorium gives the township time to review zoning, infrastructure capacity, and land use impacts before any data center applications can proceed.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Armada Township

summary: Despite vocal opposition from dozens of residents, Spalding County Commissioners unanimously approved a $3.9 billion, 5 million sq ft data center complex by Wallace Jackson LLC spanning 190 acres along Interstate 75, consisting of ten separate buildings (roughly three times the size of Lenox Square Mall). Residents cited concerns over environmental pollution, loss of generational agricultural land, and the project generating few permanent jobs relative to its scale. A second $3.7B project was proposed shortly after by a different developer.
objective: Contest Wallace Jackson 900MW data center despite community opposition

opposition_groups: Say NO to Coweta, Ok Data Center
summary: Beale Infrastructure's 'Project Mustang' drew a heated 3-hour city council meeting. Police arrested resident Darren Blanchard for trespassing after he exceeded the 3-minute speaking limit by ~30 seconds, garnering national media attention. Hundreds signed petitions opposing the project over water use and infrastructure strain.
objective: Petition to block Beale Infrastructure data center in Claremore

summary: Google's $9B Project Spring proposed on 827 acres of land annexed by Sand Springs. City Council voted 6-1 on Feb 3 to approve rezoning. The Protect Sand Springs Alliance launched a recall effort against the entire city council, gathering 130+ signatures per council member in just four hours. They need 344-541 signatures per ward and are holding collection events throughout March for a potential June 16 recall election. The Alliance's annexation lawsuit (CV-2026-00002) was moved from Osage County to Tulsa County District Court. The Osage Mineral Council is reviewing sovereignty implications as the site sits partially in Osage County.
objective: Recall officials who approved data center in Sand Springs

opposition_groups: Renwick Area Against Data Centers in West Sedgwick County
summary: Sedgwick County Commissioners unanimously voted on March 4, 2026 to extend the moratorium on data center permit applications to June 11, 2026. Two companies — Monarch Energy (300+ acres near Andale/Colwich) and NextEra Energy (800 acres near Garden Plain) — are trying to buy 1,000+ acres. State Senator Blasi pushed for a 3-year moratorium. Town halls were packed with hundreds of residents wearing 'no data center' shirts. Neighbors are alarmed by continued land acquisition activity despite the moratorium. NDAs with landowners have limited transparency.
objective: Impose 3-year data centers moratorium in Sedgwick County (Monarch Energy)

summary: Change.org petition with 5,856 signatures opposing a proposed data center on Robertsville Road in Villa Ridge, near Shaw Nature Reserve. The land (613 acres total) is owned by Diamond 66 LLC and Diamond Farms LLC; L.B. Eckelkamp Jr., CEO of Bank of Washington, requested Franklin County rezone the parcels. Franklin County is under a drought alert. After hours of public comment, the Planning and Zoning Commission voted to table the discussion for six months. The petition was started by Amanda Brown and targets Governor Kehoe.
opposition_groups: Shaw Nature Reserve / Missouri Botanical Garden
No AI Datacenters in Franklin County MO
company: Diamond Farms LLC / L.B. Eckelkamp Jr.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Villa Ridge

summary: Data center proposals near Yukon face sustained opposition. OKC Planning Commission voted 7-0 to approve rezoning 295.5 acres for Beltline Energy despite outcry. Residents concerned about water strain and electricity costs.
objective: Block Gamma Resources rezoning of 295 acres in Yukon

summary: Data Center Water Transparency Amendments. Requires data centers (10,000+ sq ft) to notify Division of Water Quality, Division of Water Rights, and water providers of intent to construct. Must report projected water usage, discharge types, treatment methods, and conservation efforts. Passed House 72-0, now in Senate.
objective: Require data center water usage notification and reporting in Utah

summary: Amazon Data Services building a $750M data center on 99-acre former Milwaukee Tool site. Clinton Board of Aldermen approved FILOT agreement Jan 15, 2026. Residents raised concerns about electricity rate increases and few permanent jobs.
objective: Oppose Amazon data center impact on rates

summary: Google uses a third of The Dalles' water (~1M gallons/day). City lobbying for HR 655 to transfer ~150 acres of Mt. Hood National Forest to triple reservoir capacity (~$70M project). Google holds groundwater rights for up to 3.88M gallons/day. Conservation coalition called it a 'cynical sweetheart deal.' Google pays $7.3M/year in property taxes after 92% discount.
objective: Organize coalition against Google data center in The Dalles

summary: Nebraska LB 1111 passed the Legislature and became law. Requires data centers to pay the full cost of new electricity infrastructure, requires public power suppliers to publicly disclose project details (name, developers, owners, location, size, electricity demand), and allows public power suppliers to impose restrictions including operational/decommissioning plans, security bonds, and community benefit agreements.
objective: Study data center impacts on electrical grid in NE

project_name: Kansas City Data Center Zoning Restrictions (citywide response to multiple projects)
opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence
summary: Kansas City Council unanimously approved zoning changes reclassifying data centers as industrial facilities, limiting them to specific zoning districts, revoking by-right status for larger data centers, and requiring City Council approval and will-serve letters from water and electric utilities.
objective: Restrict Multiple 700MW data center in Kansas City

summary: Multiple Georgia data center moratorium bills introduced in 2026 session. HB 1012 (Rep. Ruwa Romman) would bar counties and cities from issuing permits for new data centers until March 1, 2027, with exceptions for approvals issued before July 1, 2026. HB 1059 (Rep. Derrick Jackson) would establish a moratorium from July 1, 2026 to December 31, 2028, and create a 14-member Data Center Impact Commission to conduct a three-year study with a final report due October 1, 2029. At least eight Georgia towns and counties have already passed local moratoriums. Industry opposition has been fierce, with a key bill stalling after a last-minute amendment found bipartisan support. HB 1012 stalled in the House Governmental Affairs Committee and did not cross over by the March 6 deadline, effectively killing it for the 2026 session. The moratorium provisions were not included in SB 410 which did pass.
objective: Pause data center permits for study via HB 1012

opposition_groups: Say NO to Coweta, Ok Data Center (Atlas Project)
summary: Beale Infrastructure's $800M Project Atlas proposed 295 acres for an Amazon/AWS data center with 400MW capacity. The Planning Commission voted 4-1 to deny rezoning on January 19, 2026, but the proposal advanced to city council. The developer reduced the project scope to 60 acres and promised a 150-foot buffer. Opposition started a recall petition targeting city council members. Blue Owl Capital, the major backer of Beale Infrastructure, is facing financial turmoil that could derail the project.
objective: Deny Beale Infrastructure rezoning of 295 acres in Coweta

summary: Village of Godfrey unanimously approved a 180-day moratorium on data center construction applications on January 20, 2026. Trustee Rick Lauschke: "We don't know enough." Followed weeks of protests across Madison County opposing data centers in Troy, Granite City, and Godfrey.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Village of Godfrey

summary: Establishes moratorium on data center construction from July 1, 2026 to December 31, 2028. Creates 14-member Data Center Impact Commission to conduct three-year study of impacts on energy, environment, water, health, and housing. Report due October 1, 2029.
objective: Require data center energy and water reports in GA

summary: Newton County and Covington both enacted data center moratoriums near Meta's Stanton Springs campus. Covington's 180-day moratorium was enacted January 20, 2026, and the city unanimously rejected two annexation requests including Alcovy Rise LLC's 1,500+ acres.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Newton County / (rejected)

summary: A six-building, 5.1M sq ft data center campus proposed on 410 acres directly across from Parkland High School. All residents who spoke at the LVPC meeting opposed it, citing noise, emissions, power grid strain, and sinkholes across the site.
objective: Oppose CDE Acquisitions data center over noise in South Whitehall

summary: Monterey Park City Council unanimously adopted a 45-day moratorium on data center development on January 21, 2026, prompted by a proposed 250,000 sq ft data center at Saturn Park. On March 4, 2026, the Council unanimously approved placing a measure on the June 2, 2026 ballot to permanently ban data centers citywide, and extended the existing moratorium through January 21, 2027. An ordinance permanently banning data centers is also expected to be considered by the Council in April 2026.
objective: Impose 45-day data centers moratorium in Monterey Park (HMC Capital (StratCap))

summary: Naperville City Council voted 6-1 to deny Karis Critical's proposal for a 211,000 sq ft data center on the former Nokia/Lucent campus. A Change.org petition gathered 5,000+ signatures. Originally proposed as two data centers, the developer reduced to one after community opposition. Located near residential areas, Danada Forest Preserve, and a sensory playground for children with disabilities.
objective: Deny Karis Critical 36MW data center permit in Naperville

summary: Pontiac City Council unanimously passed a six-month moratorium on the consideration of data centers 'pending further study and enactment of city regulation.' The resolution cited existing zoning insufficiency to prevent data centers' potential effects on public health, utilities, and infrastructure. City officials will review potential issues and propose a zoning ordinance during the moratorium period, which may be extended.
objective: Halt data centers in City of Pontiac after Concord Infrastructure proposal

summary: Grand Blanc Township Board of Trustees passed a 1-year moratorium on data center development on January 21, 2026, joining the growing list of Michigan communities pausing data center approvals while developing appropriate zoning regulations.
objective: Impose 1-year data centers moratorium in Grand Blanc Township

summary: Pontiac City Council unanimously passed a six-month moratorium on data center applications pending further study and regulation. Mayor Mike McGuinness initiated the proactive measure after receiving data center inquiries.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan (EDRA of MI)
objective: Pause data centers in Pontiac pending regulations

summary: Sylvan Township (near Chelsea, Washtenaw County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data centers on January 22, 2026. Supervisor Amanda Nimke stated: 'We clearly can't stop them, but we can control them in a way that they're not going to be proposed in an area that will undermine a community.'
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Sylvan Township

opposition_groups: Lisle Residents Against Data Center (ad hoc)
summary: Cloud Centers proposed a 256,000 sq ft, 50MW data center on the former Lockformer toxic-contaminated site. Over 300 residents packed a public hearing, forcing postponement. 127 letters submitted, all in opposition. Concerns: diesel generators, noise, proximity to homes (<300 feet) and a grade school. Cloud Centers formally withdrew its application after over 300 residents packed a January hearing, forcing postponement when the crowd exceeded the 250-person board room capacity. 127 written submissions, all opposed. Managing partner said they "were not given a chance to present."
objective: Community pressure forces Cloud Centers to withdraw in Lisle

summary: Meta's $1.5B, 1GW data center broke ground in October 2025. El Paso Electric then quietly filed with the PUC to build a $473 million McCloud natural gas power plant exclusively for Meta, deploying 813 modular generators by 2027. Coalition organizer Verónica Carbajal stated: 'We were told Meta is going green...when we looked at the contracts ourselves, we realized that was an illusion.' El Paso, consistently ranked among the worst cities for ozone, faces air quality and water scarcity concerns in the desert. The City Council voted unanimously to intervene in the PUC regulatory process, citing concerns the plant could eventually be rolled into the general rate base. The Sembrando Esperanza Coalition has called on the city, county, and utilities to terminate their contracts with Meta. Meta subsequently tabled plans for the gas plant amid City Council pushback.
objective: Organize coalition against Meta data center in El Paso

summary: The Village of Romeo enacted a data center moratorium on January 26, 2026, joining the wave of Macomb County communities (including nearby Armada Township, Lenox Township, and Sterling Heights) in pausing data center development while drafting appropriate regulations.
objective: Pause data centers in Village of Romeo pending regulations

summary: Hundreds of residents packed a city council meeting to oppose a data center proposed on the Lutticken Farm along the Mahomet Aquifer, which serves nearly 1 million people. Opposition group has 2,200+ Facebook members. State bill SB 4004 would prohibit data centers from using Mahomet Aquifer groundwater. UPDATE: Pekin Mayor Mary Burress announced on March 9 that the city will not move forward with the proposed data center project. Her announcement was met with applause and cheers from opponents who had filled council chambers for months. The city is expected to vote March 23 to formally end its agreement with developer Western Hospitality Partners for the 321-acre site. Mayor Mary Burress announced on March 9 that the city will not move forward with the data center, met with applause and cheers. The Tazewell County Data Center Opposition Facebook group grew from 2,200 to 5,000+ members in two months.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via SB 4004

summary: Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens. Requires data centers (10+ MW peak) to cover all power demand costs including infrastructure. Prohibits state from overruling local government data center ordinances. Requires notification to local water providers. Passed House 60-7, enrolled and awaiting governor signature. Passed House 60-7, enrolled March 5, 2026. SB 135 ('Data Center Bill of Rights') passed both chambers and was delivered to Gov. Rhoden on March 12, 2026. Requires data centers with 10+ MW peak load to cover all power costs (including stranded costs if they leave), grants local governments authority over siting, and mandates water usage transparency. Separate moratorium bills (for 50+ MW facilities) failed Feb 18.  Governor Rhoden signed SB 135 on March 25, 2026.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SB 135

summary: Larimer County Commissioners voted 3-0 for a 30-day moratorium on data center permit applications on January 27, 2026, extended to 6 months after Feb 9 public hearing. Spurred by inquiries from two data center developers. County developing specific land-use regulations for data centers.
objective: Impose 30-day data centers moratorium in Larimer County

summary: Originally revoked data center sales tax exemptions; amended to impose 1% sales tax on data centers with proceeds going to host local governments. Includes controversial provision removing local zoning control for less-desirable agricultural land. Passed House 54-45, stalled in Senate.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions in IN

project_name: Franklin-Simpson Data Storage and Service Center
summary: TenKey LandCo I LLC proposed a nearly $5 billion data center on approximately 200 acres near Exit 2 of I-65 at the southern end of Franklin. Plans include three 200,000 sq ft buildings ($1.6B per facility). A lawsuit hearing was held March 17, 2026, where the judge took motions under advisement. The next hearing is scheduled for April 13, 2026.
objective: Legal challenge to TenKey LandCo data center in Simpson County

opposition_groups: Deep South Center for Environmental Justice
East New Orleans Neighborhood Advisory Commission
Deep South Center for Environmental Justice (DSCEJ)
summary: City Council voted unanimously (6-0) to enact a one-year moratorium on data centers, crypto mining, and server farms while working on zoning definitions. City Council voted unanimously on January 28, 2026 to impose a temporary moratorium on data centers, with council signaling it will likely become permanent. MS Solar Grid Data pulled its zoning permit for the New Orleans East project hours after the vote.
objective: Halt data centers and crypto mining in New Orleans after MS Solar Grid Data proposal

summary: Planning & Zoning Commission denied Corvus Investment Group's request to rezone 240+ acres to Business Park (which allows data centers) and recommended Light Industrial zoning instead.
objective: Block Corvus Investment Group rezoning of 240 acres in Fayetteville

summary: Atlas Development filed a Development of Regional Impact for 'Project Bus,' a 12-building data center campus on approximately 350 acres in Temple (northern Carroll County). The estimated $11 billion project would generate $42 million/year in ad valorem taxes and 275 jobs. The project requires rezoning, annexation, and sewer connections. Properties span Vaughn Road, Center Point Road, Terrell Road, and Mandeville Road, north and south of I-20, about 40 miles west of downtown Atlanta.
objective: Block Atlas Development rezoning of 350 acres in Carroll County

opposition_groups: Wyndham Board of Directors Smart Development Task Force
summary: HHHunt's proposed Hunting Hawk Technology Park — a 10-building, 400-acre data center campus near the Wyndham community capable of supporting up to 900 MW — was rejected by Hanover's Planning Commission on January 15, 2026 as not fitting the county's 'rural character.' Hundreds of residents from Hanover, Henrico, and Goochland counties attended community meetings, raising concerns about traffic, water use, noise, and environmental impacts. HHHunt withdrew its application on January 28, 2026. Just weeks later, developer Tract submitted a new 427-acre data center proposal along Route 33 and Mountain Road (requiring 900 MW and ~600,000 gallons/day of water), but the Planning Commission deferred that vote to March 19 for more information.
objective: Deny HHHunt 900MW data center permit in Hanover County

company: Surge Development LLC / GM Development LLC
summary: Henry County Commissioners voted 2-1 on January 28, 2026 to approve a Planned Unit Development for a proposed data center on 585 acres of agricultural land near Knightstown at the intersection of State Road 109 and I-70, despite over 80% of constituents who contacted commissioners opposing the project. Surge Development LLC and GM Development LLC are behind the proposal. Commissioner Susan Huhn cast the lone no vote. The opposition group No Henry County Data Center organized significant resistance, citing loss of farmland, water impacts, and threats to rural character. Three proposed 20-acre data centers would use valuable farm land and water.
objective: Oppose Surge Development data center over water in Henry County

summary: Brennan Investment Group withdrew its $2 billion data center proposal for land south of Penny Road and west of Pond Gate Drive in Barrington Hills, a suburb northwest of Chicago. The withdrawal came 'based on initial feedback' before a scheduled Feb. 2 plan commission meeting. A Change.org petition opposing the project had garnered 1,303 signatures citing concerns about air pollution, water quality, and tax impacts. The village considers the matter closed and canceled the Feb. 2 meeting.
objective: 1303-signature petition opposing Brennan Investment Group data center

summary: Creates Data Center Development Office. Establishes tiered permitting system (Tier 1: 1-10 MW; Tier 2: 11-50 MW; Tier 3: 50+ MW). Requires data centers to pay full infrastructure costs. Mandates water efficiency, noise controls, buffer zones, and decommissioning plans.
objective: Create tiered data center permitting system in South Carolina

summary: CyrusOne (Dallas-based) proposed a 636 MW, 1.4 million sq ft data center complex on 230 acres of farmland in Talkington Township, near Waverly, ~14 miles southwest of Springfield. Investment estimated at $500M. The zoning committee voted 5-3 against a six-month moratorium, then the ZBA recommended CyrusOne receive conditional permitted use. Union members in orange were the project's most vocal supporters. Sierra Club raised concerns about lack of transparency. The Sangamon County Board will vote March 23, 2026. County Assessor Byron Deaner estimated ~$5M/year in new property taxes. ZBA voted 5-0 on Feb. 19 to recommend approval of CyrusOne project. County Board final vote scheduled March 23 at BOS Center. Project would generate $5-6M/year in new property taxes and create 100 permanent jobs (avg. $83K salary) plus 500+ construction jobs. County rejected a proposed 6-month moratorium by 5-3 vote. County board voted 15-13 to TABLE the vote on March 23 after nearly four hours of public testimony. No new vote date set.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Sangamon County (rejected)

summary: UK-based Eneus Energy plans a 2GW, $14B data center campus on 1,785 acres near Harlingen with 16 data halls. The city-owned utility entered an agreement for 4.6M gallons/day of reclaimed wastewater for the project. Residents addressed the City Commission opposing the development, citing water and electricity concerns in the drought-prone Rio Grande Valley. Cameron County Judge Eddie Trevino said the county's authority was limited since the site is outside city boundaries. Harlingen is drafting an ordinance to cushion data center impacts as companies hunt for sprawling acreage in the area.
objective: Restrict Eneus Energy 1785-acre data center in Cameron County

summary: Carroll County Board of Commissioners voted to draft a deferral ordinance placing a temporary moratorium on data center projects, despite no developers having applied. The preemptive action mirrors moratoria already adopted by Frederick, Prince George's, Baltimore, and Montgomery counties as data center opposition spreads across Maryland.
objective: Pause data centers in Carroll County pending regulations

company: Unknown (multiple entities: Buck Canyon Properties LLC, High Table LLC, Energy Strike LLC, Hecate Energy)
opposition_groups: Adams County Residents for Responsible Development
summary: A 1,300 MW data center is proposed for the sites of two former power plants in Adams County. AES Ohio filed that the data center would start at 100 MW in 2028 and ramp up to 1,300 MW (1.3 GW) by March 2032, consuming 11.4 million megawatt hours annually -- 31 times Adams County's total electricity consumption. Residents packed a community meeting in Manchester. Alex Schaffer, a data center critic running for Adams County Commission, is organizing opposition. The Economic Development Director signed NDAs about the project. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers issued Nationwide Permit No. 39 on February 18, 2026, for the data center project near the former Stuart power plant. The permit includes special conditions to protect endangered bats, mussels, and butterflies, and a 100-foot buffer zone around three cemeteries. Additionally, Adams County voters launched a petition campaign at West Union Walmart on March 9 calling for an Ohio constitutional amendment to ban data centers statewide.
objective: Petition to block Unknown data center in Adams County

summary: Business owners and citizens of Urbana and Champaign County oppose a proposed data center near a new school, local nursing home, and condominium residences. Cedar Bog Nature Preserve has publicly supported the opposition effort. The petition highlights quality of life impacts including noise, air quality, and traffic concerns.
objective: Petition to block Thor Equities data center in Champaign County

summary: A proposed 26MW Solstice data center campus in Allen Park, Michigan has been postponed after calls for greater scrutiny. A petition gathered 1,535+ signatures, protesters rallied outside city hall, and a second petition targets non-residents in surrounding Wayne County cities. Despite a Q&A session, opposition remains fierce.
objective: Petition to block Solstice data center in Allen Park

summary: Vienna Township in northeast Ohio is using a pause on data centers to develop lasting zoning changes rather than a temporary moratorium. Trustee Phil Pegg requested zoning to place a limit of 85 decibels at the property line, and if a data center uses more than 5 megawatts of power, they must produce anything in excess on site or an adjacent site. All water usage and discharge must meet Ohio EPA regulations.
objective: Pause data centers in Vienna Township pending regulations

summary: Pacifico Energy's GW Ranch project in Pecos County, ~17 miles north of Fort Stockton, received TCEQ approval in early 2026 as the 'largest permitted data center campus' in the U.S. The 7.65 GW, 8,000+ acre facility was granted the largest air pollution permit in U.S. history (33 million tons/year GHG, approximately 5% of Canada's total emissions). On March 13, 2026, the U.S. Senate EPW Committee sent a letter to the Pacifico CEO questioning the project's environmental impacts.
objective: Rally against Pacifico Energy data center in Pecos County

summary: Solon Township (Kent County) Board unanimously approved a 6-month moratorium on new data center proposals in early February 2026, extended from an initial 90-day proposal after hearing residents' concerns. Developers are exploring farmland west of US-131, between 16 and 17 Mile roads, as a potential data center site. The property is currently zoned agricultural/residential.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Solon Township

summary: California-based Atmosphere Data Centers plans a 300 MW data center complex on 170 acres at the site of a decommissioned coal-fired power plant in Dickerson. Atmosphere filed for water withdrawal and discharge permits from the Potomac River upstream from the WSSC water intake that serves millions in the DC metro area. The Sugarloaf Citizens Association and a coalition of 20+ environmental organizations are calling for a moratorium on data center approvals until the County Council approves regulations. County Executive Elrich said the developers 'have a right to go forward' in the planning process, though he has floated a six-month moratorium. The data center debate has fueled election-year tensions.
objective: Halt data centers in Dickerson after Atmosphere Data Centers proposal

summary: Would prohibit localities from granting final approvals for rezonings, special permits, and site plans for new data centers until existing grid interconnection requests are fulfilled or July 1, 2028. Introduced by Del. Irene Shin (D-Fairfax County). A House subcommittee voted to carry the bill over to the 2027 session, effectively killing it for 2026. Virginia's legislature considered over 60 data center-related bills this session.
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
objective: Require conditional use permits for data centers in VA

summary: Washington Township, in the Columbus metro area, passed a moratorium on data center development as part of the broader wave of Ohio municipalities pushing back against rapid data center growth. The township is one of approximately 18 Ohio municipalities that have considered or enacted moratoriums pausing construction and approval of data centers, driven by concerns about rising electricity prices and infrastructure strain in communities that are experiencing the state's data center boom.
objective: Pause data centers in Washington Township pending regulations

summary: Requires data center owners to pay full infrastructure costs (SB 2128), establishes data center impact reviews (SB 2653), addresses tariff structures (SB 1999), amends qualified data center tax provisions (SB 2584), and ties WARN Act/labor compliance to tax credits (HB 2047). Multiple bills pending in the 114th General Assembly, deferred to Senate Commerce and Labor Committee hearing March 10, 2026.
objective: Require data center owners to pay full infrastructure costs in TN

summary: HB 2756 protects retail electricity customers from data center grid connection costs. Additional bills HB 2795 (preempts counties from preventing SMR construction near data centers) also advanced. Several other proposals (HB 2452, HB 4009, HB 2457) were killed or failed.
objective: Require data centers to fund grid upgrades via HB 2756

summary: Prohibits utilities from serving data centers with 100+ MW aggregate contract capacity unless the Public Service Commission approves a specific tariff with minimum requirements including contract durations, early termination fees, load ramp terms, and financial assurances.
objective: Commission legislative study on data center impacts in KY

summary: Deltona Corp proposed an 813-acre AI data center near CR 491 and Tram Road in Lecanto. The Planning & Development Commission voted 4-3 to postpone any decision. Nearly 200 residents packed meetings to oppose heavy industrial rezoning of the site. County commissioners are exploring new guidelines before any data center approvals.
objective: Block Deltona Corp rezoning of 813 acres in Citrus County

summary: Saugatuck Township in Allegan County passed a resolution enacting a moratorium on data center development in February 2026.
objective: Pause data centers in Saugatuck Township pending regulations

summary: Would permanently ban data centers from using Mahomet Aquifer groundwater, the sole-source drinking water for ~1 million people across 14 counties. Also prohibits local governments from entering NDAs with data center companies and requires transparency in water usage.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs in IL

summary: Montgomery County enacted a moratorium on new data center development through the end of 2026. The zoning text amendment ZTA 26-01 is advancing through the process to make data centers a conditional use only in industrial zones, requiring 500-foot setbacks and other conditions. The Dickerson data center project is exempt from the moratorium. Public hearing held Feb. 24, 2026.
objective: Require use permit for Atmosphere Data Centers data center in Montgomery County

summary: Baltimore County Council unanimously passed a moratorium temporarily freezing data center development through January 1, 2027, blocking permits until the Planning Board studies potential environmental and economic impacts. The unanimous vote underscored bipartisan concern about unregulated data center growth in the county.
objective: Halt data centers in Baltimore County after Security Land and Development proposal

company: Meta (nearby Middleton Township project triggered area moratoriums)
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Maumee City Council approved a 12-month moratorium on Feb. 2, 2026, joining the wave of northwest Ohio communities responding to Meta's $800M data center in nearby Middleton Township. Around 18 municipalities across the region have considered or enacted similar pauses.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Maumee (Meta)

summary: The Ashville Village Council voted 4-2 to deny EdgeConneX's annexation request for a 490-acre parcel needed for a 680-acre, 1.8GW data center campus about 17 miles south of Columbus. EdgeConneX already owns the adjacent 200-acre parcel and planned 18 major structures including a 1.8GW substation. The village had previously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on data centers on Dec. 20, 2025. Opposition was fierce since residents became aware of the project in November 2025. The company may still build on the smaller 200-acre site.
objective: Deny EdgeConneX 1800MW data center permit in Ashville

summary: Lenox Township Board of Trustees adopted a 4-month moratorium on new data center development effective February 2, 2026, with the option for one additional 4-month extension. The moratorium gives the township time to review zoning, infrastructure capacity, utility demands, and land use impacts. The township emphasized that under Michigan law, municipalities cannot use zoning or moratoria to permanently block lawful development (exclusionary zoning).
objective: Impose 4-month data centers moratorium in Lenox Township

summary: Change.org petition with 1,052 signatures opposing TeraWulf's data center at the former Century Aluminum smelter site in Hawesville. TeraWulf (via Justified DataPower LLC) purchased the 750-acre site for $200 million and plans to invest $3-4 billion to build a 'clean digital infrastructure' campus with 480 MW of existing power availability. Phase 1 targeted for late 2027. The Kentucky Conservation Committee (led by Lane Boldman) has voiced opposition, calling it a 'missed opportunity for clean aluminum manufacturing.' Kentucky House Bill 544 (Ratepayer Protection Act) was proposed in response. Community concerns include electricity rate increases and limited permanent employment (~100 jobs).
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in KY

summary: Mason City Council reviewed a referendum petition with 700+ signatures seeking to repeal Ordinance 266 (M-3 Technology Innovation zoning district) passed Feb. 2. Residents say the ordinance is 'dangerously inadequate' and doesn't sufficiently address lithium battery usage and noise concerns. The council is waiting on a legal opinion before deciding whether to repeal the ordinance or put it to voters. Mayor blamed 'outsiders' for the pushback, fueling further controversy.
objective: Force public referendum on data center in City of Mason

summary: QTS Data Centers proposed a $12B project involving five data center buildings on nearly 1,600 acres of farmland in the Town of Vienna. The 'No Data Center in DeForest' group mobilized rapidly, gaining 4,000+ Facebook followers in a village of 10,000. They printed yard signs, created an online community, and voiced opposition at numerous town and village board meetings. The DeForest Village Board voted unanimously on Feb. 3, 2026 to reject the annexation. QTS formally withdrew its application.
objective: Deny QTS Data Centers 1600MW data center permit in Village of DeForest

summary: Diode Ventures' Gemstone Technology Park received a 3-2 approval (Mayor breaking tie) to rezone 620 acres for a data center requiring 600-800MW of power, equal to about 20% of Idaho Power's current generation capacity. Layne Thornton and Toledo Dairy filed a petition for judicial review against the city, citing traffic impacts on narrow Locust Grove Rd, power and water usage, and precedent for future farmland conversion. The Murray family warned that allowing industrial development 'sets a precedent, and soon the surrounding farmland follows.' Diode Ventures hopes to break ground in 2026 but acknowledged the timeline might be optimistic.
objective: Challenge Diode Ventures rezoning in Kuna in court

summary: The Sterling Heights City Council unanimously voted to impose a one-year moratorium on any new data centers proposed in the city. Officials noted that the city's zoning restrictions, written in 1978, do not reflect modern computing infrastructure. The pause allows the planning and legal departments to study and develop new zoning regulations. Sterling Heights is part of a broader wave across southeast Michigan, where at least 27 communities now have active data center moratoria.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Sterling Heights

summary: Lodi Township (Washtenaw County) enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development on February 3, 2026. Located near other Washtenaw County communities opposing data centers including Saline, Pittsfield Township, and York Township.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Lodi Township

summary: 1778 Rich Pike LLC / Quantm Group plans 'Project Gouldsboro,' a 35-building, 1.5GW data center campus across Clifton and Covington townships on nearly 1,000 acres. Clifton Township supervisors approved a settlement with the developer in January 2026, but County Court of Common Pleas Judge Mark Powell vacated the settlement on January 30, 2026, ruling it had excluded the township's zoning hearing board. Covington Township supervisors voted to add data centers to its zoning, hearing extensive resident concerns about water resource impacts. A Change.org petition with 180+ signatures demands a statewide data center moratorium. Residents fear the project will negatively impact water resources across Northeast Pennsylvania.
objective: Halt data centers in Covington Township after 1778 Rich Pike / Quantm Group proposal

opposition_groups: DE-CLEAR (Delaware Data Center Coalition for Local, Environmental, and Accountable Regulation)
summary: Starwood Digital Ventures proposed 'Project Washington,' a 1.2GW datacenter on 580 acres near Delaware City. The facility would consume as much power as ~1 million homes, nearly twice all housing units in Delaware. Consumer advocates predict power bills could rise $70/month. Delaware's DNREC denied the Coastal Zone permit, ruling the project is 'heavy industry not compatible with the Coastal Zone.' Starwood is appealing. Dozens of residents pushed back at a tense town hall.
objective: Uphold denial of Starwood Digital Ventures data center on appeal

company: Trammell Crow Company (White Oak), Pumpkin Technology Campus developer (unnamed)
opposition_groups: Citizens for Open Governance, Columbia County Georgia
summary: Harlem resident Gregory P. Guido Jr. filed two judicial review petitions in Columbia County Superior Court challenging the Board of Commissioners' approval of data center rezoning. The first case targets the White Oak Business Park project; the second targets the Pumpkin Center project. Petitions allege: commissioners approved rezonings without verified water or sewer capacity analysis, relied on an outdated June 2025 traffic study, and failed to provide meaningful notice to the School District. Cases were filed under Georgia's new judicial review code which places burden of proof on the petitioner. Commissioners had earlier rejected a third data center proposal (Appling-Harlem Road) in a 3-2 vote.
objective: Challenge Trammell Crow Company rezoning in Columbia County in court

company: DB STU LLC (identity undisclosed; represented by Vorys, Sater, Seymour and Pease LLP)
opposition_groups: Mt. Orab Residents for Responsible Development
summary: Village Councilman Eric Lang introduced two ordinances for a 180-day data center moratorium amid community outcry over a mysterious 1,000-acre 'mega site' project requesting up to 2,000 MW. A Facebook group grew to 1,700+ members and nearly 1,000 residents signed a petition. Council members had signed NDAs about the project.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Mt. Orab (DB STU)

summary: American Tower Corporation withdrew its rezoning petition for a proposed 20,000-square-foot data center near W. 79th Street and Township Line Road in Pike Township, Indianapolis, after sustained pushback from the community. The withdrawal has been confirmed as final.
objective: Block American Tower Corporation data center through opposition in Pike Township

summary: Would revoke existing data center tax incentives established in 2021, which exempted qualifying data centers from state property and other taxes. Part of broader legislative push to reconsider data center tax breaks.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions in CT

summary: Would have imposed a one-year moratorium on construction or expansion of hyperscale data centers. Tabled by Senate State Affairs Committee 5-3 on Feb 18, 2026.
objective: Enact statewide data center moratorium in SD

summary: South Carolina S. 867, the 'Data Center Development Act', introduced January 29, 2026, is sponsored by Sens. Tom Davis (R-Beaufort, primary sponsor), Kimbrell, Garrett, Sutton, Walker, Blackmon, Turner, Zell, Kennedy, and Devine. The bill requires a state permit before a new data center can operate, and regulates permitted data centers on locations, water usage, infrastructure costs, and financial assurances. Sen. Davis described it as a 'middle ground' approach. Separately, H4583, the 'South Carolina Data Center Responsibility Act', is sponsored by Reps. April Cromer (R-Anderson), Gilreath, Oremus, Chumley, White, Reese, and Atkinson. Additional related bills include S. 724 (commercial data center water usage reporting) and S. 3401 (technology transparency). A gigawatt-scale data center proposal in the Lowcountry drew significant opposition from local residents and a bipartisan group of legislators representing Colleton County.
objective: Mandate data center water usage disclosure in SC

summary: Landowners Miles Crosby and Jennifer Singleton sued Colleton County challenging a new zoning ordinance allowing 'special exception' approval for data centers in rural areas. Eagle Rock Partners applied under the new rule to build an 859-acre, 1,000 MW data center complex near the ACE Basin, one of the largest protected coastal ecosystems on the East Coast. Seven state legislators from both parties signed a bipartisan proclamation opposing the project. Southern Environmental Law Center is representing the plaintiffs.
objective: Challenge TIF financing for EagleRock Partners data center

summary: Alabama Senate Bill 270, sponsored by Sen. Lance Bell, would require data center operators using 150+ MW to pay the full cost of grid infrastructure upgrades. The bill requires the Public Service Commission to ensure contracts recover incremental infrastructure costs from data centers and to determine that contracts promote positive benefits to all other retail electric customers without shifting costs to existing ratepayers. This is part of a broader legislative push alongside other bills targeting data center incentives and utility regulation. SB 270 passed the Alabama Senate unanimously 30-0 on approximately March 12, 2026. The bill is now pending in the Alabama House.
objective: Shield ratepayers from data center costs via SB 270

opposition_groups: Muhlenberg TWP and Community Against Data Center and/or Power Distribution Build
summary: Muhlenberg Township (Pickaway County, near Circleville) passed a 12-month moratorium on new data centers after a petition gathered 700+ signatures. Key concerns include proximity to schools, potential for private wells to dry up, and impacts on the rural residential character of the township. Darbyville is within Muhlenberg Township. The resolution (2026-10) expires February 3, 2027.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Muhlenberg Township (Pacifico Energy)

summary: New York S.9144 (Senate, Sen. Liz Krueger, co-prime sponsor Sen. Kristen Gonzalez) / A.10141 (Assembly, Asm. Anna Kelles), introduced February 6, 2026, would impose a moratorium of at least 3 years and 90 days on permits for new data centers designed to use 20+ MW. During the moratorium, the DEC must issue a generic environmental impact statement covering energy, water, air quality, GHG emissions, and e-waste; the PSC must assess rate impacts and protect residential customers from cost increases. Would not affect the public Empire AI research initiative in Buffalo. Described as the 'strongest-in-the-nation' data center moratorium. Additionally, S.6394 (the NY Sustainable Data Centers Act) requires data centers to source 1/3 renewable energy by 2030, 2/3 by 2035, and 100% by 2040. S.8540 (the AC/DC Act) addresses rate classification and cost responsibility for data center operators. Over 100 community, environmental, and faith organizations called for passage of S.9144/A.10141 in a March 2026 letter to Gov. Hochul.
objective: Enact statewide data center moratorium in NY

summary: The POWER Act (SB 4016/HB 5513) establishes comprehensive statewide standards for data center development including clean energy procurement requirements, utility cost responsibility, water use reporting, pollution controls, and community engagement. Requires data centers to submit clean energy supply plans and establishes new rate tariffs for hyperscale data centers. Governor Pritzker separately announced a two-year suspension of state tax incentives for new data center developments effective July 1, 2026. Data Center Coalition called the bill potentially harmful to the market. Competing Republican package led by Sen. Chapin Rose includes SB 4004 (permanent Mahomet Aquifer groundwater ban for data centers). Gov. Pritzker announced a two-year suspension of state tax incentives for new data centers effective July 1, requiring General Assembly approval. Industry coalition calls the POWER Act 'market-closing.'
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SB 4016

opposition_groups: Tucker County WV Data and Power
summary: Fundamental Data, a Virginia-based company, applied for an air permit for the 'Ridgeline Facility' — a natural gas power plant and data center campus between Thomas and Davis that could span 10,000 acres across Tucker and Grant counties. The West Virginia DEP issued an air quality permit authorizing construction of a 785MW natural-gas-fired power plant with diesel backup generators. In February 2026, the West Virginia Air Quality Board upheld the air permit, denying appeals from Tucker United, the West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club. The board sided with developers despite two days of testimony from citizen groups. Opposition groups argue the project threatens the area's tourism-based economy and rural character. A state law passed in 2025 stripped local authority over data center siting.
objective: Oppose Fundamental Data air permit in Tucker County

summary: Bipartisan bill HB 609 would limit sales tax exemptions to data center-specific equipment (excluding construction materials), sunset the exemption in 2036, and require data centers built after March 2026 to pay power rates that fully cover utility costs. Rep. Mickelsen separately introduced a bill to prevent the Idaho PUC from approving any new customer requiring 20+ MW without proving it would not raise rates for other customers. Multiple bills addressing tax breaks, water usage reporting, and rate protection.
objective: Reduce data center tax exemptions via HB 609

company: Undisclosed ('one of the big five or six')
opposition_groups: No Data Centers in the Driftless
summary: An unnamed company is seeking 400-500 acres in Cassville for a $1B data center. The 'No Data Centers in the Driftless' grassroots campaign was organized by Lemon Door restaurant owner Melodie Betts and led by Mount Hope resident Pete Moris. About 20 concerned citizens met at Lemon Door in Fennimore on Jan. 5, 2026 for an informational meeting. The group plans a webpage, Facebook page, and additional informational meetings. The Grant County Economic Development Corp. expects to learn by spring 2026 whether the proposal advances.
objective: Oppose Undisclosed data center in Cassville

summary: Requires electric utilities and new data centers (100+ MWH peak annual demand) to agree on contract terms that shield other power customers from construction and operation costs. Includes minimum billing requirements, performance/credit provisions, and termination protections. Passed Georgia House 159-5, now in Senate.
objective: Require utility-data center contracts shielding other customers

summary: Suspends new sales and use tax exemptions for data centers from July 1, 2026 through June 30, 2027. Prohibits local governments from entering into nondisclosure agreements related to data center water and electricity usage.
objective: Suspend data center tax exemptions and ban water NDAs in Georgia

summary: Montour County commissioners unanimously denied Talen Energy's request to rezone 800+ acres from agricultural to industrial to allow a co-located data center near the Montour Preserve nature area. The data center was expected to serve Amazon. Commission chair Rebecca Dressler said 'No evidence was provided demonstrating that the benefits outweigh documented and foreseeable public harms.' Concerned Citizens of Montour County grew from 4 members to hundreds in months; a petition gathered about 3,000 signatures, roughly twice the population of the relevant township. The rezoning denial stands. Montour County commissioners unanimously denied Talen Energy's request to rezone 800+ acres from agricultural to industrial for a data center near the Montour Preserve. Talen Energy said it still plans to develop a project and will refine plans. The grassroots group Concerned Citizens of Montour County grew from 4 members to hundreds of supporters.
objective: Deny Talen Energy / Amazon rezoning of 800 acres of farmland

summary: Hood County (40 miles SW of Fort Worth) has received at least five data center applications. Commissioners voted down a proposed moratorium 3-2, twice, despite 8 hours of public testimony. The 2,100-acre Comanche Circle project sits in a recharge zone for the Upper Trinity Aquifer and could use 1M gallons/day. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt sent a letter to AG Paxton warning that counties lack constitutional authority to impose moratoriums, chilling local opposition. Residents are still adjusting to a nearby bitcoin mining operation in Granbury where neighbors report constant noise affecting people, pets, and wildlife. Hood County commissioners rejected the moratorium a second time on Feb 25, 2026, in a 3-2 vote. Instead, they voted to seek an opinion from TX Attorney General Ken Paxton on whether counties have authority to enact development moratoriums. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt warned counties lack constitutional authority for moratoriums. Residents called for commissioner resignations after the second defeat.
objective: Propose data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Hood County (rejected)

opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence
summary: Dutch-based Nebius plans a hyperscale data center scaling to 1.2 GW -- 18 times the electricity of all 55,000 Independence households combined. The 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' Facebook group, co-founded by resident Rachel Gonzalez, grew to 2,500+ members within weeks. Over 200 residents packed a February 10 meeting. Despite overwhelming opposition, the Independence City Council voted on March 2, 2026 to approve the project, issuing $150 billion in industrial revenue bonds. Nebius received a 90% personal property tax abatement and will not pay real property taxes. A Change.org petition was also created. Nebius is projected to pay $651M in PILOT fees over 20 years. After council approved the project 5-2 on March 2, residents launched a referendum petition drive on March 3 requiring 3,700 signatures from registered voters within 30 days to force a public vote. Organizers hope to place the question on the August 2026 ballot. The petition could coincide with April 7 municipal elections. Good Jobs First sent an open letter questioning the deal's terms. The Independence City Council voted 5-2 on March 2, 2026 to approve tax incentives for a Nebius 800MW hyperscale data center campus including over $6.2 billion in property tax abatements and potentially $330 million in additional sales tax breaks. The project involves up to $150 billion in investment and 2.1 million sq ft off Route 78 and Little Blue Parkway. Residents immediately launched a referendum petition requiring 3,700 signatures within 30 days; a Facebook group 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' has ~2,900 members. They hope to place the question on the August 2026 ballot. Good Jobs First published an open letter questioning the deal's terms.
objective: Force public vote on Nebius 1.2GW data center in Independence

summary: Oregon City Council initially voted 3-2 with one abstention against granting a six-month extension to a developer for a proposed $2 billion data center near Corduroy and Wynn roads. However, the city law director later ruled that under Robert's Rules of Order, the abstention didn't count as a vote, meaning the ordinance actually passed 3-2. The situation remains contentious and legally uncertain.
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
objective: Deny Capacity 500MW data center permit in City of Oregon

summary: Hayes Township (Charlevoix County) Board of Trustees unanimously enacted a 180-day moratorium on data centers on February 10, 2026, despite no current data center proposals. After a full hour of public comment in person and over Zoom, the board struck exemption clauses that the public identified as possible loopholes. The board is drafting data center regulations for future zoning codes.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Hayes Township

summary: South Lyon City Council adopted a 12-month moratorium on new data center applications on February 10, 2026, extending it beyond the original proposal of six months to match a similar pause recently enacted in Northville.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in City of South Lyon

summary: Erie Township (Monroe County) enacted a limited moratorium on data center development on February 10, 2026, joining nearby Dundee Township (also in Monroe County) in restricting data center proposals while developing zoning regulations.
objective: Pause data centers in Erie Township pending regulations

summary: Would shift energy infrastructure costs from residential ratepayers to data centers served by Dominion Energy. The SCC estimated residential rates would drop 3.4% (~$5.52/month) while data center rates would increase 15.8%. Dominion supports the bill. Passed nearly unanimously in the Senate. Would save local governments an estimated $8.3M in 2027. The House passed the bill 66-32 on March 3, but the Senate rejected the House substitute 0-39 on March 5, sending it to conference.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in VA

summary: Bernalillo County Commission passed (4-1) the "Holding Data Center Projects Accountable" resolution on Feb 11, 2026, requiring data centers seeking county incentives to operate on 100% renewable energy, fully offset water use, and negotiate workforce standards. Not a moratorium but a conditions/guardrails framework for incentives.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Bernalillo County

company: Unknown (inquiries from out-of-town companies for paper mill site)
summary: Canton Board of Aldermen approved a 12-month moratorium on data center construction, cryptocurrency mining, and server farms after about 50 residents voiced concerns at a public hearing. Prompted by out-of-town companies inquiring about using the site of the town's decommissioned paper mill. No concrete plans exist yet. Residents spoke to the economic hardship after mill closure and skepticism that data centers would replace those jobs, noting facilities may employ only ~100 people permanently.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Town of Canton (Unknown)

summary: Washington HB 2515 would have established regulations for data centers using 20+ MW, requiring utilities to set tariffs preventing financial risk to other ratepayers, mandating annual sustainability reports on energy/water/refrigerant use and emissions, and barring data centers from receiving free carbon credits under the Climate Commitment Act starting 2028. The bill passed the House 51-41 but died in the Senate Ways and Means Committee after aggressive industry pushback. Microsoft, which has ~30 data centers in the state, publicly opposed the bill. Environmental groups, tribal nations, and ratepayer advocates supported it. This is a distinct action from the existing Washington (statewide) entry.
objective: Require sustainability reports and ratepayer tariffs in Washington

summary: Four data center bills in NH: HB 1265 would impose a one-year moratorium on data center construction while a legislative study examines environmental effects. SB 439 enables local planning boards to regulate data centers (passed Senate). HB 1739 would establish tax incentives and grid modernization requirements (recommended to be killed). HB 1724 would require utilities to report on costs and capacity related to data centers.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via HB 1265

summary: B+F Timpanogos proposed converting a warehouse in Provo's East Bay into a 66,000 sq ft, 30MW data center on 9 acres. Mayor Marsha Judkins formally opposed the project, saying an additional data center would not be in residents' best interest. Despite the mayor's opposition, the Planning Commission recommended rezoning approval 5-2 and unanimously approved the project plan. Concerns focused on water usage, the facility's proximity to Rocky Mountain University, and whether approximately 30 jobs would justify the water and power consumption. UPDATE: Provo City Council unanimously denied (7-0) the rezoning request for B+F Timpanogos Tech Center's 66,000 sq ft data center at 1507 S. and 180 East on March 11, 2026. The consensus was the city needed to complete its economic master plan first. Mayor Marsha Judkins also opposed the project.
objective: Block B+F Timpanogos rezoning of 9 acres in Provo

summary: QTS Data Centers plans a $2 billion data center at 2883 Gordon Highway that could be twice the size of Augusta Mall. The company filed to expand to six two-story buildings totaling over 2.15 million sq ft. The project is expected to create 160-220 jobs and could be completed by Q2 2026. The project flew 'under the radar' until local news coverage revealed its scale, surprising neighbors.
objective: Oppose QTS Data Centers data center in Augusta

company: Microsoft (Mount Pleasant, $13.3B project), Meta (Beaver Dam campus via Alliant Energy)
summary: Two Wisconsin PSC dockets address data center rate structures. Docket 6630-TE-113: We Energies proposed a 'Very Large Customer' rate class for customers with electricity demands above 500 MW (targeting hyperscale data centers like Microsoft in Mount Pleasant). Over 200 people joined virtual hearings on Feb 10-11, 2026, with ~85% of speakers opposing the proposal, urging that tech companies pay their full share of infrastructure costs. Critics argue the proposal would only require data centers to pay 75% of infrastructure costs, leaving 25% subsidized by the public. Docket 6680-TE-115: Alliant Energy's Individual Contract Rate Agreement to serve a data center campus in Beaver Dam (widely assumed to be Meta). The PSC required Alliant to refile the contract with fewer redactions after advocates raised transparency concerns. A coalition of 42 environmental justice, faith, conservation, and community organizations submitted formal comments opposing the proposals.
objective: Require environmental justice review for data center siting

summary: Requires new data centers with 20+ MW demand to generate their own power off-grid, with a 10-year transition for existing facilities and $1 million/day penalties for violations. Also requires public disclosure of current and projected utility usage. Introduced by Senators Josh Hawley (R-MO) and Richard Blumenthal (D-CT).
objective: Require data centers to generate own electricity in DC

company: LiquidCool (seeking exemption for Loring data center)
summary: Maine LD 307 ('Resolve, to Establish the Maine Artificial Intelligence Data Center Coordination Council'), sponsored by Rep. Melanie Sachs (D-Freeport), would establish a data center coordination council within the energy resources department to evaluate policy options for data center development. During a February 12, 2026 work session, Sachs proposed a moratorium amendment to pause building, permitting, and establishment of data centers with loads above 20 MW until July 1, 2028. The amendment was opposed by Sen. Matt Harrington (R-York), who said it would harm a data center development in his district. The LiquidCool Solutions AI data center at the former Loring Air Force Base (2 MW, using patented immersion-cooling technology, no water for cooling) would likely not be affected by the moratorium; LiquidCool expects a waiver since the facility is 26 MW total and uses existing underutilized power generation. The Energy, Utilities and Technology Committee voted 8-5 (party-line, Democrats in favor) on March 6, 2026, to advance LD 307 with amendments to the full legislature. The bill imposes a moratorium on data centers with 20+ MW electric load until ~October 2027 and creates a Data Center Coordination Council. Full floor vote has not yet occurred.
objective: Pause data center permits for study via LD 307

summary: Two competing approaches: SB 26-102 requires data centers to pay full electricity and grid investment costs, mandates hourly matching of 100% renewable electricity from new sources starting 2031, and prohibits discounted 'economic development rates' for data centers consuming 30+ MW. The competing HB 26-1030 takes the opposite approach with a 20-year sales/use tax exemption for qualifying data centers.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SB 26

project_name: Wilmington Data Center Campus (nearby trigger for Blanchester moratorium)
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Blanchester Village Council unanimously approved a moratorium on Feb. 12, 2026, after AWS proposed a $3B data center in nearby Wilmington (which was tabled) and other projects emerged in Mt. Orab. Council cited concerns about being 'circled' by surrounding proposals.
objective: Halt data centers in Blanchester after Amazon/AWS proposal

summary: Metrobloks proposed an urban data center on 14 acres at the former Sherman Drive-In site. Residents organized protests citing environmental justice concerns, as research shows Black communities near data centers report worsened air quality. Hearing examiner approved despite opposition; the neighborhood plans to appeal.
objective: Challenge Metrobloks air quality permits in court

company: Microsoft (Mount Pleasant data center), Meta (Beaver Dam data center)
summary: SB 1061 would ban data center construction until the state establishes a data center planning authority, prohibits energy/water cost-shifting to residents, creates a land and community funding mechanism, eliminates subsidies, mandates public reporting, creates pollution regulations, requires 100% renewable energy, requires prevailing wages, restores PSC planning authority, prohibits NDAs between data centers and government, and creates enforcement/penalty structures. Intent is not permanent prohibition but responsible development.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SB 1061

opposition_groups: Walla Walla chapter, Democratic Socialists of America
summary: Port of Walla Walla unanimously approved $36M sale of 553 acres at Wallula Gap Business Park to AWS for a $4.8B, 16-building, 3.4M sq ft data center campus. College Place Mayor objected, saying Port cannot 'wash its hands' of environmental harm. Opposition focused on water consumption, noise, and loss of agricultural heritage.
objective: Regulate data center development in Walla Walla County

summary: York Township (Washtenaw County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development on February 12, 2026, after representatives for developer Sansone Group discussed a possible 1-gigawatt data center on 200 acres of property owned by Toyota at the corner of Willis and Platt roads. The proposed site is roughly 6 miles from Augusta Township's potential $1B data center, 10 miles from the Oracle/OpenAI $7B Saline Township project, and 8 miles from the UMich/Los Alamos Ypsilanti Township data center.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in York Township (Sansone Group)

summary: HB 593 (Rep. Josh Bray, R) requires data centers to cover transmission and infrastructure costs, generate their own power or purchase on the open market, with a $75,000 nonrefundable application fee. Unanimously passed the House Economic Development and Workforce Investment Committee on Feb 26, 2026. A companion bill HB 544 (Reps. Adam Moore & William Lawrence) prohibits utilities from serving data centers with 100+ MW aggregate capacity without a PSC-approved specific tariff. HB 593 cleared the House Economic Development and Workforce Investment Committee on Feb 26, 2026. Requires data centers to sign contracts covering transmission/infrastructure costs, pay $75K non-refundable application fee. Supported by Sierra Club and Kentucky Resources Council.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via HB 593

summary: Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on the development of new data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities on February 13, 2026. The ban applies to all development approvals for data centers, data processing facilities, cryptocurrency mining operations and 'any uses associated with data processing facilities,' including web services, hosting, and genome sequencing. The moratorium applies to unincorporated areas and lasts through February 2027.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Chatham County

summary: Dickson City Borough Council adopted regulations that define, regulate and restrict data centers as special exceptions in the town's light manufacturing district, limiting data centers to Enterprise Street near Eagle Lane. The ordinance halted a proposed Bell Mountain data center campus. About 300 people attended the public hearing, with all 25 speakers either raising concerns about data centers or outright opposing them. Borough council stated 'We are not a sacrifice zone.'
company: Dickson City Development LLC / Kriger Construction
objective: Restrict data centers to industrial zones in Dickson City

summary: The Southern Environmental Law Center and Earthjustice, on behalf of the NAACP, sent a notice of intent to sue xAI and subsidiary MZX Tech LLC for operating 27 unpermitted natural gas turbines (495 MW capacity) at a former power plant in Southaven without Clean Air Act permits. The turbines are allegedly the largest industrial source of NOx in the 11-county Memphis metro area. The Safe and Sound Coalition, a Southaven group, gathered 900+ petition signatures calling for xAI operations to be shut down. At a packed Feb. 17 regulatory hearing, community members expressed concerns about the 41 permanent turbines xAI has applied for. Boxtown, the nearest Memphis neighborhood, faces cancer risk four times the national average. This is a distinct action from the existing Southaven entry covering the $20B investment.
objective: Challenge xAI / MZX Tech air quality permits in court

summary: Hubbard Township trustees voted 2-1 to approve a resolution placing a one-year moratorium on data centers entering the township in February 2026. The township attorney reviewed the moratorium proposal and suggested it would be a wise idea, providing a 12-month window for the zoning commission to engage the public and develop appropriate regulations.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Hubbard Township (Bristolville 25 Developer)

summary: A Scioto County resident filed a lawsuit accusing the county commission of violating Ohio's Open Meetings Act by conducting negotiations with Google for a $1 billion, 500,000-square-foot data center in Franklin Furnace behind closed doors. Commissioners admitted to signing NDAs that prevented them from publicly naming Google or discussing project details for months. Google was given a 75% tax abatement for 15 years. The secrecy sparked a separate legislative response: two Ohio lawmakers introduced a bill to ban NDAs for elected officials tied to their public duties.
objective: Sue over open meeting violations in Google approval

summary: Preserving Montgomery County LLC (formed Dec. 27, 2025 by Sabrina Cope of Truxton) filed a lawsuit in Cole County Circuit Court alleging the commission violated Missouri open meetings and open records laws (Sunshine Law). The lawsuit seeks to void $35B in industrial revenue bonds and the development agreement for Amazon/AWS. Attorney Steve Jeffrey represents the plaintiffs (residents, business owners, farmers). The project is located at the I-70/MO-19 intersection.
objective: Sue over open meeting violations in Amazon/AWS approval

summary: HB 706 bans utilities from recovering data center infrastructure costs by shifting them onto other customers and requires long-term service agreements of at least 12 years. Mirrors AEP's existing tariff requiring data centers to cover 85% of energy costs. Senate Democrats introduced additional bills to eliminate tax breaks, require developers to cover infrastructure costs, and impose greater oversight. Around 18 municipalities have enacted local moratoriums on data centers.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via HB 706

company: Oppidan Investment Co. / Connect Data Centers
opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development
summary: First Minnesota city to enact a data center moratorium. One-year pause through February 17, 2027 on facilities using more than 20 MW or within 500 feet of residential land. Eagan City Council voted 4-0 on February 17, 2026 to adopt the ban after growing concern over data center impacts on residential neighborhoods.
objective: Halt data centers in City of Eagan after Oppidan Investment Co. proposal

summary: San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 at 2:14 AM on Feb. 17 to deny zoning amendments for a $1.5B, 380MW data center by Highlander SM One LLC (Fort Worth) on 200 acres on Francis Harris Lane. Over 100 residents spoke against it during 8+ hours of testimony. The overflow room roared with cheers when the denial vote came. Part of a broader fight in Hays County where 5 data centers have been proposed amid historic drought conditions. San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 at 2:14 AM on February 18, 2026 to deny the Highlander SC One / Maberry data center's rezoning proposal. The proposed $1.5B, 199-acre facility would have consumed 70,000 gallons of water daily (25 million gallons annually). Water supply was the defining issue. The overflow room roared with cheers when the vote came. Maberry must wait six months before resubmitting.
objective: Deny Highlander SM One rezoning of 200 acres in San Marcos

summary: DC BLOX is the company behind the Lowndes County data center proposal. DC BLOX's CEO informed the landowner (Pope Langdale) the facility could create up to 400 jobs with $135,000 average salary and would use closed-loop cooling. Lowndes Citizens Against Data Centers (LCAD), organized with Alpha Kappa Alpha Upsilon and the Valdosta State University Anthropology Club, hosted a town hall where citizens pushed back on the potential development. In fall 2025, a parcel was rezoned for the potential data center.
objective: Block DC BLOX rezoning of 720 acres in Lowndes County

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Richfield Township (Lucas County) passed a 12-month data center moratorium on Feb. 17, 2026, part of a wave across northwest Ohio triggered by Meta's $800M data center in nearby Middleton Township. Residents had packed a June 2025 trustee meeting to oppose a potential rezoning. Joins Maumee, Waterville, and Monclova Township.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Richfield Township

summary: Proposes a three-year moratorium on hyperscale data center construction including associated power generation and transmission infrastructure. Multiple legislators involved: Sen. Katie Muth, Sen. Rosemary Brown (R) who supports restricting data centers to industrial zones, and Rep. Jamie Walsh (R) who plans a separate regulatory package. Bipartisan opposition with over 50 planned data centers facing resistance from community groups across the state.
objective: Enact statewide data center moratorium in PA

company: CloudBurst Data Centers / Highlander SM One LLC
summary: Water advocates identified 5 data centers proposed for the Hays County region during historic drought. County Judge Ruben Becerra proposed a 30-day moratorium on high-water-use building permits but was forced to table it after county attorneys warned it would trigger a lawsuit the county would likely lose. San Marcos City Council scored a major win Feb. 17, voting 5-2 at 2:14 AM to block a $1.5B Highlander SM One data center after 8+ hours of testimony from 100+ residents. Hays County aquifers are at historic lows. Hays County Judge Ruben Becerra proposed a moratorium on permits for large industrial water users (>20,000 gal/day) but the Commissioners Court tabled it on Feb 25 after county attorneys warned it would trigger a lawsuit the county would lose. State Sen. Bettencourt warned counties lack authority for moratoriums. Water advocates have identified 5 potential data center developments in the region and continue organizing.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after CloudBurst Data Centers proposal

summary: Ann Arbor Township Board of Trustees passed a 12-month moratorium on data centers. No developer has approached township officials about a data center; the moratorium is a proactive measure reflecting concerns about water, energy demand, and environmental impacts seen in neighboring communities.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan (EDRA of MI)
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Ann Arbor Township

summary: Columbiana City Council unanimously approved a new ordinance imposing zoning restrictions on future data centers, becoming the first Alabama municipality to pass such restrictions. Requirements include: 60 ft max height (including rooftop equipment), 300 ft setback from residentially zoned districts, 25 ft landscape buffer, sound study demonstrating facility does not exceed 67 dB, and full enclosure or screening/fencing. Future data centers require a special permit from the Board of Adjustments. The restrictions do not apply retroactively to the existing DigiPower X facility (22MW expanding to 55MW by 2027). Came after a packed town hall where residents voiced concerns about noise, water, and energy impacts.
objective: Impose 300-foot data center setbacks in Columbiana

summary: SB 1586 would have brought 373 acres of rural land into Hillsboro's UGB for tech industries, with ~1,400 acres for future industrial use. Sen. Sollman withdrew after pushback. About 1,500 of 1,700 acres are actively farmed. Oregon Ag Trust director said constituents made clear they're 'not interested in poorly planned economic development that destroys farmland.' Hillsboro has seen 84% growth in data center development.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via SB 1586

summary: xAI purchased a power plant in Southaven and plans to invest $20B+ in data centers in DeSoto County (Colossus 2). The company installed 27 unpermitted gas turbines capable of generating 495MW — equivalent to a conventional power plant — without obtaining any permits. The turbines roar 'like jet engines' day and night, causing respiratory problems and insomnia for residents. The Safe and Sound Coalition gathered 900+ signatures demanding shutdown. At a Feb 2026 public hearing packed with hundreds of attendees, not a single person spoke in favor of xAI's proposed permanent air permit. Earthjustice and the SELC, on behalf of the NAACP, sent a 60-day notice of intent to sue for Clean Air Act violations. The facility would likely be the largest industrial source of NOx in the 11-county Memphis metropolitan area. Governor Tate Reeves called it the largest private investment in Mississippi history.
objective: Sue xAI for unpermitted turbines in Southaven DeSoto County
