opposition_groups: Stop Data Centers in Maumee, Monclova, Waterville & Whitehouse
summary: Monclova Township trustees unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on Feb. 18, 2026 after residents packed multiple meetings. Although no data center has been proposed in the township, Meta's nearby facility in Middleton Township prompted preemptive action. Approximately 18 municipalities across northwest Ohio now considering or enacting similar pauses.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Monclova Township

summary: Elmwood Township Board unanimously approved a 9-month moratorium on data centers (reduced from originally proposed 12 months). No known data center plans exist for the county; the moratorium is precautionary. The board tabled the measure at its January 21 meeting before passing the amended version in February.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan (EDRA of MI)
objective: Impose 9-month data centers moratorium in Elmwood Township

summary: Governor Pritzker proposed a 2-year suspension of the Data Center Investment Program (enacted 2019, offers up to 20 years of tax exemptions) during his 2026 budget address. Driven by ICC/IPA/IEPA Resource Adequacy Study projecting capacity shortfalls by 2029 in Northern IL. 37 projects built under the program.
objective: Commission legislative study on data center impacts in IL

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: A Meta data center spanning 715,000 sq ft under construction in Middleton Township was revealed in April 2025, anticipated operational in 2027 with 100 employees. Over 150 residents packed a Wood County Planning Commission meeting demanding transparency about the $800M facility. Concerns include massive water consumption, electricity usage, farmland loss, and environmental and health impacts. A power plant for the data center was fast-tracked and approved with no public hearings. Nearby communities enacted moratoriums in response: Monclova Township approved a 12-month moratorium in February 2026, and Maumee City Council approved a 12-month moratorium on February 2, 2026.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Middleton Township (Meta)

opposition_groups: NJ Build Up Resistance Now (NJ B.U.R.N.)
summary: New Brunswick City Council voted unanimously on Feb 19, 2026 to remove data centers as a permitted use from the Jersey-Sandford Redevelopment Plan, killing the Amzak Capital-backed 27,000 sq ft AI data center proposal. The site at 100 Jersey Avenue was restored to its original designation as public park space. The decision came after hundreds of residents rallied inside and outside City Hall in opposition.
objective: Deny Amzak Capital Management data center on 22 acres in New Brunswick

summary: Iowa HF 2447, proposed by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids), creates new reporting requirements and utility rate structures for data centers. Data centers must file quarterly reports with the Iowa DNR on water usage (source, volume, efficiency) and quarterly energy reports with the Iowa Utilities Commission (power consumption, on-site generators, sustainability metrics like waste heat reuse). All data must be publicly shared online within 30 days. The bill also requires the Iowa Utilities Commission to create a separate rate class for data centers to prevent grid upgrade costs from being shifted to residents. Separately, HF 2261 would create a new water utility customer class for energy facilities requiring 20+ MW. The bill survived legislative funnel week in February 2026.
objective: Create separate utility rate class for large data centers in IA

summary: Over 1,700 residents signed a petition opposing a potential data center on 500+ acres of prime farmland near Shaker Village at Pleasant Hill. Hundreds packed the Mercer County Fairgrounds to voice unified opposition. The petition effort continues to grow as the community mobilizes against the proposal.
objective: Oppose Unknown data center in Mercer County

opposition_groups: Stop Data Centers in Aurora IL
summary: The City of Aurora is developing new data center regulations as its existing moratorium nears expiration. The proposed rules include maximum noise levels of 59 dB daytime and 49 dB nighttime, a ban on roof-mounted chiller units within 1,500 feet of residential/educational/hospital uses, and ground-mounted chillers and generators at least 1,000 feet from such uses. The regulations stem from extensive noise complaints by residents near the existing CyrusOne data center. Over 200 residents voiced concerns at an open house on Feb. 12 and at a Planning and Zoning Commission meeting. CyrusOne's permanent sound solution is due by Sept. 30, 2026.  Council voted March 24 to adopt 'the strongest data center regulations in Illinois' — 56 dB day/46 dB night noise caps, 1,500 ft generator setbacks, mandatory impact studies.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Aurora

summary: Dryden Town Board unanimously banned data centers and cryptocurrency mines, becoming the first town in New York State to outlaw data centers. The ban followed an 18-month land-use moratorium and was driven by concerns over energy draw, water use, and noise.
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Town of Dryden

summary: Three northeast Wisconsin towns -- Two Creeks, Two Rivers, and Mishicot -- passed resolutions calling on Manitowoc County to place a one-year moratorium on new data center permits and construction, with an option to extend for six months. The resolutions were prompted by Texas-based Cloverleaf Infrastructure approaching local landowners with offers to purchase farmland for a data center. The area is targeted due to its proximity to the Point Beach nuclear power plant. Farmers are concerned about losing agricultural land and residents worry about changes to the rural character. The resolutions go to the Manitowoc County Planning and Park Commission, then to the full county board for consideration.
objective: Pause data center permits for statewide study in WI

summary: Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) introduced the AI Data Center Moratorium Act on March 25, 2026, proposing a nationwide halt on new data center construction until Congress passes federal AI legislation establishing worker protections, consumer safeguards, environmental standards, and civil rights provisions. The bill formalizes Sanders' earlier moratorium call from December 2025. It is unlikely to advance — Sen. John Fetterman (D-PA) called it 'China First' and most lawmakers of both parties have rejected moratorium proposals. Food & Water Watch endorsed the bill, saying it is 'desperately needed as Americans' energy bills skyrocket.'
objective: Call for national moratorium on data center construction

summary: Township supervisors voted 3-2 to reject a 120,000 sq ft, three-story 'boutique' data center on 18 acres off Route 724 before the proposal was even formally submitted. Over 100 residents attended, mostly in opposition. Developer Envision Land Use subsequently abandoned the plan entirely. The proposed site was at 299 W. Schuylkill Road, adjacent to Route 100 near a PECO substation.
objective: Deny Envision Land Use data center on 18 acres in North Coventry

summary: Developer of Sentinel Grove Technology Park, a 15-million-square-foot data center in Fort Pierce, withdrew its application in anticipation of Gov. DeSantis's proposed HB 1007 restricting hyperscale data centers on agricultural/sensitive lands.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via HB 1007

summary: Denver Mayor Mike Johnston and city council jointly announced a one-year moratorium on new data center construction, expected to begin spring 2026 pending council approval. The moratorium followed growing opposition, particularly from the GES Coalition in Elyria-Swansea, which delivered a 'Good Neighbor Proposal' to CoreSite opposing their DE3 expansion in one of Denver's most environmentally burdened neighborhoods. The community has faced decades of industrial pollution including the former Vasquez Boulevard/I-70 Superfund site.
objective: Halt data centers in Denver after CoreSite proposal

company: Gemini Data Center SD LLC (Gemini Capital)
opposition_groups: Lincoln County residents (Commissioner Joel Arends supporters)
summary: Lincoln County Commissioner proposed a 1-year moratorium on hyperscale data centers requiring 100+ MW, but no commissioner seconded the motion, killing the measure. The commission voted 3-2 to refer regulations to Planning and Zoning.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium after Gemini Data Center SD proposal

summary: The public hearing on Amazon's $2B data center rezoning was postponed due to a paperwork error (duplicated parcel numbers). The Planning and Zoning Commission hearing is now rescheduled for March 23, 2026. Community opposition remains strong, with dozens of residents packing prior meetings to oppose the 330+ acre industrial rezoning. No decision has been made yet.
objective: Block Amazon/AWS rezoning of 330 acres in Sunbury

summary: Cobb County Board of Commissioners voted unanimously to implement a 180-day moratorium on new data center permits and rezoning applications in unincorporated areas. Resolution cited noise pollution, air quality impacts from cooling systems and backup generators, aesthetics concerns, and significant effects on power and water utilities.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Cobb County (Grindcap (MMM Acquisitions))

summary: Franklin County Commissioners voted unanimously to extend the data center moratorium through February 2027, preventing new applications while the county gathers information and develops regulations for large-scale data center operations. The extended timeline provides additional study time beyond the original one-year term.
objective: Halt data centers in Franklin County after ABEI Energy proposal

summary: Multiple data center bills saw different outcomes: HB 1005 (50-year sales tax exemption for data centers) was defeated 9-3 in House State Affairs Committee. SB 232 (one-year moratorium) passed committee but was killed in full Senate vote. SB 135 ('Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens' from Sen. Karr) passed committee 5-4 - it would set protections for utility ratepayers and resources while banning tax exemptions for data centers. Bills addressing noise levels, minimum distances from residences, and water/electricity regulation were also halted.
bill_name: SB 135 - Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens
objective: Shield ratepayers from data center costs via HB 1005

company: Meta (Lebanon data center), Amazon ($11B Lake Michigan data center), Google ($2B Fort Wayne campus), Sabey (Decatur Township proposal)
summary: Over 200 people from 40 counties attended a 'People's Hearing on Data Centers' at the Indiana State Library on Feb 24, organized by Hoosier Action. Attendees delivered postcards urging legislators and Gov. Braun to regulate data centers. Rep. Shackleford's HB 1245 (requiring IURC to study data center energy impacts) never advanced. Citizens Action Coalition has called for a statewide moratorium since October 2025. Local moratoriums are advancing: Fulton County approved a one-year moratorium on March 3; Starke County and Dearborn County also enacted moratoriums.
objective: Halt data centers in Indiana after Meta proposal

summary: Mooringsport Mayor Tyler Gordon, Michael Craft, and Mary Blakemore filed a petition for judicial review in Caddo District Court challenging the Shreveport City Council's approval of a special use permit for a TXLAAR LLC data center. The lawsuit alleges the city violated transparency laws by using NDAs, and that the facility would use up to 7.5 million gallons of water/day and discharge 2.5 million gallons of contaminated wastewater daily. The Sierra Club Delta Chapter supports the lawsuit and is working to join as co-plaintiffs. The facility would include seven 400,000 sq ft buildings.
objective: Challenge TIF financing for TXLAAR data center

summary: Change.org petition with 650 signatures calling for a ban on data center construction in Everett, MA. Started by Stephanie McColaugh on Feb 24, 2026 and gaining signatures rapidly (453/week). The petition appears to be preemptive rather than targeting a specific announced project, citing concerns about utility rate increases, water depletion, and air/noise pollution. No specific developer or project has been publicly announced for Everett. Massachusetts enacted a data center sales tax exemption in November 2024 and a $3 billion hyperscale facility has been proposed in Westfield.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Everett

summary: West Rockhill Board of Supervisors advanced a preemptive data center zoning ordinance restricting data centers to the township's industrial district adjacent to Hilltown Township and Telford Borough along State Road. Notable requirement: data centers must be powered by solar energy and served by public water and sewer. The ordinance includes definitions, building regulations, and accessory use standards. No data center proposals have been submitted; supervisors are taking proactive action. Public hearing scheduled for April 15, 2026 after review by township and county planning commissions.
objective: Restrict data centers to industrial zones in West Rockhill

summary: East Rockhill Board of Supervisors unveiled a data center regulation ordinance nearly identical to neighboring West Rockhill's. The ordinance restricts data center construction to the township's industrial districts at the Pennridge Airport. While no data center applications have been received, the ordinance serves as a preemptive measure. Township solicitor Will Oetinger stated: 'By enacting an ordinance, we put guardrails around the development of a data center.' The board agreed to revisit the ordinance at its next meeting after gathering additional information.
objective: Restrict data centers to industrial zones in East Rockhill

summary: City of Hilliard is suing to block Amazon's plan to install 228 natural gas-fed solid oxide fuel cell units on a 6-acre site adjacent to its data center campus. The Ohio Power Siting Board approved the project under HB 15, preempting local review. Opposition group 'Protect Hilliard' formed; residents worry about pollutants near an elementary school. NEW: Amazon is also seeking a Title V operating permit for 158 Tier 2 diesel backup generators at 4120 Scioto Darby Creek Rd. Ohio EPA public hearing scheduled March 19, 2026 at Hilliard Community Center. Public comment period through March 25. Residents mailed 160+ signed petition letters to Ohio EPA asking them to require Tier 4 generators instead.
objective: Challenge state preemption of local authority in Hilliard

summary: Portage enacted a temporary moratorium on data centers and battery energy storage facilities through end of year to allow the city to review zoning rules and potential impacts. A public hearing on new ordinance amendments was scheduled for March 2026, part of a broader trend of at least 27 Michigan communities pausing data center development.
objective: Pause battery energy storage systems in City of Portage pending regulations

opposition_groups: Pacific ROOT Coalition (Pacific Residents Organizing for Our Tomorrow Coalition)
summary: Atlanta-based Beltline Energy pitched a $16B, 16-building datacenter campus on 500 acres requiring annexation into Pacific. Hundreds of residents packed Pacific High School in opposition. The Missouri Department of Conservation flagged endangered species habitat on the site. The developer tabled the request and subsequently shifted the same project to unincorporated Franklin County jurisdiction, where it appeared on the March 17, 2026 Planning & Zoning Commission agenda.
objective: Oppose Beltline Energy data center in Pacific

opposition_groups: Phillipsburg Town Council Anti-Data Center Coalition
summary: Phillipsburg Town Council voted unanimously to approve first reading of an ordinance banning data centers in all zones, citing water and energy demands. Final vote and public hearing scheduled for March 10, 2026. Officials referenced the town's Master Plan prioritizing rural character and agricultural heritage.
objective: Ban data centers in all zones in Phillipsburg

summary: Dearborn County Board of Commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on rezoning proposals for commercial solar, battery storage, and data centers effective February 26. The county is collecting public feedback for two months on potential rule changes. No applications will be accepted during the moratorium period.
objective: Pause data centers and solar in Dearborn County pending regulations

summary: Amazon announced $12B investment in three data center campuses across northwest Louisiana (west Shreveport, near Blanchard, and north of Benton in Bossier Parish). Construction expected to begin March 2026. Community opposition centers on water use (plans to draw from Caddo Lake and Cross Lake), air quality concerns in a state already ranking worst for air quality, transparency issues with elected officials signing NDAs, and potential utility cost increases. Mooringsport village officials voted unanimously for an environmental study on Caddo Lake impacts, but the Caddo Commission declined to vote on water restrictions.
objective: Rally against Amazon/AWS data center in Caddo-Bossier

summary: Boone Town Council discussed a moratorium on data center construction, with Mayor Dalton George saying the town should be proactive and 'recognize the environmental damage that these data centers and similar uses like crypto-mining can do.' A public hearing is scheduled for March 23, 2026, to further discuss the moratorium. Modeled after Canton's 12-month moratorium.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Town of Boone

summary: Columbus City Council discussed a proposed $5.18 billion, 900-acre data center campus (Project Ruby) in northeast Muscogee County near Harris and Talbot County lines. About half the citizens at the meeting voiced concerns about environmental and community impacts. A Change.org petition opposes the project. Developer Habitat Real Estate Partners selected Layfield Road for the facility, which would generate 195 permanent jobs and up to $68.7M/year in property taxes by 2030. Next public meeting is March 6, 2026.
opposition_groups: Stop Data Center in Muscogee County
objective: Petition to block Habitat Real Estate Partners data center in Columbus

summary: Community members rallied outside Fort Meade City Hall to protest a proposed 1.2-gigawatt, 4.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center on over 1,000 acres being developed by Stonebridge. The city previously approved rezoning and a tax break, but residents are demanding a halt before a final site plan vote expected in March. Concerns center on massive water and energy drain in a small rural Polk County city, noise from low-frequency sound, and diesel backup generator pollution. The project would be one of the largest data centers in the US.
objective: Block Stonebridge rezoning of 1330 acres in Fort Meade

company: T5 Data Centers (existing); second developer proposed
summary: Kings Mountain City Council voted 5-2 to approve a six-month moratorium on new data centers while the city studies impacts on water supplies, noise, and power costs. One major data center already exists on Shelby Road. Councilmember Annie Thombs said 'That's great, but what else is it going to bring? We don't know. Everything that has a dollar sign in front of it doesn't mean it's good.' The moratorium follows a growing wave of data center opposition across North Carolina.
objective: Halt data centers in City of Kings after T5 Data Centers proposal

summary: Bipartisan House Bills 5594-5596 would create the 'Data Center Regulation Act' and halt construction of new data centers in Michigan until April 1, 2027. The moratorium would give local communities time to assess project ramifications. At least 25 Michigan communities have enacted local data center moratoriums. Gov. Whitmer opposes the bills, calling any legislation preventing economic growth 'an automatic non-starter' that would be vetoed.
objective: Enact statewide data center moratorium in MI

summary: SB 484 establishes a comprehensive framework for regulating large-scale data centers (50+ MW peak load), requiring PSC to adopt baseline tariff and service requirements, preserving local government control over planning. Passed Senate unanimously. HB 1007 is more restrictive, prohibiting hyperscale data centers (25+ MW) on environmentally sensitive lands, imposing siting prohibitions, water permitting restrictions, and banning economic incentives. Multiple bills reflect DeSantis-backed data center restrictions.
objective: Establish comprehensive regulatory framework for 50MW+ data centers

summary: Penzance Management announced a $4B Bedington Campus data center in Berkeley County, the first certified 'High Impact Intelligence Center' under West Virginia's HB 2014. The project is exempt from local zoning, noise, lighting, and land use controls under the state certification framework. The designation represents a significant precedent for state preemption of local data center regulation.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via HB 2014

opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence
summary: Missouri has multiple data center bills. HB 3362 and HB 3364 (Reps. Colin Wellenkamp, Scott Miller, Mike Costlow), titled the 'AI Infrastructure, Grid Integrity and Water Resource Protection Act', would require large-load consumers to pay the full cost of electricity and infrastructure (including grid upgrades and new generation capacity) and create a water permit requirement from the DNR for facilities using 2+ million gallons of fresh water per day or 80% of local available capacity. Such facilities would need to build new freshwater capacity or create closed-loop freshwater systems. Separately, SB 1396 (Sen. Mike Henderson, R-Desloge) would alter 2018 economic development incentives to prevent data centers from taking advantage of energy incentive programs that were not designed for them.
objective: Require closed-loop cooling for data centers in MO

summary: Spring Hill City Council voted on October 23, 2025 to move forward with rezoning 316 acres from Rural Residential to M-1 Industrial for a potential data center, though no end user or specific business has been identified. About 300 homes would be impacted, and Spring Hill High School is only 3/4 mile from the proposed site. Approximately 45 people were originally notified about the rezoning. A public hearing was rescheduled to March 25, 2026. Residents are organizing to fight the rezoning, raising concerns about noise, traffic, property values, and proximity to schools.
objective: Block rezoning of 316 acres in Spring Hill

summary: The Missouri AI Infrastructure, Grid Integrity and Water Resource Protection Act is filed as HB 3362 and HB 3364 by Reps. Colin Wellenkamp, Scott Miller, and Mike Costlow (R-Dardenne Prairie). The legislation would establish separate utility rate structures for large customers with 50+ MW of annual peak demand, and create a permit system for large water users requiring facilities withdrawing 2+ million gallons/day to obtain 5-year permits from the DNR. If water use exceeds 80% of available local capacity, the facility must build out new freshwater capacity. The Missouri Future Caucus has prioritized this legislation. The bills had not yet been sent to committee as of late February 2026.
bill_name: HB 3362 / HB 3364 - AI Infrastructure Grid Integrity and Water Resource Protection Act
objective: Prevent data center cost pass-through to residential rates in MO

summary: Air Products is appealing to the Upper Macungie Township Zoning Hearing Board for a proposed 2.6 million-square-foot, three-building data center complex on their former 194-acre headquarters site along Cetronia Road. The project predates the township's new data center ordinance adopted December 4, 2025, so it would be reviewed under old zoning rules. Air Products requested a delay until May 27, 2026 to decide whether to proceed. Township adopted data center regulations in December 2025 but they won't apply to this project.
objective: Legal challenge to Air Products data center in Upper Macungie

summary: Planning commission meeting was overwhelmed when too many residents showed up to oppose an expanded data center proposal, forcing officials to reschedule to March 9 at a larger venue. Sentinel Data Centers and Green Fig Land LLC seek to expand a previously approved data center by 61% to over 1.6 million sq ft on roughly 100 acres at 13 S. Bacton Hill Rd. The project straddles East and West Whiteland townships.
company: Sentinel Data Centers / Green Fig Land LLC
objective: Rally against Sentinel Data Centers data center in East Whiteland

summary: Brookings City Council voted unanimously to reject a $644K sales-tax rebate for Sequitor Edge's proposed $41 million co-location data center. The denial came alongside state-level defeats: the House State Affairs Committee voted 9-3 against a 50-year data center sales tax break, and the Senate killed two data center incentive bills in a chaotic session when a key senator went missing and couldn't be located even by the governor.
opposition_groups: Brookings City Council members (tax rebate opposition)
objective: Deny Sequitor Edge data center permit in Brookings

summary: Would bar state and local governments from granting permits, approvals, or incentives for new data centers until January 1, 2028. Referred to House Ways and Means Committee. Introduced after mounting statewide opposition including the defeat of Project Spero in Spartanburg and ongoing fights in Marion, Colleton, and York counties.
objective: Block data center permits pending new regulations in SC

summary: Hall County Commission voted 4-0 to approve a 180-day moratorium on data centers, planned industrial development, and detention centers. Chairman David Gibbs noted the moratorium can be extended 'months or years.'
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Hall County

summary: Comprehensive energy affordability bill that passed the House 128-27. Includes data center provisions to protect ratepayers from subsidizing data center energy costs. Also addresses clean energy procurement, nuclear energy restrictions, and offshore wind deadlines. Now in Senate.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in MA

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Spartanburg SC
summary: TigerDC formally withdrew its $3 billion 'Project Spero' AI data center from Tyger River Industrial Park after Spartanburg County Council unanimously voted down two Fee-In-Lieu-Of-Tax (FILOT) agreements. Weeks of strong public opposition, a 'No Data Centers in Spartanburg' Facebook group with nearly 3,000 members, and a petition with 4,000+ signatures sealed the project's fate. The project would have employed only 50 people. County councillors refused the tax incentive after backlash over energy costs, health, and environmental concerns. UPDATE: More than two dozen protesters gathered at the Spartanburg County courthouse on March 7, then marched down Magnolia Street chanting about data center concerns. Northmark Strategies has started construction on one project; a second project is planned at 300 Jones Road. Separately, SC utility companies report data center proposals declining in the state.
objective: Deny TigerDC 400MW data center permit in Spartanburg County

summary: Scranton Materials LLC filed an appeal after the Ransom Township Board of Supervisors rejected its zoning amendment application that would have permitted data centers at 819 Newton Road. The company argues the township's zoning ordinance unlawfully excludes data centers, claiming Pennsylvania municipalities must allow every type of lawful land use somewhere within their borders. The proposal drew dozens of residents and elected officials who opposed the plans at multiple public hearings. The township's existing noise and building height requirements were cited as too restrictive for data centers.
objective: Sue Scranton Materials over noise violations

summary: Marana residents filed a lawsuit against the Town of Marana after the Town Clerk rejected referendum petitions with over 2,800 signatures that sought to force a public vote on the town council's Jan. 6 unanimous rezoning approval for Amazon's Project Blue data center on two 300-acre parcels. The petitions were invalidated on a technicality -- they omitted legal property descriptions required for zoning ordinances. A Pima County judge heard arguments on March 4. Three residents also launched town council campaigns in response. Separately, the No Desert Data Center Coalition filed suit against Pima County for approving a related land sale and rezoning.
objective: Challenge Beale Infrastructure rezoning in Marana in court

summary: Weld County commissioners are developing a data center zoning ordinance (Code Ordinance 2026-01) that would limit data centers to industrial or agricultural zones and require site plan or special review. Triggered by Global AI's purchase of 438 acres near Windsor for a data center. The county extended the timeline for the ordinance's final reading to allow more public input. Facilities under 50MW total capacity would fall under these rules.
objective: Restrict Global AI 438-acre data center in Weld County

summary: The Town of Leland initiated a preemptive text amendment to regulate data center facilities, requiring closed-loop cooling systems, a 60% renewable energy mandate, and restricting data centers to heavy industrial districts via conditional rezoning only. No data center has been proposed within town boundaries, but staff acted proactively given rapid regional growth of the industry, particularly nearby Microsoft plans for a 1,385-acre campus in North Carolina. A public hearing and final decision by Leland Town Council is scheduled for March 19, 2026.
objective: Block rezoning of 1385 acres in Leland

summary: Amazon's $10 billion data center campus in Canton, the largest capital investment in Mississippi history, occupies 1,713 acres across two sites in Madison County. Construction has transformed the area with major road widening and utility upgrades. AP and national coverage revealed 700+ diesel generators powering the site, with residents of the Idlebriar neighborhood reporting dust, asthma, and constant noise complaints. The scale of diesel generation has drawn scrutiny over air quality impacts in the surrounding community.
objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center over noise in Canton

summary: Mason County planning commission approved an ordinance paving the way for a 2,000-acre data center by an undisclosed company. The ordinance has been formally adopted despite significant opposition from residents and the We Are Mason County coalition. Concerns center on water resources and agricultural land preservation.
objective: Oppose Undisclosed data center in Mason County

summary: Linn County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved one of the most comprehensive local data center zoning ordinances in the country on Feb. 18, 2026. Creates a new data-center-specific zoning district with requirements including comprehensive water study, water-use agreement before construction, 1,000-foot setbacks from residential zones, noise and light pollution limits, road damage compensation, and community betterment fund contributions. County planning director says no ordinance in Iowa asks for more requirements.
objective: Impose 1000-foot data center setbacks in Linn County

summary: Southern Environmental Law Center and Sierra Club filed a lawsuit challenging Georgia EPD's air permit for four new gas-and-oil combustion turbines at Georgia Power's Plant Bowen near Cartersville. The expansion is driven by data center power demand. The groups argue Atlanta already exceeds federal ozone limits and that EPD disregarded nonattainment permitting requirements. The appeal is before the Georgia Office of State Administrative Hearings. If built, the units would emit hundreds of additional tons of smog-forming pollution annually.
objective: Challenge Georgia Power air quality permits in court

summary: Despite growing opposition, Middlesex Township Planning Commission approved Phase 1-A of a proposed $15 billion, 700-acre, 18-building data center complex (Pennsylvania Digital 1/PAX-1) on Country Club Road near Carlisle. Led by Carlisle Development Partners LLC. The plan goes to the Board of Supervisors in March. Would create ~450 jobs and generate $65M in tax revenue. Community concerns center on massive water and power demands in a rural area.
company: Carlisle Development Partners / Powerhouse Data Centers
objective: Rally against Carlisle Development Partners data center in Middlesex Township

summary: Stillwater County residents submitted a formal petition to county commissioners requesting transparency, accountability, and protective action regarding proposed data center development. The petition demands disclosure of tax abatements and public subsidies, protection of community water and electricity resources, direct public engagement before any approvals, and a formal letter to the Montana Public Service Commission requesting ratepayer protections. Part of broader Montana data center opposition—eleven developers are reportedly in talks with NorthWestern Energy statewide.
opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
objective: Petition demanding data center transparency in Stillwater County

summary: Silver Fox 606 LLC (developer Nelson Ferreira, Juno Beach) proposed a ~2 million sq ft data center campus with AI lab, four buildings, and an FPL substation in Indiantown. The site includes 200 acres of wetlands and habitat for protected species. South Florida Wildlands Association has raised environmental concerns. First of three public hearings expected March 2026.
objective: Rally against Silver Fox 606 data center in Martin County

company: Cumulus Real Estate Holdings LLC (Talen Energy subsidiary)
summary: Rezoning request to allow data centers on 460 acres of rural land in Hollenback Township was deemed denied and is now heading to Luzerne County Court. Residents packed meetings to demand answers about data center impacts. Landowners were reportedly offered $175K per acre, causing a divisive community split.
objective: Challenge Cumulus Real Estate Holdings rezoning in Hollenback Township in court

company: Multiple (statewide moratorium targeting hyperscaler data centers)
project_name: NY Data Center Moratorium Bill S9144/A10141
summary: Over 100 New York groups called for passage of the statewide three-year data center moratorium bill, the 'strongest-in-the-nation' proposal that would prevent construction of new data centers while state agencies study environmental and economic impacts. Food & Water Watch coordinated the coalition effort.
objective: Halt data centers in New York after Multiple proposal

opposition_groups: Coalition for Community Centered Data Centers in Southern Maryland
summary: The Charles County Planning Commission voted to recommend denial of Zoning Text Amendment #25-187, which would have established zoning regulations for data centers in the county. Chairman Kevin Wedding cited concerns about higher electric bills for residents. Board member Denard Earl noted that Maryland is conducting a statewide data center impact study expected to conclude in September 2026, suggesting the county wait for those results. Board member Semia Hackett questioned whether approving one facility could open the door to additional projects. The recommendation now moves to the Board of Commissioners for final consideration.
objective: Restrict TeraWulf 250-acre data center in Charles County

summary: Oregon legislators added a provision to Governor Kotek's HB 4084 blocking new data center projects from eligibility for Enterprise Zone property tax breaks until summer 2027. Data centers reaped over $45 million in enterprise zone tax benefits last year and $85 million this year. Sen. Jeff Golden (D-Ashland) pushed the amendment after threatening to propose a full bar on data center and crypto mining tax breaks. Oregon has roughly 125 data centers, mostly in the Portland area and near Columbia River hydroelectric dams. This is a distinct legislative action from the existing Oregon (statewide) entry which covers earlier regulatory discussion. HB 4084 extends the enterprise zone tax break program from 5 to 10 years but was amended to temporarily bar data centers from receiving the expanded tax breaks until summer 2027. Data centers currently account for the majority of enterprise zone tax breaks ($85M in 2026). Kotek supports the amendment. The 2026 short session has ended; bill awaits governor's signature.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via HB 4084

summary: Directs DCED to develop a model zoning ordinance for municipalities to regulate data centers, including standards for noise, water, electricity, and light pollution. Passed House Energy Committee 14-12 along party lines.
bill_name: HB 2151 - Data Center Model Zoning Ordinance
objective: Develop state model data center zoning ordinance (HB 2151)

opposition_groups: No Data Centers Fulton County IN
summary: Fulton County commissioners voted 2-1 to approve a one-year moratorium on data center construction, effective immediately. The vote followed a 6-1 recommendation from the Area Plan Commission on Feb. 28. The moratorium gives the county time to develop comprehensive data center regulations.
objective: Halt data centers in Fulton County after Decennial Group proposal

summary: Lyon Township (Oakland County) board voted unanimously to enact a 180-day moratorium on data centers and all proposed developments 100,000+ sq ft on March 3, 2026. The moratorium followed months of community opposition to 'Project Flex,' a 1.8 million sq ft AI data center by California-based developer Verrus approved by the planning commission in September 2025 on 172 acres zoned industrial. Residents didn't learn about the project until December. The moratorium does not affect Project Flex, only newly proposed data centers.
opposition_groups: NO DATA CENTER in Lyon Township
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Lyon Township (Verrus)

summary: Ypsilanti City Council held a public hearing on March 3 to consider both a 60-day emergency moratorium and a separate 365-day resolution on data center zoning permits. The 60-day measure comes from city administration as a preventive step, while the 365-day resolution was introduced by Ward 3 Council member Amber Fellows. This is a separate action from the earlier Ypsilanti Township petition. Council approved both the 60-day emergency ordinance and the 365-day resolution. This is preemptive -- no active data center proposals in Ypsilanti city proper.
company: University of Michigan / Los Alamos National Laboratory
objective: Impose 60-day data centers moratorium in City of Ypsilanti (University of Michigan)

summary: Residents are challenging the validity of Tobyhanna Township's data center overlay zoning before the Zoning Hearing Board in Monroe County. The hearing is scheduled for March 11, 2026 at 5pm. Part of a broader grassroots resistance movement in Pennsylvania against over 50 proposed data center projects across the state.
opposition_groups: Tobyhanna Township Residents Against Caughbaugh Road Data Center
objective: Legal challenge to Pennsylvania Glacial Till data center in Tobyhanna Township

summary: Royalton voters approved a five-year moratorium on AI and crypto data centers via show-of-hands vote at Town Meeting Day. About 154 of the town's registered voters participated. No data centers have been proposed for the town; the ban is preemptive. The Planning Commission will write the policy into the town plan for Selectboard approval. State Senator Becca White spoke at the meeting about her statewide moratorium bill (S. 205).
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Royalton

summary: Anchorage Assembly held public hearing on Ordinance AO 2026-27 to amend Title 21 of the Municipal Code, defining data centers as a distinct land use and restricting them to conditional approval in industrial, port, airport, and public lands zones. Requires landscaping buffers, setbacks, enclosed power equipment, and detailed impact reviews. Current definitions date to the 1990s. Next public hearing scheduled March 24.
objective: Restrict data centers to industrial zones in Anchorage

summary: Rural communities in North Caddo Parish organized a boycott of the official data center community meeting in Blanchard on March 3. Residents near Caddo Lake oppose a proposed data center campus on State Line Road and Blanchard Latex Road near Mooringsport. State Rep. Danny McCormick has demanded transparency around NDAs signed by local officials. The Village of Mooringsport unanimously approved a resolution on Feb. 12 calling for an environmental study. Caddo Commission declined to vote on a one-year water moratorium proposed by Commissioner Chris Kracman.
objective: Halt data centers in Blanchard after Amazon/AWS proposal

summary: Delta County Planning Commission considered a resolution to suspend data center and cryptocurrency mining projects for 12 months while a zoning ordinance is developed. Commission Chair Amy Berglund said no known data center plans exist for the county but cited concerns over utility demand and water usage. Final decision expected in April 2026.
objective: Impose 12 month data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Delta County

summary: Penn Forest Township supervisors approved a comprehensive data center ordinance at a well-attended meeting at Penn's Peak. The ordinance creates a new zoning overlay district covering 750 acres along Route 903, with requirements including: 30-acre minimum lot, 60 ft height cap plus 15 ft for rooftop equipment, noise and vibration standards, woodland protection (no more than 50% disturbance), Area of Influence water feasibility studies, and a decommissioning cost estimate from an independent engineer. Described as the 'least worst option' by community members who feared that without regulation, developers could exploit existing industrial zoning.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Penn Forest Township

summary: Mercer County Commission voted 3-2 for a one-year moratorium. NextEra subsidiary Boulevard Associates had been acquiring land options on ~530 acres under NDA. About 50 residents attended, most supporting the pause.
objective: Halt data centers in Mercer County after NextEra Energy Resources proposal

summary: Commissioners voted unanimously to initiate a potential 24-month moratorium after residents discovered AWS job postings revealing a secret data center plan in Lusby. A previously undisclosed NDA between commissioners and AWS was uncovered. A pending permit for 'Minut Developers LLC - Data Center' found on MDOE's website.
objective: Impose 24-month data centers moratorium in Calvert County (Amazon)

summary: Urbana City Council voted 6-1 to approve a 12-month moratorium on data centers over 10,000 sq ft, halting a proposed 460,000 sq ft Thor Equities facility off Routes 55 and 68. Over 40 citizens spoke at the meeting. The site consists of three parcels purchased for $5 million. Concerns center on impacts to Cedar Bog, water, electricity, noise, and property values. The only dissenting vote came from the mayor's wife. Urbana city council passed a 12-month moratorium on March 3, 2026 by a 6-1 vote, blocking any new data center over 10,000 sq ft. Thor Equities' proposed $1B Urbana Technology Hub (460,000 sq ft near Ohio 55 and US 68) is now in limbo. Over 4,000 online signatures were collected opposing data centers in Champaign County within two weeks.
opposition_groups: No Data Centers In Urbana, OH
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Urbana (Thor Equities)

summary: Google abandoned unincorporated Linn County entirely and is pursuing annexation into the City of Palo (pop. 1,407) to avoid the county's strict data center ordinance. Google also backed out of a $500K water balance study. The proposed facility would use 12 million gallons/day. County officials called the move 'fundamentally wrong.' Palo has no data center-specific zoning regulations.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in Palo

summary: McDuffie County Planning Commission recommended approval (5-1) of Project Azalea, a proposed data center development including up to six buildings across 200 acres on Randall Hunt Road near a large electrical substation. The facility would require 400 megawatts of energy and represent over $1 billion in investment. Residents raised concerns about lack of transparency, water and environmental impacts, with one resident noting she learned of the proposal through social media after purchasing a home specifically to escape data centers in neighboring Columbia County. A public hearing before the Board of Commissioners is scheduled for March 17, 2026.
objective: Rally against Williams Brothers Development data center in McDuffie County

summary: Requires data centers to be located on land zoned for industrial or manufacturing uses (or unzoned as of July 1, 2025) to qualify for the SB 98 sales tax exemption.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via SB 98

summary: During a rare winter storm on January 22, 2025, Marion County Council approved a $2.4B data center under the code name 'Project Liberty' while most residents were snowed in and unaware. The project is by Stream Data Centers (operating as Eagle Myra LLC) in the county's industrial park on US Highway 501 Bypass, with up to six buildings in two phases. Stream said the facility would create only 20 permanent jobs. Marion County Council passed all three readings of the ordinance under a nondisclosure agreement, preventing officials from making details public. Residents packed a subsequent council meeting demanding transparency. In March 2026, South Carolina lawmakers submitted a resolution to temporarily halt data center projects statewide. Opposition continues with a Change.org petition opposing the ordinance.
objective: Petition to block Stream Data Centers data center in Marion County

summary: Louisville Planning Commission voted 8-1 to approve Kentucky's first hyperscale data center near Shively, despite 20+ residents opposing. The facility will require 400 MW at full capacity — about 68% of all LG&E customers' combined usage.
objective: Oppose Poe Companies data center in Louisville

summary: Repeals state sales and use tax exemption for new data centers while grandfathering existing projects. Locks into law that data centers must cover full cost of new generation and transmission infrastructure. Passed Senate 32-21 on Crossover Day (March 6, 2026). Phases out tax breaks: new DCs can't receive exemptions, existing companies keep them until 2028/2031. Also incorporates original SB 34 language requiring data centers to pay for grid infrastructure. Now goes to the House.
bill_name: SB 410 - Data Center Tax Exemption Repeal
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via SB 34

summary: Champaign County proposed a 12-month moratorium on data centers with 10,000+ sq ft processing area in unincorporated areas. ZBA vote scheduled March 26, 2026, County Board vote expected April. Primary concerns: water usage from the Mahomet Aquifer (serving ~1 million people) and energy usage. County Board unanimously created a data center task force. ZBA public hearing held March 11; ZBA vote scheduled March 26, County Board vote expected April. County Board unanimously created a data center task force to develop comprehensive regulations. A petition with 4,000+ online signatures opposes data centers in the county.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Champaign County

summary: Board of Supervisors voted to deny a massive 2,200-acre datacenter campus proposal that would have included 84 datacenter buildings and a 3,500 MW natural gas power plant. Strong community opposition cited environmental and quality-of-life concerns.
opposition_groups: Save Our Farmlands, No Power Plant No Data Center
objective: Deny Uranium (proposed), various 3500MW data center permit in Pittsylvania County

summary: Amazon Web Services withdrew a conditional use permit application for a 7.2 million-square-foot data center campus on 1,370 acres in Louisa County's Mineral area (south of Davis Highway/Route 22, north of Jefferson Highway/Route 33, surrounding Northeast Creek). At a town hall meeting in late June 2025, community members voiced concerns about noise, traffic, water use, and energy bills, with some passing out stickers reading 'Don't Loudoun my Louisa.' Amazon withdrew in a letter to county leaders on July 23, 2025, citing community feedback. The county has three other data centers in development, none of which required the conditional use permitting process that allows community input.
opposition_groups: Louisa County residents (Don't Loudoun my Louisa)
objective: Require use permit for Amazon data center in Louisa County

summary: Spotsylvania County has approved data centers and is trying to implement regulations. In December 2025, the board voted 4-3 to approve design standards requiring special-use permits for data centers in commercial, agricultural, and industrial districts. Vice Chair Lori Hayes, Jacob Lane, and Chair Chris Yakabouski dissented, preferring a stronger SUP (special use permit) requirement. Yakabouski noted Loudoun, Prince William, and Henrico have all moved to require SUPs. AWS has committed $35 billion in Virginia by 2040, including $6 billion for a data center spanning Spotsylvania, Caroline, and Stafford counties. The board signaled it may strengthen requirements further in 2026.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Spotsylvania County

summary: GigaLand withdrew its original datacenter application in Fauquier County after significant community opposition, but resubmitted a scaled-down proposal in early 2026 — reduced by 55% to 998,000 sq ft and 600 MW. The Board of Supervisors remains skeptical of the revised plan. Residents continue to organize against the project, citing concerns about rural character and environmental impacts.
objective: Deny Gigaland 600MW data center permit in Fauquier County

summary: A second datacenter proposal near Catlett Station in Fauquier County was withdrawn after community pushback. Part of a wave of datacenter opposition across northern Virginia's rural counties.
objective: Deny Headwaters Site Development data center on 67 acres in Fauquier County

summary: After a six-hour meeting, Goochland's Board of Supervisors voted 4-1 to approve a technology overlay district allowing data centers in the eastern part of the county. More than 80 residents from Goochland and neighboring counties attended, most in opposition. Four property owners have now filed a lawsuit challenging the TOD approval, and the county approved $250,000 for outside legal counsel to defend the case in Circuit Court.
objective: Legal challenge to QTS Realty data center in Goochland County

summary: In June 2025, the Frederick County Board of Supervisors voted by consensus against moving forward with applications for two large data centers: the proposed 644-acre Meadow Brook Technology Park and 105-acre Winchester Gateway 2. Over 100 people attended the meeting, most voicing fears over data centers ruining the county's rural landscape. Most supervisors felt too many unanswered questions surrounded the projects' potential effects. The dual rejection was part of a broader wave of Virginia data center cancellations — at least 25 projects were canceled in 2025, nearly four times as many as 2024.
objective: Deny Winchester Gateway data center on 749 acres in Frederick County

summary: Residents near STACK Infrastructure's datacenter in Culpeper organized opposition over persistent noise from cooling systems. The fight led to noise ordinance discussions and demands for better sound mitigation.
objective: Organize coalition against STACK Infrastructure data center in Town of Culpeper

summary: Mecklenburg County Board of Supervisors enacted a moratorium on datacenter development to study the impacts of proposed large-scale facilities near the Virginia-North Carolina border.
objective: Pause data centers in Mecklenburg County pending regulations

summary: Orange County adopted new zoning regulations restricting datacenter development, including setback requirements and noise limits, following community concerns about proposals in the rural county.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Orange County

summary: Fluvanna County enacted a moratorium on datacenter development while studying zoning changes to address the growing wave of datacenter proposals spreading from Louisa and Albemarle counties.
objective: Pause data centers in Fluvanna County pending regulations

summary: Black Mountain Power assembled 450+ acres for a $10B data center campus between Forest Hill and Everman. Rezoning approved in phases despite opposition from neighbors and Weston Gardens botanical garden. An 80-acre expansion vote was delayed twice — by council on Feb 10, and by the developer on March 11. A private neighborhood meeting was held March 12. Next votes expected April 8/May 12, 2026.
objective: Rally against Black Mountain Power data center in Fort Worth

summary: Skybox Datacenters' request to rezone ~30 acres off East Old Settlers Boulevard for a single 75-MW, ~250,000 sq ft data center was approved unanimously by Round Rock City Council on February 12, 2026, over strong community resistance. Around 25 citizens spoke at the Dec. 4, 2025 public hearing, 200+ emailed the council, and over 3,000 signed an online petition opposing the project. The 'Protect Round Rock' organization mobilized opposition citing concerns about particulate matter/air pollution from generators, noise (audible and low-frequency), generator testing frequency, invasive site lighting, and potential access from residential Chandler View Trail. Public Citizen called the approval another example of 'community ignored' in Round Rock, which now has nine approved data centers.
objective: Block Skybox Datacenters rezoning of 30 acres in Round Rock

summary: A billion-dollar CyrusOne data center under construction below the Lake Whitney dam has drawn scrutiny from area residents. The 'Save Lake Whitney' campaign has gathered approximately 6,000 petition signatures claiming the lake's level could drop due to water consumption. The opposition maintains a website at savelakewhitney.com. CyrusOne has filed for 2 additional buildings at the site.
objective: Petition to block CyrusOne data center in Bosque County

summary: Residents of Mitchell Bend outside Granbury have been fighting Marathon Digital Holdings' (MARA) bitcoin mine since 2022, alleging 'permanent hearing loss, lack of sleep, headaches, tinnitus, and vertigo' from 24/7 noise. Earthjustice represents plaintiffs in a nuisance lawsuit; a court denied MARA's motion to dismiss and plaintiffs filed a motion to compel discovery. Residents attempted to incorporate Mitchell Bend as a city to gain municipal regulatory authority over the mine, drawing national attention. MARA counter-sued to block the incorporation vote. A judge allowed the vote to proceed but voters ultimately rejected incorporation. A third power plant near the mine was under judicial review in September 2025. MARA's counter-lawsuit alleged officials kept incorporation secret.
objective: Challenge TIF financing for MARA Holdings data center

summary: Riot Platforms operates a 400-MW bitcoin mining facility in Corsicana (Phase 1 of a planned 1 GW campus) using immersion-cooling technology. Riot now owns 858 acres after 2025 acquisitions. The facility needs ~1.5 million gallons of water/day — roughly an eighth of Corsicana's daily maximum. Riot is drilling wells into the Woodbine Aquifer. An electrician filed complaints to the SEC, Texas AG, and ERCOT alleging Riot is misclassifying its facility as a data center for tax exemptions and misusing public funds. A protest was held at the Navarro County facility. Riot is evaluating converting ~600 MW of remaining capacity to AI/HPC uses. Noise from cooling fans can reach 95 decibels (like standing next to a running motorcycle).
objective: Rally against Riot Platforms data center in Corsicana

summary: Sailfish proposed Comanche Circle: 2,600-acre, 5GW data center park near Comanche Peak nuclear plant. 'Protect the Paluxy Valley Inc.' opposes, citing threats to aquifer recharge zone and Dinosaur Valley State Park watershed. Commissioners gave conditional approval Jan 13, 2026.
objective: Oppose Sailfish Digital Ventures data center over water in Hood County

summary: Trustees unanimously passed 12-month moratorium on data centers and small modular nuclear reactors. Second Adams County township to do so. Near former Killen power plant.
objective: Impose 12-month moratorium on data centers in Adams County

summary: Home of the former Stuart power plant, passed one-year voluntary moratorium on data centers. Area targeted for massive data center requiring 1,300 MW — 31x the county's current usage.
objective: Halt data centers in Sprigg Township after 68 Yards proposal

summary: Trustees enacted 12-month moratorium while overhauling zoning code that has no data center language. Rejected shorter 6-month proposal.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Scioto Township (rejected)

summary: City Council enacted interim development control overlay for data centers. Planning Commission voted unanimously in March to extend 9 months through Feb 2027. Data centers not currently defined in Cincinnati's zoning code.
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
objective: Create data center overlay zone in Cincinnati

summary: Opposition organizing against data center with 115 diesel-fired generators and 36 cooling towers, emitting 236 tons/year of nitrogen oxides. Ohio EPA public hearing set. Buckeye Environmental Network leading opposition. Google was revealed as the company behind Project BOSC on March 17, 2026. $500M investment including $50M for local infrastructure. 200+ acres in American and Sugar Creek townships. Standing-room-only EPA hearing on March 11. Google's third Ohio data center, joining $7.2B total state investment since 2019.
objective: Rally against Google (via Bistrozzi) data center in Lima / Allen County

summary: Dexter Township adopted a 180-day moratorium on data center applications via Resolution #26-705, joining at least 19 Michigan communities that have paused data center development. Residents and officials raised concerns about massive water and energy demands, potential cost increases for existing ratepayers, and industrialization of rural landscapes. The moratorium gives the township time to study impacts and draft appropriate zoning regulations.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Dexter Township

summary: Planning commission approved one-year moratorium on data center applications while developing regulations for large-scale energy projects.
objective: Pause data centers in Zeeland Township pending regulations

summary: Planning commission voted 5-2 to recommend rezoning for a 24MW data center with 16MW fuel cell plant, reversing December rejection. City council hearings scheduled March-April 2026.
objective: Block Deep Green rezoning of 2.7 acres in Lansing

summary: Residents opposed conversion of former R.R. Donnelly printing plants into data centers. City drafted zoning ordinance requiring special exception hearings, noise standards, and energy/water reporting. Community benefits agreement negotiated with $20M in contributions.
objective: Oppose CoreWeave data center in Lancaster City

summary: Board unanimously rejected 1,280-acre, 15-building 'Project Hazelnut' data center campus after 200+ residents packed the meeting. Would use electricity for 90,000 homes. Developer still pursuing state DEP permits despite local rejection.
objective: Deny NorthPoint Development data center on 1280 acres in Hazle Township

summary: 'Save Frackville' residents presented 81 letters opposing five-building data center on conservation-zoned forest. Supervisors voted 3-0 to invalidate zoning code portion, delaying project 6 months for rewrite.
objective: Restrict KRNL Data Centers data center via zoning in New Castle Township

summary: Township adopted temporary moratorium on data center applications. When township also passed restricting zoning amendments, developer filed exclusionary zoning challenge over 155-acre parcel.
objective: Halt data centers in Dorrance Township after Brewster Land Company proposal

summary: Developer filed exclusionary zoning challenge for 1,000-acre, 34-building campus ($14.25B). Settlement vacated by judge Jan 2026 for excluding zoning hearing board. Legal battle continues.
objective: Legal challenge to 1778 Rich Pike / Quantm Group data center in Clifton Township

summary: County proposed zoning amendment covering 20 municipalities. Defines three data center tiers, requires special exception approval, mandates 500-foot setbacks from residential/agricultural zones.
objective: Impose 500-foot data center setbacks in Luzerne County

summary: Supervisors paused data center zoning revisions after massive public opposition. So many residents turned out crowd spilled into parking lot, forcing postponement. Golf course property removed from consideration after pushback.
objective: Pause data center zoning after public opposition in East Manchester

summary: Requires data centers to submit annual energy and water consumption reports to DEP, including measures undertaken to improve efficiency. Passed House Energy Committee 14-12 on party-line vote.
bill_name: HB 2150 - Data Center Energy and Water Reporting Act
objective: Require data center energy and water reports in PA

summary: Council voted down redevelopment plan after raucous residents raised questions about data center component. Standing-room-only meeting featured Twin Rivers community residents warning of grid strain.
objective: Deny data center permit in East Windsor

summary: Requires data center operators to submit semi-annual water and energy usage reports to the Board of Public Utilities for three years. Senate Environment and Energy Committee reported favorably (3-1-1).
bill_name: S3379 - Data Center Water and Energy Reporting
objective: Require semi-annual data center water and energy reports (S3379)

summary: After County Council voted 5-2 to adopt 2,615-acre 'Critical Digital Infrastructure' overlay zone, residents formed referendum committee. Board of Elections approved petition Jan 26; two-thirds of ~15,000 signatures gathered by early March. Backed by 13+ nonprofits including Farm Bureau. The DCRC submitted 22,169 petition signatures to the Board of Elections by the March 20 deadline, exceeding the ~15,000 required. Signatures must now be validated.
opposition_groups: Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee
objective: Put data center decision to public vote in Frederick County

summary: Senate President Bill Ferguson introduced legislation banning data center construction on land benefiting from tax increment financing (TIF) in Baltimore City, targeting Baltimore Peninsula. Also requires PSC oversight of transmission line projects. BGE subsequently paused its $537M South Baltimore transmission upgrade.
objective: Regulate data center development in Baltimore City

summary: City Council voted 5-1 to approve 12M sq ft, 21-building data center on 1,632 acres despite overwhelming public opposition. Planning & Zoning split 3-3. Projected $224M annual tax revenue. Water usage 1.5M gal/day. Issue defined a GA Senate special election.
objective: Voters reject officials who approved data center in Forsyth

summary: Atlas sought to rezone 588 acres from agricultural for 6-building data center. 'Stop the Data Center' campaign gathered 700+ petition signatures in first week. Planning Commission recommended denial. Atlas withdrew before final vote.
opposition_groups: Stop the Data Center in Bartow County
objective: Block Atlas Development data center through opposition in Adairsville area

summary: $6B, 500MW data center on 486-acre former WWII/Manhattan Project industrial park on Coosa River. Council unanimously approved zoning modifications. Mayor signed confidentiality agreement. Site has contaminated groundwater.
objective: Oppose WHP Development data center over water in Childersburg

summary: $10B hyperscale campus named '2025 Worst Economic Development Deal of the Year.' 66% property tax break for 30 years plus state tax exemptions. 500MW at full capacity. Mississippi Power delayed coal plant closure to fuel campus.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Compass Datacenters in Meridian

summary: DC BLOX building subsea cable landing station and data center in Palm Coast Town Center, connecting up to 8 undersea cables for Google. Project cloaked in secrecy. Flagler Beach initially rebuffed easement request; eventually agreed to $200K per cable.
objective: Oppose DC BLOX data center in Palm Coast /

summary: Establishes restrictive regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers (25+ MW). Bans data centers from agricultural zoning districts, requires 500-foot setbacks, bans economic incentives, imposes water permitting restrictions. Session ended without committee action.
objective: Ban data centers from agricultural zones with 500-foot setbacks

summary: Would repeal Arizona's data center sales and use tax exemption program, which cost the state ~$38.5M in fiscal year 2025. Companies currently receive exemptions for spending $50M+ (Maricopa/Pima) or $25M+ (other counties) on data center construction.
bill_name: SB 1463 - Data Center Tax Incentive Repeal
objective: Repeal Arizona data center tax exemption program via SB 1463

summary: Creates the Tennessee Data Center Impact Review Act establishing formal review process for data center impacts on communities, environment, and infrastructure. Deferred in Senate Commerce and Labor Committee to March 17, 2026.
bill_name: SB 2653 - Data Center Impact Review Act
objective: Create Tennessee Data Center Impact Review Act (SB 2653)

summary: Republican bill directing PSC to ensure data center utility costs are not passed to other ratepayers. Requires renewable energy facilities serving data center load to be on data center property. Requires hiring Wisconsin workers. Passed Assembly 53-44.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in WI

summary: Democratic bill creating new 'very large customer' electric rate class for data centers. To qualify for tax exemption, data centers must derive at least 70% of energy from renewables and pay prevailing wages. Establishes water usage reporting requirements.
objective: Tie data center tax breaks to prevailing wage requirements

summary: Omnibus DLGF bill with data center provision requiring companies receiving future sales tax exemptions to pay up to 1% of electricity bill savings each quarter to local governments. Passed House 91-3, Senate 48-0. Signed by Gov. Braun on March 12, 2026.
objective: Require data centers to pay 1% of tax savings locally

summary: Creates 13-member Data Center Study Commission to examine environmental impacts, water use, grid reliability, noise/light pollution, farmland preservation, and local economic effects. Commission must report findings within 6 months. Passed House Technology & Innovation Committee 9-3. Committee voted 9-3 to advance HB 646; expected House floor vote before end of March.
objective: Mandate data center water disclosure via HB 646

summary: The City of Sulphur Springs paired with developer MSB Global to build what could be one of the largest AI data centers in the country: 30 buildings on 5,000 acres that Luminant had gifted to the city. A temporary injunction was issued February 10, 2026, blocking power system construction. The city manager was subpoenaed. The judge dismissed fraud claims but denied the city's motion on counterclaims. The Texas AG sided with the city in the dispute.
objective: Sue over concealment in MSB Global approval process

summary: Planning and Zoning Commission voted 4-3 to reject 936-acre hyperscale AI data center zoning change. ~10 residents opposed converting Low Density Single Family area to General Industrial. Developer withdrew and plans to revise.
objective: Deny Texas Solar Works data center on 936 acres in Lubbock

summary: San Antonio City Council directed staff to review zoning regulations for data centers after learning that 59 future data center projects are in various stages of planning, with CPS Energy projecting energy needs of approximately 26,000 megawatts. Current regulations allow data centers in almost every zoning category except residential. A pair of data centers already used 463 million gallons of water in 2023-2024 while residents face Stage 3 watering restrictions. Mayor Gina Ortiz Jones expressed support for an expedited review of possible amendments to the Unified Development Code addressing noise, impervious cover, and sustainability standards, rather than waiting for the regular five-year amendment cycle in January 2027.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in San Antonio

summary: Misae Investments' Mojo Ranch project proposes a renewable energy and data infrastructure hub on 2,742 acres near Stockdale. Trees have already been bulldozed on the site. 'Protect Wilson County Water' has formed as an opposition group, collecting 1,385 petition signatures in just 5 days. The developer's water usage claims changed from 2 million gallons/day to 15,000 gallons/day, raising credibility concerns among residents.
objective: Petition to block Misae Investments data center in Wilson County

summary: EdgeConneX is building a $440 million first-phase data center on a campus at the northeast corner of FM 535 and Wolf Lane in Cedar Creek, Bastrop County. The full project will span 2.8 million sq ft across four buildings over 5-8 years. The first building is scheduled for completion in June 2026. Bastrop County Commissioners Court awarded a 10-year property tax abatement in December 2024. The project would generate ~$100 million for Bastrop ISD and ~$7 million for the county during the abatement period. Despite its massive size, it will create only about 60 permanent jobs ($5.8M annual payroll). Residents raised concerns about noise, traffic on FM 535 (an 'already very busy and dangerous road'), and disruption to rural quality of life. EdgeConneX also acquired an adjacent 180-acre parcel to expand the campus.
objective: Oppose EdgeConneX data center over noise in Bastrop County

summary: Township Board voted unanimously for 6-month moratorium on data center permits. Preemptive action — no proposal pending. Created subcommittee to study zoning amendments.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Cascade Township

summary: Microsoft seeking to rezone 356 acres (purchased from Steelcase for $45.3M) for data center. Dec 2025 hearing canceled when 250+ overflowed venue. Microsoft terminated NDAs with Gaines and 3 other West MI communities in March 2026. Planning Commission hearing expected April.
objective: Block Microsoft rezoning of 356 acres in Gaines Charter

summary: Establishes regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers. Requires PSC to ensure data centers pay their own utility costs. Prohibits certain NDAs. Preserves local government land use authority. Passed both chambers March 13, 2026 in weakened form; signed by Governor DeSantis, effective July 1, 2026. Major provisions include separate utility rate class and transparency requirements. Governor DeSantis signed SB 484 on March 24. Effective July 1, 2026.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SB 484

summary: Board of Commissioners unanimously enacted 30-day moratorium on data center applications. Existing ordinance limits DCs to Stanton Springs overlay, but development creeping into incorporated areas. Moratorium extended through April 8, 2026 (originally 30 days). Staff reviewing development ordinances. Data centers have crept into incorporated areas like Covington, Social Circle, and Porterdale through city annexation.
objective: Impose 30-day data centers moratorium in Newton County

summary: Monroe County commissioners passed moratorium on new data center applications while developing zoning restrictions. Set to be lifted at first March 2026 commission meeting.
objective: Pause data centers in Monroe County pending regulations

summary: MDEQ Permit Board granted air permit for 41 gas turbines powering Colossus 2 despite 300+ opponents at the Feb hearing and zero public support. NAACP protested the hearing being scheduled on Election Day with only 5 days notice. 27 turbines already operating without permits. The permit was approved March 10-11, 2026. The NAACP has called the facility a civil rights issue given its proximity to a majority-Black community.
objective: Rally against xAI / MZX Tech data center in Southaven

summary: Amazon's $10B data center complex (5 buildings operational, 7 under construction on 1,000+ acres) generating neighborhood opposition. Idlebriar residents report dust causing asthma in children, construction traffic. MDEQ permits show 240 tons/yr NOx (9x Nissan plant) from 700+ diesel generators.
objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center in Canton / Idlebriar

summary: LPSC refused to investigate Meta's sale of 80% of its $27B Richland Parish data center to Blue Owl Capital. Meta retains only 20% stake with a 4-year lease. Earthjustice/UCS warned ratepayers could be stuck paying for 3 new Entergy gas plants if Meta exits.
objective: Investigate Meta sale of data center stake to protect ratepayers

summary: TigerDC formally withdrew $3B Project Spero after council denied tax incentives amid sustained opposition. 'No Data Centers in Spartanburg' Facebook group gained ~3,000 members; petition reached ~4,000 signatures. Concerns included brownouts, water consumption, environmental impacts.
objective: 4000-signature petition opposing TigerDC data center

summary: Hut 8's $10B River Bend AI data center campus received zoning approval. Phase I: 245 MW facility, operations expected Q2 2027. Near decommissioned River Bend Nuclear Station. 75 direct + 193 indirect jobs.
objective: Oppose Hut 8 data center in West Feliciana

summary: After council secretly approved $2.4B Project Liberty during a rare winter storm under NDA, residents packed subsequent meetings demanding transparency. Project listed on agenda only as 'Project Liberty' with no details.
objective: Mobilize opposition to Stream Data Centers data center in Marion County

summary: Requires EPA to collect and report quarterly on data center water consumption, water reuse, pollutants, and impacts on local water systems. Requires EIA to collect data every 6 months on total energy consumed by each US data center.
objective: Require data center energy and water reports in DC

summary: Moves the sunset date for the high-technology data center equipment sales and use tax exemption from December 31, 2031 to December 31, 2026, effectively ending the exemption program. The exemption has cost the state an estimated $778M/year.
bill_name: HB 559 - Data Center Equipment Tax Exemption Sunset
objective: Accelerate Georgia data center tax exemption sunset to 2026

summary: Moves sunset date for data center sales tax exemption from January 1, 2032 to January 1, 2027. Functionally identical in intent to HB 559.
bill_name: SB 408 - Data Center Tax Exemption Sunset
objective: Accelerate data center tax exemption sunset (HB 559)

summary: Revises data center sales tax and property tax exemptions. Requires qualifying businesses to receive electricity under rate schedules that fully recover all utility costs. Mandates written notice to local water providers and Department of Water Resources detailing anticipated water consumption needs.
bill_name: H 820 - Data Center Tax Exemption Reform
objective: Create data center property tax exemptions in ID

summary: Addresses state tax incentives for data centers created by House Bill 521 in 2020. Part of legislative efforts to rein in data center tax exemptions and curb impact on power and water resources.
objective: Create data center tax incentive program in ID

summary: Requires data center developers to disclose projected energy, water, carbon emissions, noise, and environmental impacts at application stage. Imposes penalties for noncompliance. Session ended without committee hearing.
bill_name: HB 1517 - Florida Data Center Transparency Act
objective: Require data center energy and water reports in FL

summary: Bipartisan effort to override Gov. DeWine's line-item veto of the data center sales tax exemption repeal in budget bill HB 96. Current exemptions cost $140M+/year in lost revenue. Override requires 60 House + 20 Senate votes. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta oppose override.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions via HB 96

summary: Reintroduced after Murphy pocket veto. Requires utilities to develop tariff for data centers using 100+ MW monthly. New large load customers must post financial guarantees for 85% of requested service for 10+ years. Reported from Senate Committee with amendments.
bill_name: S731 / A796 - Large Load Data Center Tariff
objective: Reintroduce large-load data center tariff after Murphy veto (S731)

summary: First award under NJ's Next New Jersey AI program: $250M tax break ($50M/yr x 5 years) for $1.8B, 250MW facility at former Merck campus. Critics note program lacks community benefits agreement requirements of older NJ incentive programs.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for CoreWeave in Kenilworth

summary: Requires large-scale data center operators to report environmental and resource impacts publicly. Mandates jurisdictions with 10,000+ residents create formal data center management plans by 2027. Includes $1,000/MW payment into EmPOWER program for low-income assistance.
bill_name: HB 1411 - Data Center Planning and Transparency Act
objective: Mandate public environmental reporting from data centers (HB 1411)

summary: Authorizes counties to establish a special personal property tax subclass for data centers and set a rate up to 2.5x the real property tax rate. Emerged from Frederick County's push for local taxing authority over data center equipment.
bill_name: HB 1595 / SB 427 - Data Center Property Tax Subclass
objective: Reform data center property tax rules in MD

summary: House Minority Whip Jesse Pippy (R) proposed a county-specific moratorium on new data centers until the statewide impact study (due Sept. 1, 2026) is completed. Separate from the county council's unanimous Feb. 2 vote to pause permitting and the citizen referendum drive (15,000+ signatures).
bill_name: Pippy Frederick County Data Center Moratorium
objective: Pause data center permits for statewide study in MD

summary: Voluntary executive program offering expedited state permitting for data center developers who commit to bringing/paying for their own power generation, transparency and community engagement, hiring local workers, and environmental protection including water conservation. Critics note standards are voluntary with no enforcement.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Pennsylvania
