summary: City of Industry's City Council approved zoning amendments on December 17, 2025 allowing data centers citywide. Plans submitted in January 2026 outline converting the mostly vacant Puente Hills Mall into an industrial complex with a potential 10 MW data center. Other proposed sites include Alfred S. Madrid Middle School and IBC Building 10. The Bassett Unified School District unanimously voted on January 27, 2026 to oppose the zoning changes. LA County residents expressed alarm about data centers being proposed without community engagement.
objective: Oppose EdgeCore data center in City of Industry

summary: Oakley City Council approved the 164-acre Bridgehead Industrial Project on March 11, 2026 in a 4-1 vote, but with an amendment excluding data centers after sustained community opposition. Residents raised concerns about water and electricity demands at public hearings and Planning Commission meetings. On February 9, 2026, the developer (Oxfoot Oakley) agreed to remove data centers from the proposal. The project was approved as a logistics/industrial hub without data center uses.
objective: Rally against data center in City of Oakley

summary: On March 12, 2026, the City Council postponed action on data center moratorium evaluation after a verbal altercation during public comment led police to evacuate the chamber. Debate rescheduled to March 18, 2026. City Council unanimously passed a 45-day moratorium on March 19, 2026.
objective: Impose 45-day data centers moratorium in City of El Monte

bill_name: SB 978 - Data Center Community Accountability Act
summary: Bans backup diesel generators at data centers, prevents data centers from placing electricity costs onto ratepayers, directs PUC to establish a special rate structure for large-scale energy users (75+ MW), ensures hyperscale data center construction uses local skilled workforce and pays prevailing wages. Introduced by Sen. Sasha Renée Pérez (D-Pasadena).
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in CA

summary: CalEthos, a Tustin-based startup, is planning a hyperscale data center campus in Imperial County along Highway 111 between Niland and Calipatria, while a separate project by Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing (IVCM) proposes a 330MW, 950,000-square-foot facility nearby. The city of Imperial filed a petition for writ of mandate in December 2025 to force environmental review, and a grassroots group called NIMBY Imperial has gathered thousands of opposing signatures with roughly 50 volunteers. Community concerns center on the facility's potential consumption of 750,000 gallons of water per day, its 330MW energy demand representing nearly 30% of the Imperial Irrigation District's available supply, and the developers' attempts to bypass CEQA environmental review.
objective: Petition to block CalEthos data center in Imperial Valley

summary: Amazon's Boardman/Morrow County data centers alleged to have worsened a nitrate water contamination crisis. Amazon uses polluted groundwater for cooling; wastewater sprayed on farmland infiltrates the aquifer. Wastewater nitrate levels averaged 56 ppm — 8x Oregon's safety limit. Of 70 wells tested, 68 violated federal limits. Rolling Stone investigation linked data centers to cancer clusters. Class-action RCRA lawsuit filed. Amazon fined $10,000 by DEQ in Oct 2025.
objective: Challenge Amazon/AWS water withdrawal in court

bill_name: Gov. Kotek Data Center Advisory Committee
summary: Gov. Tina Kotek created a seven-member Data Center Advisory Committee to develop policy recommendations for data center expansion aligned with the state's 'Prosperity Roadmap.' Committee chaired by Margaret Hoffmann and Michael Jung, with policy recommendations due to Legislature by October 2026.
objective: Oppose data center development in Oregon

bill_name: S. 3682 - Power for the People Act
summary: Directs FERC to mandate grid operators create specific queues for data center electricity connection requests, encourages states to establish rate classes specific to data centers, and requires data center operators to cover the full cost of generation, transmission, and distribution upgrades. Includes prevailing wage and registered apprenticeship requirements.
objective: Create dedicated FERC interconnection queues for data centers

bill_name: Executive Order No. 2 - Utility Costs Emergency
summary: Gov. Mikie Sherrill signed on her first day in office, declaring a state of emergency on utility costs. Directs BPU to require electric utilities to report on energy requests from data centers ('ghost loads'). Identifies data center demand surge as key driver of NJ's energy crisis.
objective: Require data centers to report energy consumption in NJ

bill_name: PUCT Data Center Water Use Survey
summary: Public Utility Commission of Texas conducting a survey of water use by data centers and virtual currency mining facilities. Authorized by state budget rider from Rep. Armando Walle. Survey asks about direct water use, cooling technology, and indirect water use. Results shared with Texas Water Development Board and TCEQ, due to legislature by end of 2026. Texas has 464 data centers with 70+ under development.
objective: Study data center impacts on water resources in TX

summary: Earthjustice and seven community/conservation organizations challenged a Montana PSC protective order shielding NorthWestern Energy's letters of intent to provide electricity service to data centers from public view. Groups argue the order unconstitutionally shifted the burden of proof to the public and exceeded PSC's legal authority. PSC granted the order in December after NorthWestern filed in September.
objective: Challenge protective order shielding NorthWestern data center deals

summary: Harlingen city officials are drafting ordinances to regulate data center development in response to Eneus Energy's proposed 1,785-acre data center on Cameron County land near Valley International Airport. Two parallel approaches are being developed: Mayor Norma Sepulveda and City Attorney Mark Sossi are drafting zoning-based regulations, while Commissioner Daniel Lopez's proposed ordinance focuses on natural resource preservation. Concerns were heightened after the Harlingen WaterWorks System board entered into an agreement to potentially supply the data center with up to 4.6 million gallons of sewer water per day.
objective: Restrict Eneus Energy 1785-acre data center in Harlingen

summary: UPDATE: New Castle County Council voted 12-0 on March 11 to approve comprehensive data center regulations after 7 months of deliberation. Key provisions: 1,000-foot buffer from residential zones (500 ft with noise study), ban on open-loop cooling (unless using reclaimed water), mandatory use of energy-efficient generators, site restoration requirement. Regulations will NOT apply retroactively to projects already in the pipeline. Separately, Starwood Digital Ventures' appeal of its Coastal Zone permit denial is scheduled for hearing March 24, 2026.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in New Castle County

summary: Requires businesses using 30+ MW of electricity to obtain a certificate to operate from the Delaware Public Service Commission, which may mandate grid modernization payments or additional energy generation commitments. Stalled in Senate Energy Committee amid union and Republican opposition.
objective: Require PSC operating certificate for 30MW+ facilities in Delaware

summary: QTS Data Centers is undertaking a multi-phase expansion of its 194-acre East Windsor campus on Princeton Hightstown Road, formerly occupied by McGraw-Hill, with plans for 554,000 square feet of modernized facilities. Residents from the Twin Rivers community packed Planning Board hearings to challenge the expansion, raising concerns about water consumption, energy grid strain, and environmental risks from the facility's growing footprint.
objective: Oppose QTS Data Centers data center over water in East Windsor

summary: Nonbinding presidential proclamation announced during 2026 State of the Union. Seven tech companies (Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, xAI) pledged to build/buy their own power generation, pay for all grid infrastructure upgrades, negotiate separate rate structures, and invest in local communities.
objective: Regulate data center development in United States

bill_name: H.R. 6529 - Protecting Families from AI Data Center Energy Costs Act
summary: Directs FERC to convene a technical conference within 90 days on how large electricity loads — especially AI data centers — affect residential and small commercial customers' bills. FERC must submit a report with recommendations to Congressional energy committees within 180 days.
objective: Direct FERC technical conference on data center rate impacts

opposition_groups: Western New York Environmental Alliance (ReThink STAMP)
summary: The Genesee County Industrial Development Agency proposed $801 million in sales and mortgage tax breaks for Stream Data Centers to build at the STAMP industrial park, working out to $6.4 million per job for the 125 positions promised. Investigative reporting revealed a potential conflict of interest, as the IDA leading the environmental review could earn $76-126 million in fees from the deal. The Tonawanda Seneca Nation, whose territory abuts STAMP, opposes the project over concerns that 24/7 noise would harm their ability to hunt wildlife in the Big Woods.
objective: Oppose STREAM Data Centers data center over noise in Genesee County

summary: The Seneca County Board of Supervisors voted unanimously in March 2026 to join Tompkins County in asking the New York DEC to reject TeraWulf's modified water permit for its proposed 400MW AI data center at a former coal plant site on Cayuga Lake in Lansing. TeraWulf seeks to withdraw over one million gallons of water per day for cooling, with returned water at elevated temperatures that critics say would encourage harmful algal blooms threatening drinking water and tourism. Local groups have also filed a lawsuit against TeraWulf and the Lansing zoning board.
objective: Challenge TeraWulf for treaty rights violations

summary: UPDATE: After Natelli Investments withdrew the New Hill Digital Campus application on March 5, Mayor Pro Tem Terry Mahaffey proposed a one-year moratorium on March 10. Town council voted unanimously to move forward. Mayor Jacques Gilbert also proposed a non-standing committee to research data center impacts.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Town of Apex

summary: Developer Tract proposed the Mountain Road Tech Park — a 427-acre, 900-MW data center campus along Route 33 and Mountain Road near the Hanover-Henrico line. The project would include five buildings, requiring up to 600,000 gallons/day of water. The Planning Commission unanimously deferred the proposal. It is being reconsidered at the March 19, 2026 meeting.
objective: Rally against Tract data center in Hanover County

summary: Red Post Energy Group signed a letter of intent with Wise Innovation Hub Venture (OASIS) to develop the Maverick Project — a phased natural gas power plant for a data center complex adjacent to Lonesome Pine Regional Industrial Facility. The project comprises nine buildings totaling 2 million sq ft with 600 MW capacity and an on-site gas power plant. Appalachian Voices opposes the project. The complex would create 500-700 construction jobs and 30-50 operations jobs per building.
objective: Oppose Red Post Energy Group data center in Wise County

summary: The Moore County Metro Council is considering a two-year moratorium on data center construction and expansion, which would take effect April 20, 2026. During the moratorium, the county would stop accepting new applications for special use permits, zoning amendments, and building permits related to data centers. County staff would study potential effects on energy use, water demand, noise, and land use in coordination with local utility providers.
objective: Pause data centers in Moore County pending regulations

summary: TenKey LandCo is seeking to build three 200,000-square-foot data center facilities with on-site natural gas power generation on over 200 acres off I-65 near Franklin, Kentucky (population under 11,000). The Franklin Planning and Zoning Commission approved a preliminary development plan in March 2026 after hours of heated public comment. TenKey also filed a lawsuit to strike down a county land use ordinance that posed regulatory barriers to the project.
objective: Rally against TenKey LandCo data center in Simpson County

summary: Three citizen groups -- Tucker United, West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club -- filed an appeal challenging the state Air Quality Board's decision to uphold an air quality permit for the proposed Ridgeline power plant and data center in Tucker County, located between Davis and Thomas. The 500-acre facility would include a natural gas power plant fueling a data center. The appeal argues the WVDEP wrongly classified the facility as a 'synthetic minor source' rather than a 'major source' of emissions.
objective: Challenge Fundamental Data air quality permits in court

bill_name: H.727 - Vermont Sustainable Data Centers Act
summary: Regulates data centers requiring 20+ MW power capacity. Focuses on proper siting, environmental protections, and ratepayer cost controls. Referred to Committee on Energy and Digital Infrastructure.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in VT

summary: Establishes property, tangible, sales and use tax exemptions as incentives for qualified data centers locating in Rhode Island. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10.
objective: Repeal data center tax exemptions in RI

summary: Requires data centers needing large amounts of electricity to fund the cost of necessary infrastructure improvements to supply that electricity. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study.
objective: Study data center impacts on electrical grid in RI

bill_name: LD 713 - Exclude Data Centers from Tax Exemptions
summary: Excludes data centers from Maine's Business Equipment Tax Exemption (BETE) program (100% property tax break) and the Dirigo Business Incentives Programs. Also requires the Maine DECD to study other state incentives data centers may benefit from. Presented to Taxation Committee March 12, 2026.
objective: Create data center property tax exemptions in ME

summary: Sentinel Data Centers has an agreement to occupy 1 million square feet on two floors of a former paper mill in Jay, Maine, employing 800-1,000 construction workers and 125 permanent staff. The project faces an existential threat from LD 307, a bill advancing in the Maine Legislature that would impose a two-year moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Notably, the developer has not encountered local opposition from Jay residents, partly because the project does not seek special tax breaks.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Sentinel Data Centers in Jay

summary: Northern New England Energy Company is proposing the 'Sanford Woods Industrial and Technical Campus,' a 100-300MW data center covering over 1,000 acres along the Mousam River in Sanford. The developer claims the facility would generate its own power using fuel cells, solar, and battery storage without burdening Maine's grid. The project is threatened by LD 307, a proposed state moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Local residents have raised concerns about noise, environmental damage, and the significant changes a facility of this scale would bring.
objective: Oppose Northern New England Energy data center over noise in Sanford

summary: Would codify a 'bring your own power' mandate effective July 1, 2026. Generators supplying electricity to large customers (20+ MW) via private connection must obtain a PURA license and produce 100% of projected demand plus reserve margin using new generation. Large customers must post financial guarantees covering 85% of requested service for 10+ years.
objective: Codify bring-your-own-power mandate for data centers in Connecticut

summary: A massive estimated $10 billion data center project backed by Georgia Lt. Gov. Burt Jones and his family is proposed for Butts County as part of the Highway 16 Interstate Health Development near I-75, which would include 11 million square feet of data centers. The project is expected to consume over 4.5 million gallons of water per day, more than tripling Butts County's current water usage. Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr accused Jones of using his office to benefit his family's property interests, and environmental advocates have flagged the lack of available information about the facility's cooling technology and energy demands.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Butts County

bill_name: SB 265 - Data Center Tax Incentive Reform
summary: Limits data center abatement periods to 20 years starting Jan 1, 2027. Mandates large-scale data centers using 100+ MW pay state sales and use taxes. Governor may waive taxes for economically struggling counties. Passed Senate March 13, 2026.
objective: Limit data center tax abatements to 20 years in Alabama

summary: Imposes a one-year moratorium on construction of new large-scale solar power facilities, prompted by opposition to a 4,500-acre solar project in Stockton meant to power Meta's Montgomery data center expansion. Exempts TVA solar capacity and projects already under construction.
objective: Enact statewide data center moratorium in AL

summary: Following Governor Jeff Landry's announcement of Amazon's $12 billion investment in three interconnected data center campuses across Caddo and Bossier parishes, Commissioner Chris Kracman proposed a one-year moratorium on developments using water from Caddo Lake, but the resolution failed to receive a second from any other commissioner. Amazon has pledged to limit cooling water use to less than 13% of the year and invest $400 million in public water systems in the Caddo-Bossier area.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Caddo Parish

summary: As part of Amazon's $12 billion Northwest Louisiana data center investment, STACK Infrastructure is developing a 1,089-acre 'Highway 3 Technology Park' north of Benton in Bossier Parish, planned for over 1.3 million square feet across six buildings. The Bossier Parish Police Jury scheduled a public hearing for March 18, 2026 -- the first opportunity for public comment since the project was confirmed. Residents have raised concerns about air quality, proximity to homes, water supply, and rising utility costs.
objective: Rally against data center in Bossier Parish

summary: Creates public records exemption allowing local governments to keep data center siting plans confidential for up to 12 months. Includes 5-year sunset provision (Oct 2031). Companion to SB 484.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via SB 484

opposition_groups: Stop the Monticello Data Centers MN
summary: UPDATE: Monticello residents rallied against two proposed data centers (600+ acres combined, 4M+ sq ft). An online petition exceeded 1,200 signatures. Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy filed a lawsuit against the city challenging the review process. A Facebook opposition group quickly gained traction. Residents gathered at local parks to distribute yard signs.
objective: Legal challenge to Monticello Tech data center in Monticello

opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development
summary: The Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development (370 members) and eight residents filed a lawsuit in Dakota County District Court to block Tract's approved plan to build up to 12 data centers across 343 acres, a $5 billion project on the former Fountain Valley Golf Club site. The lawsuit alleges the city failed to address Minnesota DNR concerns about groundwater impacts, risks to private wells, and cumulative water sustainability effects. Castle Rock Township separately sued Farmington for breach of the Orderly Annexation Agreement.
objective: Legal challenge to Tract data center in Farmington

summary: Virginia-based CloudHQ received preliminary approval from the Chaska City Council in October 2024 for a 1.4-million-square-foot data center at the West Creek Corporate Center off Engler Boulevard, despite vocal opposition from residents of the adjacent Clover Ridge neighborhood. Residents objected during a three-hour council meeting, citing the building's height at 72 feet (double the 36-foot zoning limit requiring a variance), construction noise, and the facility's incompatibility with the residential surroundings. The council voted 4-1 in favor.
objective: Oppose CloudHQ data center over noise in Chaska

summary: The Warrenton Board of Aldermen voted unanimously in January 2026 to approve a conditional use permit for a Crusoe data center on a 340-acre parcel, consisting of two 805,000-square-foot buildings and a 40,000-square-foot administration building. More than 40 residents spoke both for and against the proposal, with opponents raising concerns about noise, light pollution, water supply impacts on aquifers, and the pace of the approval process.
objective: Require use permit for Crusoe data center in Warrenton

summary: Gateway Digital Campus and Provident Data Centers are seeking to rezone roughly 600 acres of agricultural land near I-44 and Robertsville Road in Villa Ridge for a hyperscale data center, sitting directly adjacent to the Shaw Nature Reserve. The project returned to the Franklin County Planning & Zoning Commission on March 17, 2026 despite being tabled for 6 months in January. Both Gateway and the Beltline Energy project are on the same March 17 agenda, alongside proposed county-wide data center regulations including 400ft setbacks, 800-acre site limits, and sound limits.
objective: Block Gateway Digital Campus rezoning of 600 acres of farmland

summary: The city of Osawatomie entered a pre-development agreement with Alcove Development/Northland Data Centers for a $900 million to $1.1 billion data center project on 115 acres of city-owned land, requiring one million gallons of water and 150MW of power per day. Residents have pushed back, citing the city's aging water infrastructure, fears of noise, and lack of community input. The Miami County Commission is considering a temporary moratorium countywide while proper regulations are developed.
objective: Oppose Alcove Development data center over water in Osawatomie

summary: Would allow privately built power generation facilities (1,000+ MW) to serve large industrial customers like data centers, with eminent domain protections. Drafted to enable Google's proposed fourth Nebraska data center requiring 1,000-3,000 MW of power, powered by a privately built natural gas plant.
objective: Prohibit eminent domain for data center land acquisition

opposition_groups: Say NO to Data Centers in Troy IL
summary: The Troy Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers in March 2026, following the collapse of Cloverleaf Infrastructure's proposal for a 1.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center sited less than a mile from neighborhoods, daycares, and senior living facilities. Hundreds of residents packed a meeting at Triad High School in February to oppose the project. The moratorium prevents new data center applications while the planning commission strengthens zoning ordinances. Troy Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers on March 13, after Cloverleaf Infrastructure's 500MW proposal collapsed following massive public opposition. Hundreds of residents packed Triad High School in February. City Council final passage expected March 16.
objective: Halt data centers in Troy after Cloverleaf Infrastructure proposal

summary: HydraVault received a full building permit from Chicago to construct a 76,000-square-foot, 20-megawatt AI data center at 2538 S. Wabash Avenue in the South Loop. Residents reported opposition due to energy consumption, water use, and the lack of public hearings — the project was classified as a 'minor change' by zoning officials, bypassing community review.
objective: Oppose HydraVault data center over water in Chicago South Loop

opposition_groups: Block Project Steel and Project Cardinal
summary: After a nearly six-hour contentious city council meeting, Yorkville approved Pioneer Development's 'Project Cardinal' — a 1,037-acre, 1.8-gigawatt data center campus with 14 buildings totaling 17 million square feet, plus a $51 million upfront payment. Residents spoke against it, citing water usage, noise, air quality, and property value concerns. Yorkville City Council approved Project Cardinal at 12:51 AM on March 12 after a nearly 6-hour contentious meeting. The 1,037-acre campus (Pioneer Development LLC) includes 14 buildings totaling 17M sq ft. Development agreement includes $51M in upfront payments over 4 years and $68.25M directed to the school district ($91M total). First data center operational by 2029.
objective: Rally against Pioneer Development data center in Yorkville

summary: Cloverleaf Infrastructure has been in detailed discussions with Edwardsville officials since mid-2025 about a data center south of New Poag Road. Obtained emails revealed extensive behind-the-scenes planning despite public assurances of 'no formal proposal.' Residents for Responsible Growth organized a meeting of nearly 300 people in March 2026.
objective: Rally against Cloverleaf Infrastructure data center in Edwardsville

summary: Metrobloks proposed a 154,372-square-foot data center at 505 N. Sherman Drive in Martindale-Brightwood, one of Indianapolis's oldest historic Black neighborhoods. The Indianapolis NAACP reversed its initial support, and six city-county councilors called on Metrobloks to pause or withdraw, citing pollution compounding decades of existing environmental burdens including lead-contaminated soil.
objective: Oppose Metrobloks data center as environmental injustice

summary: Sabey Data Centers proposed a 1-million-square-foot, 250-megawatt data center on 130 acres in Decatur Township, filing for a variance of use rather than full rezoning — bypassing a City-County Council vote. Dozens organized under 'Protect Decatur Township,' citing public health risks and property value impacts. The MDC hearing examiner recommended approval.
objective: Block Sabey Data Centers rezoning of 130 acres in Indianapolis

summary: Sprigg Township trustees passed a 12-month voluntary moratorium on data centers in late February 2026, in response to a proposed facility near the former Stuart power plant requiring 1,300 megawatts — 31 times Adams County's annual power consumption. First of two Adams County localities to act, followed by Monroe Township.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Sprigg Township

summary: Monroe Township trustees unanimously passed a 12-month moratorium on data centers and small modular nuclear reactors in early March 2026, also establishing a zoning commission for the 27-square-mile jurisdiction including the former Killen power plant. Second Adams County locality to act after Sprigg Township.
objective: Impose 12-month moratorium and create zoning commission in Monroe

summary: Ashville Village Council unanimously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on December 20, 2025, in response to EdgeConneX's proposal on 680 acres including an on-site 200-megawatt natural gas power plant. Residents lined yards with 'No Data Center' signs. EdgeConneX withdrew its annexation application but reportedly already holds land zoned for data center use.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Ashville (EdgeConneX)

summary: Columbus City Council held hearings in March 2026 examining rapid data center growth in central Ohio, with large facilities potentially requiring up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Of 130 data centers in the area, only 18 fall within city limits, limiting regulatory authority. Some councilmembers called for a moratorium though no legislation has been introduced.
objective: Rally against data center in Columbus

summary: Bipartisan bill prohibiting NDAs between data center companies and municipalities. Still allows withholding genuine trade secrets. Prompted by revelation that at least 4 Wisconsin communities signed secrecy deals. Passed Senate Committee 4-1.
objective: Ban data center NDAs in Wisconsin (original bipartisan bill)

summary: Port of Walla Walla unanimously approved $36M sale of 553 acres at Wallula Gap Business Park to Amazon Web Services for a $4.8B, 16-building data center campus. The Amazon deal has been confirmed. Community members voiced opposition at the port meeting, citing concerns about water usage, agricultural land loss, and impacts on the Columbia River ecosystem.
objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center over water in Walla Walla

summary: Tulsa City Councilor Laura Bellis proposed a 365-day moratorium on new data center construction citywide. The ordinance would pause acceptance, processing, and issuance of building permits for data centers while a working group studies impacts. The moratorium was heard at the March 11 council meeting and a final vote is scheduled for March 25, 2026. Bellis says not all colleagues are on board. The moratorium would not affect existing Project Anthem ($800M, 340 acres in east Tulsa). Bellis is open to amendments including a shorter duration.  Tulsa City Council passed the moratorium unanimously on March 25, amended to 9 months (through Dec 31, 2026). Exempts Project Anthem phases 1-2 and Project Clydesdale.
objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Tulsa

summary: SB 1488, sponsored by Republican Sen. Sacchieri, would have imposed a three-year moratorium on data centers with electrical load greater than 100 MW until November 1, 2029. Directed the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to study impacts on water supply, utility rates, property values, and ideal siting locations. The bill missed key legislative deadlines and died without advancing from committee.
objective: Pause data center permits for study via SB 1488

summary: Mat-Su Borough Assembly passed a resolution 5-2 on March 2, 2026 to partner with Terra Energy Center Corp. to market borough-owned land near Big Lake and Port MacKenzie for data center or industrial development. Mayor Edna DeVries vetoed the resolution, citing concerns about entering a marketing agreement without full vetting, unapproved development locations, and lack of conditional use permit discussion. Override requires at least five Assembly votes at the next regular meeting.
objective: Rally against Terra Energy Center data center in Matanuska-Susitna

summary: Shreveport Metropolitan Planning Commission denied a special use permit for a 2.8 million sq ft data center at 7340 Greenwood Road (Resilient Technology Park) on a 4-4 vote on December 3, 2025, after major community backlash over noise, water use (7.5M gallons/day), and environmental impacts. The Shreveport City Council overturned the denial on December 18, 2025 in a unanimous 7-0 vote, and separately approved selling up to 7.5M gallons of water daily to the facility. A subsequent lawsuit was filed accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations.
objective: Deny Franks Investment Company conditional use permit in Shreveport

summary: Hermiston City Council voted in September 2025 to annex 800 acres of land to attract more data centers, with only one councilor (Jackie Linton) voting against. Linton raised concerns about water supply impacts and inadequate review time. Amazon Web Services already has dozens of data centers across Umatilla and Morrow counties and is building two more in Hermiston. The city is positioning itself as a major hub for cloud computing alongside nearby Boardman and Umatilla.
objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center over water in Hermiston

summary: The Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act of 2026, authored by Rep. Brad Boles (R-Marlow). Requires data centers adding 75+ MW to pay their proportional share of power infrastructure costs, establishes separate rate structures for large load customers, mandates minimum 10-year service commitments to prevent stranded costs, and prohibits passing infrastructure expenses to residential ratepayers. Passed unanimously through both the House Utilities and Energy committees; headed to House floor. Aligns with Trump's Ratepayer Protection Pledge. HB 2992 (Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act by Rep. Brad Boles) passed House Utilities Committee 7-0 and House Energy and Natural Resources Oversight Committee unanimously. Now eligible for full House floor vote.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via HB 2992

summary: Three-bill package introduced by Sens. Bayer, Shink, and Geiss (D). SB 761 prohibits consumptive water withdrawals exceeding 2 million gallons/day by data centers. SB 762 requires Michigan Public Service Commission to publish annual reports on data center energy and water usage. SB 763 prohibits passing data center infrastructure improvement costs to residential ratepayers. Referred to Senate Committee on Energy and Environment.
objective: Ban data center water withdrawals over 2M gallons/day (SB 761-763)

summary: The Montana Right to Compute Act (MRTCA), signed by Governor Gianforte on April 16, 2025, establishes a legal right to own and use computational resources (hardware, software, algorithms, data centers). Requires any government regulation restricting computing resources to be 'demonstrably necessary and narrowly tailored.' Applies broadly to all computational resources, effectively limiting local governments' ability to restrict data center siting or operations through zoning or regulation. Also requires AI systems managing critical infrastructure to have human override capabilities.
objective: Establish right to compute, limiting local data center regulation

summary: Expands Mississippi's data center tax incentive program. Grants sales and use tax exemptions to data centers investing $250M+ and creating 35+ jobs at 125% of average state wages. Adds electricity purchases to exempt 'state taxes.' Provides automatic 10-year extension eligibility for qualifying expansions ($100M+). Excludes cryptocurrency mining. Gives 60-day cure period for audit noncompliance. Effective July 1, 2025.
objective: Create data center tax incentive program in MS

summary: Part of Arkansas IMPACT economic development package. Expands data center sales and use tax exemptions by reducing the qualified investment threshold from $500M to $100M. Creates new 'Qualified Large Data Center' category for multi-site facilities investing $2B+ within 10 years. Shifts compliance enforcement from AEDC to Department of Finance and Administration. Signed April 10, 2025; key provisions effective October 1, 2025.
objective: Create data center tax incentive program in AR

summary: The Generating Arkansas Jobs Act of 2025 allows electric utilities to finance 'strategic investments' (infrastructure for economic development projects like data centers) during construction through a new rate increase rider, capped at keeping rates 10% below the national average. Includes emergency clause. Enables utility buildout for data centers valued at a combined $12B+ planned for Conway, Little Rock, and West Memphis.
objective: Regulate data center development in Arkansas

summary: Introduced by Sens. Whitehouse (D-RI) and Fetterman (D-PA). Establishes emissions performance standard for electricity used by data centers and crypto-mining facilities exceeding 100 kW IT capacity. Creates region-specific emissions baselines reduced by 11% annually from 2027-2034, reaching net-zero by 2035. Fees collected fund zero-carbon generation, long-duration storage, and grants to lower residential electricity costs. Referred to Committee on Environment and Public Works; 9% chance of enactment per GovTrack.
objective: Set federal emissions standards for data center electricity

summary: Executive Order 14318, 'Accelerating Federal Permitting of Data Center Infrastructure,' signed by President Trump on July 23, 2025. Directs agencies to streamline NEPA reviews, establish new categorical exclusions for data center and energy projects, and designate qualifying projects ($500M+ capital or 100+ MW load) as 'transparency projects' for expedited FAST-41 review. Authorizes use of federal land for data centers; DOE selected four initial sites. Directs Commerce Dept. to provide financial support including loans, grants, and tax incentives.
objective: Require data centers to report energy consumption in US

summary: Requires annual report disclosing information about each data center operator receiving a sales tax exemption, including whether job creation and investment goals have been met.
bill_name: HB 784 - Data Center Tax Exemption Reporting
objective: Mandate transparency and reporting from data centers in VA

summary: Establishes $3/sq ft tax on data center facilities above 25,000 sq ft starting January 1, 2027. Dedicates at least $250M annually to State Park Conservation Resources Fund, Virginia Land Conservation Fund, statewide trail system, and Virginia Tribal Commitment Fund.
objective: Impose $3/sq ft tax on data centers for conservation (SB 393)

summary: Prohibits utilities from recovering costs of transmission lines and energy generation serving data centers over 100 MW from residential ratepayers. House subcommittee recommended continuing to next session.
bill_name: HB 503 - Data Center Cost Recovery Prohibition
objective: Prohibit utility cost recovery for 100MW+ data center lines (HB 503)

summary: Sponsored by Sen. Elgie Sims Jr. (D). Requires data centers receiving state tax credits to enter into labor peace agreements with unions representing workers who operate and maintain critical systems. Under LPAs, employers agree not to interfere with unionization efforts while unions agree not to strike. Would add labor requirements to Illinois's existing Data Center Investment Program. Moving through the Senate.
objective: Require labor peace agreements for data center tax credits

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Stokes County
Occaneechi Band of the Saponi Nation
summary: In March 2026, the Southern Coalition for Social Justice filed a lawsuit (Hairston Clan v. Stokes County) in Stokes County Superior Court challenging the 3-2 commissioner vote that approved rezoning for Project Delta, a proposed 1,800-acre AI data center near Walnut Cove by the Dan River. Plaintiffs argue commissioners approved the project without properly considering environmental impacts (air quality, water usage, noise), and that the land holds significance for historically Black farming communities and the Occaneechi Band of the Saponi Nation. The project is speculative -- Engineered Land Solutions has no tenant lined up.
objective: Challenge Engineered Land Solutions air quality permits in court

summary: Henrico's Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested rights claim for a data center complex on nearly 200 acres along Darbytown Road in the Varina district on Feb. 26, 2026. CEO David Wagner had filed the claim in October 2025, arguing the company had a right to build under the pre-June 2025 rules. But a key factor was that Wagner did not purchase the site until November 2025 -- a month after filing his claim. The company had previously withdrawn its provisional use permit application. Henrico adopted new rules in June 2025 requiring all hyperscale data centers to go through public hearings. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.
objective: Uphold denial of Centra Logistics data center on appeal

summary: Reston-based WestDulles Properties filed an appeal of county staff's determination that a conditional use permit (CUP) is required before building a data center at the James River Industrial Center site. Chesterfield's new zoning ordinance, effective Jan. 1, 2026, made data centers a conditional use in industrially zoned land. WestDulles argues the project doesn't require a CUP because data centers were a by-right use under the previous ordinance in the I-3 Heavy Industrial district. The site was originally eyed for a sprawling $2B Shandong Tranlin Paper Co. production facility that never materialized. The matter is slated for the Chesterfield Board of Zoning Appeals in May 2026. The site is adjacent to the planned Commonwealth Fusion Systems nuclear fusion power plant.
objective: Require use permit for WestDulles Properties data center in Chesterfield County

summary: Powhatan County Board of Supervisors voted 5-0 on Oct. 29, 2025 to approve rezoning of approximately 61 additional acres to expand the planned Province Group data center campus from 120 to 181 acres (1.5M to 2M sq ft). This came despite the Planning Commission's unanimous recommendation of denial on Oct. 7. The $2.7B campus near the Powhatan-Chesterfield line could eventually house up to four data center buildings requiring 300-400 MW of power and a new substation. Estimated annual tax revenue at full build-out: $9.7 million. The original 120-acre campus was approved 3-2 in October 2024.
objective: Block Province Group rezoning of 181 acres in Powhatan County

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: Amazon proposed three 110-foot-tall data center buildings totaling nearly 1.1 million sq ft on 52 acres along Ashton Avenue near Manassas Mall, sandwiched between three schools and a townhome development. The Prince William County Planning Commission voted 6-1 in June 2024 to recommend denial due to noise, wildlife, and neighborhood proximity concerns. The Board of County Supervisors shelved the proposal indefinitely in October 2024 and it was deferred again in January 2025 to a date uncertain. Amazon maintains it still plans to build on the site but no hearing date is set.
objective: Deny Amazon Web Services data center on 52 acres in Prince William

summary: DeKalb County Commission is considering data center regulations proposed by County Mayor Matt Adcock after receiving an inquiry about locating a nuclear-powered data center in the county. The proposed resolution would prohibit data centers within 5,000 feet of residences, schools, daycares, parks, churches, commercial, or industrial establishments. Applicants would need permits with appeal rights. Commissioner Tom Chandler called the resolution 'a sham' arguing the 5,000-foot restriction makes permit approval impossible anywhere in the county. The action was tabled to allow public input; no final vote recorded.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in DeKalb County

opposition_groups: West Virginia Center on Budget & Policy
summary: Would restore local government control over data center siting, reversing provisions of the 2025 Power Generation and Consumption Act that stripped counties of jurisdiction. Never received committee hearing.
bill_name: SB 658 - Data Center Local Control Restoration
objective: Restore local government authority over data centers in WV

summary: Senate budget proposes accelerating data center tax exemption sunset from 2035 to January 2027, redirecting $1.6B/year to tax rebates, transportation, and water infrastructure. House budget preserves exemption but adds clean energy requirements. Unresolved; General Assembly reconvenes April 22.
bill_name: FY2027-28 Budget - Data Center Tax Exemption
objective: Accelerate data center tax exemption sunset in VA

summary: The Maricopa County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved a modernized zoning ordinance establishing specific rules for data center development, effective January 9, 2026. The new rules limit data centers to two industrial zoning districts (IND-2 and IND-3), requiring full rezoning for any other land.
objective: Block data center rezoning in Maricopa County

opposition_groups: Arizona Center for Law in the Public Interest
summary: The No Desert Data Center Coalition filed a lawsuit alleging Pima County violated Arizona's Open Meeting Law when rezoning for 'Project Blue.' Separately, AG Kris Mayes challenged the Corporation Commission's approval of a 286 MW power deal between Tucson Electric Power and the developer, arguing it violates the state constitution.
objective: Sue Pima County over Open Meeting Law violations (Beale rezoning)

summary: CoreSite is building a 600,000-square-foot data center in Elyria-Swansea, one of the most polluted zip codes in the country (80216), which proceeded as a by-right use without community review. Hundreds packed a February 2026 meeting demanding transparency, and delivered a 'Good Neighbor' proposal. Denver's mayor subsequently joined calls for a moratorium.
objective: Mobilize opposition to CoreSite data center in Denver

summary: Tucson began developing new zoning regulations in February 2026 for data centers over 50,000 square feet, requiring water/power disclosure and empowering the city to fine or shut off water if usage exceeds allotment by 120%. Heavy industrial zones designated as most suitable locations.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Tucson

summary: Despite community opposition from RAZE and the Ak-Chin Indian Community, Pinal County approved three major data center projects in November 2025 totaling ~3,900 acres, including the $33 billion Vermaland 'La Osa' project near Eloy. Residents raised concerns about desert water use, noise, cultural impacts to tribal land, and infrastructure strain.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Pinal County

opposition_groups: Iron County Citizens for Responsible Growth
summary: 350+ residents packed Festival Hall on March 5, 2026 for a hearing on the Antelope Data Center proposed for 640 acres on Antelope Springs Road, 15 miles NW of Cedar City. Of 15+ speakers over 2 hours, only one supported the project. Concerns: water (2.6M gallon initial fill), utility prices, farmland loss, noise, pollution.
objective: Rally against data center in Iron County

summary: Millard County Commission approved rezoning of 4,400+ acres from agricultural to forest/range for Creekstone Energy solar farm to power AI data centers, despite resident opposition. Concerns about ruining land's multipurpose use, visual impact, and long-term consequences.
objective: Oppose Creekstone Energy data center in Millard County

opposition_groups: Empowerment Congress of Doña Ana County
summary: Two lawsuits filed against Doña Ana County over 'Project Jupiter' — a 1,400-acre data center complex near Sunland Park with $165B in Industrial Revenue Bonds. Empowerment Congress and NMELC allege Open Meetings Act violations and improper LEDA tax rebates. Project includes natural gas power plants and desalination plant. Could emit more greenhouse gases than Albuquerque and Las Cruces combined.
objective: Sue over open meeting violations in OpenAI approval

summary: Prohibits counties (<500K pop) from denying zoning approval for small modular reactors after federal design certification and site permitting. Allows SMR construction colocated with large industrial users (data centers) without ACC environmental compatibility certificate. Cleared House on party-line vote Feb 26, advancing to Senate. Gov. Hobbs vetoed similar bill in 2025.
objective: Allow SMR colocation with data centers in Arizona counties

summary: CyrusOne filed plans for a 400-megawatt data center campus in Whitney, Hill County, with an initial $750 million building due by October 2026. Residents packed a town hall citing water use from Lake Whitney (a petition with 6,000 signatures), noise, and tax abatements. The opposition has become a key issue in the 2026 Republican primary for Texas Senate District 22.
objective: 6000-signature petition opposing data center in Hill County

opposition_groups: Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers
summary: Amazon is building a 1-million-square-foot data center at the Keystone Trade Center in Falls Township, fast-tracked through Governor Shapiro's PA Permit Fast Track Program. Residents cite energy grid strain, increased electricity rates, and diesel generator impacts. A regional survey found 42% of SE Pennsylvania residents oppose data centers vs 34% in support.
objective: Oppose Amazon data center in Falls Township

summary: Bryan City Council proactively annexed 162 acres near the RELLIS campus and adopted a Planned Development zoning district allowing data centers with stricter-than-normal standards including increased setbacks, noise requirements, and screening specifications. No data center project has been formally proposed yet.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Bryan

summary: The Oneonta Town Board passed a 12-month moratorium on AI data centers in March 2026 after residents presented more than 700 signatures, prompted by Eco-Yotta Inc.'s plans to rezone 150 acres of farmland. This came weeks after the nearby Town of Dryden instituted an outright ban following its own 18-month moratorium.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Oneonta (Eco-Yotta)

summary: Developer proposing to demolish White Clay Corporate Center (former JPMorganChase offices) and replace with 847,000 sq ft, three-building data center complex on 44-acre site. Already properly zoned. New Castle County Council passed new data center regulations March 10.
objective: Rally against data center in Newark

summary: Grant County Commissioners passed a 24-month moratorium on data centers on March 2, 2026. The Area Plan Commission began drafting ordinance language focusing on transparency, resource impacts, and local benefits, including questions about client disclosure and annual energy/water reporting.
objective: Impose 24-month data centers moratorium in Grant County

summary: 795-acre data center (Hillwood/PowerHouse) on south Joliet farmland above depleting aquifer. Plan Commission approved 7-1 despite 4+ hours of opposition testimony. Hundreds packed City Hall. Would be largest data center in Illinois. Plan Commission approved 7-1 on March 5. At the March 17 City Council meeting, a marathon 6.5-hour public hearing forced a recess -- vote delayed to Thursday March 19 due to election day restrictions. Developers promise $100M community contribution, 7,000-10,000 construction jobs, and 700 permanent jobs. Would be the largest data center in Illinois at $20B, 1.8GW, 795 acres. Joliet City Council approved the $20B PowerHouse/Hillwood campus 8-1 on March 20 after a 6.5-hour public hearing. Only Councilmember Suzanna Ibarra voted no.
objective: Rally against Hillwood data center in Joliet

summary: Lake County Council approved stringent data center regulations: only permitted in industrial zones with special approval, 200-ft setbacks from residential/agricultural, 55dB noise max, underground utilities, decommissioning plans, and annual firefighter training requirements.
objective: Impose 55 dB noise limit on data centers in Lake County

bill_name: Senate Democrats Data Center Accountability Package
summary: Package of 6+ bills by Sen. Kent Smith (D-Euclid): Data Center Grid Cost Responsibility Act, Tax Break Ban, Powering Ohio Communities Act, Local Development Protection Act, Power Reliability and Fairness Act, and Responsible Water Use Act (cap daily use at 5M gallons).
objective: Cap data center water consumption in OH

summary: Thomas County Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on AI data center construction in January 2026. Commission Chair Mark NeSmith said the board was 'certainly not anti-data centers' but preferred to be proactive in protecting the community while gathering more information.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Thomas County

summary: Google proposing 1GW data center on 546 acres near Irwinville in agricultural zone. Planning Commission voted to recommend approval Feb 26. Residents concerned about water (1M+ gallons/day), noise, air pollution, and farmland loss. Change.org petition active.
objective: Petition to block Google data center in Irwin County

summary: Clarksdale exploring 648-acre data center between Sunflower River and Roundyard neighborhood. Contentious city board meeting considered rezoning from commercial/agricultural to light industrial. Board tabled vote after failed motion. Stipulations included $5M impact fee, water usage guarantees, and setbacks.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Clarksdale

summary: House companion to SB 265. Limits abatements for data center projects over $200M to 20 years. Requires data centers to pay real property taxes and sales/use taxes allocated to General Fund. Passed House 103-0.
objective: Regulate data center impacts via SB 265

summary: Amazon's two Madison County campuses house 700+ diesel generators capable of producing ~2,000MW. Amazon seeking MDEQ approval for 100+ additional generators. Nearby subdivision residents report spike in dust, traffic, noise, and congestion.
objective: Oppose Amazon data center over noise in Madison County

summary: Geronimo Power proposing data center near Reading. Planning Commission voted against allowing data centers in agricultural preservation district. Standing-room-only public hearing Feb 4, 2026. Commissioners approved AUAR environmental review 4-1 but discussed moratorium.
objective: Oppose Geronimo Power data center in Nobles County

summary: MCEA filed an appeal against Faribault over an 'inadequate' environmental review of Archer Datacenters' proposal on 87.6 acres, alleging the developer dropped estimated greenhouse gas emissions by 98% between draft and final stages without explanation, and failure to analyze cumulative impacts from the wave of nearby data center proposals.
objective: Challenge data center air permits in Faribault

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: Board of Supervisors voted to eliminate by-right approval for data centers in the Data Center Opportunity Zone Overlay District, requiring special use permits for all future data centers.
objective: End by-right data center approvals in Prince William overlay zone

summary: Culpeper County unanimously required conditional use permits for data centers on industrial land in September 2025, impacting 3,000+ acres across 140+ parcels that previously allowed by-right construction. Five projects in the Culpeper Technology Zone remain vested. The Piedmont Environmental Council and Coalition to Save Culpeper pushed for the stronger regulations.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Culpeper County

summary: GigaLand LLC resubmitted a scaled-down data center proposal near Remington after withdrawing the original in September 2025, reducing building space by 55% (from 2.2M to 998,662 sq ft) and cutting substation capacity from 1.2 GW to 600 MW, with a $3 billion estimated cost on 202 acres. Opposition groups including Protect Fauquier and the Piedmont Environmental Council continue monitoring.
objective: Oppose data center development in Fauquier County

summary: Authorizes a federal study on the environmental, economic, and public health impacts of data centers, with a focus on communities of color and low-income communities. Requires the Secretary of Energy to conduct the study and report findings to Congress. Endorsed by NRDC, Climate Justice Alliance, GreenLatinos, Food & Water Watch.
objective: Require data center energy and water reports in US

summary: Originally filed as HF 2447, renumbered to HF 2690 after committee approval on Feb 23, 2026. Amendment H-8214 filed March 12. Key provisions: quarterly water/energy reporting, public disclosure within 30 days, separate utility rate class for data centers using 20+ MW. Sponsored by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids). First-of-its-kind legislation targeting water utilities specifically.
objective: Require data center energy and water reports via HF 2447

summary: Requires data center operators to generate all electricity they consume on-site rather than drawing from the grid. Mandates 75% renewable by 2035 and 100% renewable by 2040. Civil penalties up to $100,000 per day for violations.
objective: Require data centers to generate 100% of electricity on-site

summary: House companion to S. 1475. Establishes emissions performance standard for electricity used by data centers and cryptomining facilities. Creates fee system; collected fees fund zero-carbon generation, long-duration energy storage, and grants to lower residential electricity costs.
objective: Establish emissions fee system for data centers (House companion)

bill_name: H.R. 5227 - Unleashing Low-Cost Rural AI Act
summary: Bipartisan bill requiring Departments of Energy, Interior, and Agriculture to study the impact of AI and data center growth on energy supply in the United States, with a focus on rural communities.
objective: Commission legislative study on data center impacts in US

summary: DOE directed FERC to standardize interconnection of large loads (>20 MW, primarily data centers). Proposes expedited 60-day studies for flexible loads, 100% network upgrade cost responsibility for large load customers. Final rule due April 30, 2026.
objective: DOE order standardizing large-load grid interconnection rules

summary: FERC ordered PJM Interconnection to develop rules for colocating data centers at power plants. Creates three new transmission services for co-located loads. Major victory for owners of gas-fired and nuclear plants seeking to serve data centers directly.
objective: Order PJM to develop data center colocation rules at power plants

summary: Regulates energy consumption by data centers. Requires annual disclosure reporting, prohibits fossil fuel PPAs, directs PSC to establish a surcharge/discount plan. Mandates progressive transition to renewable energy (33% by 2030, 67% by 2035, 100% by 2040).
objective: Ban data center development NDAs in NY

summary: Requires electric/gas corporations to establish an independent classification of service for large energy use facilities separate from other rate classes. Authorizes DPS to develop financial surety requirements. Prevents data center costs from being blended into residential rate cases.
objective: Regulate data center development in New York

summary: HB 1724, sponsored by Rep. Wendy Thomas, requires public utilities to submit annual reports detailing transmission costs, wholesale electricity market impacts, and generation capacity adequacy, with specific analysis of data center-related demand. Mandates public hearings and review by the state energy agency. Referred to the House Science, Technology and Energy Committee with a public hearing held February 10, 2026.
objective: Require utility reporting of data center transmission impacts in NH

summary: Would have appropriated $500K to the State Engineer to study large-scale industrial water use by data centers, carbon capture, and hydrogen projects. Advanced 8-1 out of House Agriculture Committee but did not pass full chamber.
objective: Cap data center water consumption in WY

summary: Allows state to sell or lease Mississippi DPS land in Meridian to Compass Datacenters for expansion of existing data center property. Passed both chambers.
objective: Authorize land sale for data center development in MS

summary: Would have redirected 80% of local ad-valorem taxes collected over $1B from large projects (including data centers) into a new state fund, with 20% to local government/schools. Author did not bring to committee vote.
objective: Redirect data center tax revenue to state fund

summary: ~50 residents pushing back on a 1-million-sq-ft, five-building East Thistle Data Center at 48th St and Thistle Landing Drive, replacing a largely vacant office park. Residents say they were unaware of data center implications when zoning was approved two years prior. Concerns include noise, health impacts, and water.
objective: Oppose Menlo Equities data center over water in Ahwatukee

summary: Tract acquired 2,069 acres near Buckeye Municipal Airport for a 1.8 GW data center park (up to 40 data centers, 20M sq ft). A Change.org petition garnered ~100 signatures citing water concerns. Tract and Buckeye agreed to cap water at 2,000 acre-feet.
objective: 100-signature petition opposing Tract data center
opposition_groups: Buckeye Residents Against Data Centers (ad hoc)

summary: Reno Planning Commission denied Oppidan's 5 MW evaporative-cooling data center on Stead Blvd in a 4-2 vote. City Council unanimously overturned the denial in March 2025. Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter opposed, concerned about precedent for evaporative-cooling water consumption.
objective: Deny Oppidan 5MW data center permit in Reno

summary: The Summit Ridge Data Center, a 100 MW off-grid natural gas-powered facility, was approved by City Council in Oct 2024 without adequate public awareness. Residents rallied outside City Hall, feeling 'blindsided.' A Change.org petition has 1,350+ signatures. Concerns about air quality from natural gas generators, noise, and lack of transparency.
objective: Petition to block DCPC data center in Santaquin

summary: City Council voted 10-0 for a 360-day moratorium on data center development. UPDATE: Lowell City Council voted 10-0 on March 11 to impose a 360-day moratorium on the expansion of existing data centers and the construction of new ones, with an option to extend by 180 days. This is the first data center moratorium fully implemented in Massachusetts. The moratorium targets the Markley Group's 352,000 sq ft facility in Sacred Heart neighborhood.
objective: Impose 360-day moratorium on Markley Group data center expansion

summary: Year-long moratorium enacted, freezing the McLouth Steel site data center proposal. Part of a broader Michigan moratorium wave.
objective: Halt data centers in Gibraltar after Raeden proposal

summary: Scioto Township trustees in Pickaway County enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development after a heated public meeting. Trustees debated duration, ultimately agreeing a full year was necessary to craft legally sound zoning regulations. Concerns centered on rising residential electricity costs, environmental strain from water usage, and noise pollution. Part of a broader wave across Ohio where generous sales tax exemptions made rural communities prime targets.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Scioto Township

summary: The Village of DeForest announced on January 27 that QTS Data Centers' proposed $12 billion data center was 'not feasible.' The project required annexing 1,600 acres from the Town of Vienna, but a state Department of Administration report concluded the annexation was not in the public interest, citing concerns about water and sewer services. Village staff recommended rejection and QTS subsequently withdrew.
objective: Community pressure forces QTS to withdraw in DeForest

summary: Monroe Township Council adopted an amendment to the Hexa Builders Redevelopment Plan adding data centers as permitted use. Residents suspect a proposed 1.6 million sq ft warehouse is actually a data center in disguise. A variance for 1.4 million gallons/day of water was granted. The Pinelands Commission is reviewing the application and can approve or deny the changes, giving residents a key avenue to intervene.
objective: Oppose Hexa Builders data center over water in Monroe Township

summary: Constellation Energy purchased over 600 acres near Essex and is seeking rezoning and annexation. Much of the land was sold by village board members or their relatives. The Essex Coalition launched a petition urging industrial protections and is working to revive the village's dormant zoning board of appeals. Constellation has not officially announced a data center but residents fear one is planned given the site's proximity to power infrastructure.
objective: Block Constellation Energy rezoning of 600 acres in Essex

opposition_groups: Sierra Club Illinois (Heart of Illinois Group)
summary: About two dozen south and southwest suburban residents gathered at a Frankfort forum organized by environmental advocates to voice concerns about data center proposals in Joliet, Minooka, Essex, Coal City, and Yorkville. Residents fear higher utility bills, regional power outages, declining air quality, and strained water supplies from the cluster of proposed facilities in the greater Will County area.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Frankfort

summary: Kosciusko County Area Plan Commission unanimously denied (8-0) Prologis's rezoning request for 554 acres of farmland near Leesburg from agricultural to industrial for a 10-building data center campus. County Commissioners upheld denial 3-0 on April 22. Over 100 residents attended, and a petition collected 1,000+ signatures. Residents cited loss of farmland and impact on the local Amish farming community.
objective: Deny Prologis rezoning of 554 acres of farmland

summary: Washington Township in northwestern Franklin County passed a 90-day moratorium on data centers in unincorporated parts of the township on December 8, 2025. The township also asked the city of Dublin, which the township encompasses, to adopt a moratorium as well. The action follows the broader wave of Central Ohio communities pushing back against the data center boom centered on New Albany.
objective: Impose 90-day data centers moratorium in Washington Township

summary: St. Albans Township held a public hearing on March 10, 2026 on a proposal to become the first township in Licking County to ban data centers. The township sits on the eastern edge of the New Albany data center corridor, home to 40+ data centers in western Licking County. Residents expressed concerns about water and electricity consumption from the nearby facilities. St. Albans Township became the first in Licking County to ban data centers. Public hearing held March 10 with 25 attendees. Trustee Mike Washington read the resolution and opened public comment. A court reporter documented testimony. The ban was approved.
objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in St. Albans Township

summary: Oppidan, an Excelsior-based developer, paused its data center project in Hampton after citing that the state would take too long to permit backup generators. Minnesota's permitting process for diesel backup generators takes 12-18 months. Amazon also abandoned its Becker data center after regulators required a major state permit for 250 diesel generators.
objective: Oppidan pauses Hampton data center over generator permitting delays

summary: Two Creeks, along with Two Rivers and Mishicot, held a joint town board meeting and approved identical resolutions calling on Manitowoc County to adopt a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction, with option to extend 6 months. These three northeast Wisconsin towns acted proactively to prevent data center siting in their communities.
objective: Impose 6 month data centers moratorium in Two Creeks

summary: Two Rivers joined Two Creeks and Mishicot in approving identical resolutions at a joint town board meeting calling for a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction in Manitowoc County, with option for 6-month extension.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Two Rivers

summary: Mishicot joined Two Creeks and Two Rivers in approving identical resolutions at a joint town board meeting calling for a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction in Manitowoc County, with option for 6-month extension.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Mishicot

summary: In Janesville, Viridian Partners' proposal to redevelop the retired GM/JATCO plant into an $8B, 800 MW data center campus will go directly to voters on the November 3, 2026 ballot. The referendum would require voter approval for any development exceeding $450M on the site. A Marquette Law School poll found 70% of Wisconsin voters now say data center costs outweigh benefits (up from 55% in October 2025), with water use the #1 concern (52%).
objective: Put Viridian data center to public vote

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan (EDRA of MI)
summary: South Lyon enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development on February 10, 2026. The city is adjacent to Lyon Township in Oakland County, where a data center has already been approved.
objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in South Lyon

summary: Saginaw enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development, joining the expanding list of Michigan communities implementing temporary pauses while developing local regulations.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Saginaw

summary: Wildcat Ridge is the first of six data center campuses proposed in Archbald and one of at least 12 across Lackawanna County. At a 4-hour public hearing in March 2026, a raucous crowd drowned out testimony from developers. The project would consume up to 3.3 million gallons of water per day and require 1,600 MW of power -- more than the entire Lackawanna Energy Center can produce. If all Archbald proposals proceed, data centers could cover 14% of the borough.
objective: Rally against data center in Archbald

summary: St. Charles, which in August 2025 became the first U.S. city to enact a citywide data center construction ban (one-year moratorium unanimously approved by all 10 council members after the secret 'Project Prism' controversy), is now considering making the ban permanent. The city's Planning & Zoning Commission voted 6-2 on Feb 10 to table the permanent ban for two months. The permanent ban would define data centers in city code but not place them as an allowed use in any zoning district, effectively prohibiting them. A final vote is now expected in April 2026.
objective: Convert St. Charles moratorium into permanent citywide ban

summary: Lowell City Council voted to impose a 360-day moratorium on new data center construction and expansion, following years of noise complaints from neighbors of the 50 MW Markley Group facility (built on the former Prince Macaroni Manufacturing site). Councilor Kim Scott, representing the Sacred Heart neighborhood where the data center operates, initiated the motion to require updated zoning including buffer zones, noise standards, and utility rate protections.
objective: Extend 360-day moratorium on data center construction in Lowell

summary: After Bessemer City Council approved rezoning 700 acres from agricultural to industrial in Nov 2025, the Planning & Zoning Commission recommended rezoning an additional 914 acres. Residents filed a lawsuit challenging the original rezoning as arbitrary spot zoning. A final hearing is scheduled for March 19, 2026. The project would be one of the largest data centers in the world at 4.5 million sq ft across 18 buildings.
objective: Challenge Logistics Land Investments rezoning in Bessemer in court

summary: Despite unanimous opposition from the planning & zoning board and many residents, Forsyth City Council approved conditional rezoning of 1,632 acres for a hyperscale data center campus by Trammell Crow Company. The project would use 1.5 million gallons of water per day -- nearly 40% of the city's permitted daily withdrawal. Nearby Monroe County had already passed its own moratorium and denied a similar project.
objective: Block Trammell Crow Company rezoning of 1632 acres in Forsyth

summary: Forsyth County enacted a strict data center ordinance that prohibits data center cooling systems from using the county water system, effectively barring most hyperscale data center development. The ordinance puts significant guardrails that may effectively eliminate any data center or crypto operation from setting up in the community.
objective: Ban commercial crypto mining in Forsyth County

summary: The City of Covington council imposed a 180-day moratorium on new data center requests on January 5, 2026. During the moratorium, city staff will review current ordinances and make recommendations. Data centers have been seeking to annex into incorporated cities like Covington and Social Circle to bypass Newton County's stricter overlay requirements.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Covington

summary: Newton County Board of Commissioners unanimously voted to enact a moratorium on the acceptance of applications for data center development. The county already restricts data centers to the Stanton Springs overlay, but developers have been circumventing this by seeking annexation into incorporated cities within the county.
objective: Pause data centers in Newton County pending regulations

summary: Griffin City Council unanimously passed a 180-day moratorium on data center permitting, applications, and similar technology facilities including cryptocurrency mining. The moratorium is in place through mid-July 2026 to give city planning staff time to study current codes and develop data center regulations.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers and crypto mining moratorium in Griffin

summary: A 437-acre plot on the outskirts of the 3,000-person town of Hogansville is the center of a data center debate. Residents are concerned about property values, water and energy consumption, and utility bills. City officials signed an NDA about the project at least 18 months before informing the public, angering residents who demanded transparency.
objective: Oppose data center over water concerns in Hogansville

company: Meta (via Beignet LLC / Blue Owl Capital)
summary: Meta's Hyperion data center -- the company's largest -- is under construction in Richland Parish requiring 2,000 MW and 5 million gallons of water per day from groundwater. Earthjustice filed motions against a risky 'Frankenstein financing' deal that could leave ratepayers paying for 3 new gas plants if Meta exits after 4 years. Low-income families were evicted from a trailer park for worker housing. Median home prices rose 80% since early 2024.
objective: Challenge Meta's 2000MW data center groundwater withdrawal

summary: Natelli Investments withdrew its annexation and rezoning applications for the 190-acre New Hill Digital Campus on Shearon Harris Road near Apex on March 5, 2026, following sustained community opposition. The Apex Town Council subsequently debated but did not adopt a proposed one-year moratorium, instead referring the issue to the Environmental Advisory Board.
objective: Community pressure forces Natelli Investments to withdraw in Town of Apex

summary: NorthMark Strategies' $2.8 billion Project Spero data center advanced to second reading via 5-2 vote despite massive public opposition, with hundreds packing council chambers. A Facebook group ('No Data Centers in Spartanburg') gained ~3,000 members and a petition collected ~4,000 signatures. SELC warned the facility could emit harmful air pollution from on-site natural gas power. Previously, TigerDC abandoned a separate $3B proposal after public protests caused council members to switch their votes.
objective: 4000-signature petition opposing NorthMark Strategies data center

summary: SELC filed a lawsuit in January 2026 on behalf of Colleton County residents challenging a Nov 2025 zoning ordinance that added data centers as a 'special exception' in rural zones including the environmentally sensitive ACE Basin. Eagle Rock Partners then proposed an 859-acre, 1,000 MW data center complex under the new ordinance. A bipartisan group of state legislators also drafted a resolution urging the county to oppose the project. Nine data centers total have been proposed in the county.
objective: Legal challenge to Eagle Rock Partners data center in Colleton County

summary: BrightRidge, the local electric utility, placed a moratorium on new data center projects in its service area in May 2025 after community concerns about a potential south Johnson City crypto mine/data center site. Johnson City Commission is considering a $96,880 noise and vibration study to shape data center regulations before its heavy industrial moratorium expires June 4. A Limestone data center (7% of BrightRidge sales = 9,800 household equivalents) is set for closure in March 2026.
objective: Pause data centers and crypto mining in Johnson City pending regulations

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: On August 7, 2025, the Prince William County Circuit Court invalidated the Digital Gateway rezoning approvals, declaring the ordinances 'void ab initio' due to failure to comply with notice requirements. The Court of Appeals held oral arguments on February 25, 2026. PWC has spent an additional $400K on defense. A written decision is pending.
objective: Challenge QTS Realty Trust / Compass rezoning in Prince William in court

summary: Stafford County has three data center projects under construction totaling over 7 million sq ft. The nonprofit Protect Stafford formed after the county received a large number of applications to rezone residential/agricultural land to industrial uses. The group's president warns Stafford could become the next Data Center Alley, saying officials were unprepared for the volume of applications.
objective: Organize coalition against data center in Stafford County

summary: The Meade County Fiscal Court voted to deny rezoning of 138 acres near Brandenburg from agricultural/residential to light industrial for a proposed data center by PowerHouse Industries. The Planning and Zoning Commission had unanimously voted against the rezoning. Some 150 speakers attended a special commission meeting to voice concerns about water and electricity usage, environmental impact, and the rushed planning process.
objective: Deny PowerHouse Industries rezoning of 138 acres of farmland

summary: xAI operated 27 gas turbines at its Southaven facility without air permits, violating the Clean Air Act according to the NAACP, which threatened a federal lawsuit. At a 2.5-hour public hearing on Feb 17, 2026, not a single person spoke in favor of xAI's proposed permanent air permit for 40+ gas turbines. Residents reported respiratory problems and headache-inducing 24/7 noise. A local group 'Safe and Sound' documents decibel levels. Despite opposition, MDEQ approved the air permit on March 11, 2026.
objective: Challenge xAI (MZX Tech) air quality permits in court

summary: Developers withdrew a Maricopa County application to rezone 529 acres for an AI data center campus near Goodyear and Buckeye after grassroots and municipal opposition. Both cities formally opposed, citing water scarcity and incompatibility with residential neighborhoods.
objective: Developers withdraw 529-acre data center rezoning near Goodyear

summary: Pima County proposed zoning code amendment requiring data centers to obtain Conditional Use Permits instead of by-right on industrially zoned land. Would mandate staff review, hearings, and public comment. Driven by backlash from Project Blue approvals.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Pima County

summary: Quincy hosts 53 data centers from 11 providers, consuming 45% of Grant PUD's average load (~800 MW) and paying 75% of the city's property tax revenue. Former Mayor Patty Martin warned about long-term impacts. NPR/OPB reported concerns about hydropower sustainability as snowpack declines.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Microsoft in Quincy

summary: Gov. Ferguson signed executive order forming workgroup to evaluate data centers' impact on energy, taxes, and jobs. Preliminary report delivered Dec 2025. ProPublica investigation found Washington gives data centers some of the state's largest corporate tax breaks while they threaten clean energy goals.
