objective: Form state workgroup on data center policy in WA

summary: Oregon CUB accused PGE of circumventing the POWER Act. PGE built $174M in substations exclusively for data centers but proposed assigning 47% of costs to residential customers. CUB analysis shows PGE would allocate 34-45% of new supply/transmission costs to households despite data centers driving demand. PUC decision expected April 2026.
objective: Oppose transmission lines for Portland General Electric data center

summary: HR 655 would transfer ~150 acres of Mt. Hood National Forest to The Dalles to triple reservoir capacity. Passed House Dec 9, 2025; before Senate Energy Committee. Conservation coalition calls it a 'cynical sweetheart deal' to serve Google's growing water demands. City denies it's for Google but water plan references 'large industrial user' needing ~1M gallons/day.
objective: Cap data center water consumption in Oregon

summary: City Council established a 180-day moratorium on data centers in all zoning districts. First moratorium for the Illinois Valley area. Mayor cited need for guardrails before entertaining mega data center proposals.
objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Ottawa

summary: The City of Forney is considering new zoning rules that would formally regulate data center development within city limits. A public hearing is scheduled for March 17, 2026 on a proposed ordinance amending Forney's Comprehensive Zoning Ordinance. The amendment would include a formal definition of a data center, establish specific development regulations, and require a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) in several zoning districts. Operational requirements include full enclosure of cooling equipment, generators, and other infrastructure. The Planning and Zoning Commission has already reviewed and recommended approval.
objective: Require conditional use permits in Forney

summary: Center for Coalfield Justice led protest as county commissioners held invite-only meeting with data center developers. Next Generation Land Company proposed 150,000 sq ft facility in rural West Finley. Protesters cited impacts on 23 dairy farms.
objective: Rally against Next Generation Land Company data center in West Finley Township

summary: Planning Commission voted 5-3 to deny a 300 MW power substation behind residential developments in Bristow, intended to power data center campus. NOVEC has 10 days to appeal.
objective: Deny NOVEC 300MW data center permit in Prince William

summary: ~200 people packed Beloit Public Library to oppose a possible data center on a working gravel pit. Cambrin LLC (linked to Meta) signed a pre-development agreement with the town in July 2025. 'No Beloit Data Center' Facebook group was created.
objective: Oppose Cambrin (Meta) data center in Town of Beloit

summary: Clean Water for North Carolina and Walnut Cove residents filed lawsuit challenging the 3-2 rezoning vote from January 12, alleging commissioners did not adequately consider environmental impacts on 1,800-acre Project Delta site.
objective: Sue for environmental review in Stokes County

summary: Residents and Center for Coalfield Justice members protested in downtown Washington, PA on March 12, chanting 'Our land, our power, our land, our water' as county commissioners met with data center developers in an invite-only meeting. Next Generation Land Company proposed a 150,000 sq ft data center on 147 acres in the rural community of West Finley. Protesters cited impacts on 23 dairy farms in the county, including water consumption and 24/7 noise affecting livestock.
objective: Rally against Next Generation Land Company data center in Washington County

summary: Brazoria County commissioners unanimously rejected a package of tax abatements sought by Nightpeak Energy for its proposed $3.5 billion Old Ocean Data Center campus outside Sweeny, about 65 miles southwest of Houston. The project calls for two 310-megawatt hyperscale data centers paired with two 310-megawatt combustion turbine power plants on a 158-acre site. Each data center carries a projected price tag of roughly $1.5 billion, while the power plants would cost about $250 million apiece. Commissioners declined the incentives after residents and local officials raised concerns about noise, water consumption and power demand. Local opposition partly stems from residents' experiences with another data center in nearby Brazoria that has disrupted the lives of some constituents.
objective: Deny Nightpeak Energy data center on 158 acres in Brazoria County

summary: The Texas Public Utility Commission is implementing SB 6 through multiple rulemaking dockets. PUCT Chair Thomas Gleeson proposed rules to pare back proposed requirements for data centers seeking to connect to ERCOT, lowering fees for large loads and adding alternative standards developers can use to demonstrate site readiness. Over 220 GW of large load interconnection requests have been filed with ERCOT (nearly quadrupled from 56 GW in Sept. 2024), with 70%+ being data centers. This exceeds twice ERCOT's peak summer demand of ~85 GW. Industry experts warn much of this is 'phantom load' that may never materialize, complicating grid planning. The State Republican Executive Committee passed a near-unanimous resolution calling for 'rigorous independent assessments' and a pause on open-loop water-cooled data centers.
objective: Implement SB 6 data center grid rules via PUCT rulemaking

summary: The Fredericksburg Planning Commission voted unanimously (7-0) in three separate votes to recommend disapproval of Penzance Development's proposed four-building data center campus at 1500 Gateway Boulevard near I-95, finding the proposal was not in compliance with the city's Comprehensive Plan. The campus would have totaled 2.1 million sq ft with ~600 MW power demand on 84 acres. Key issues included proposed transmission lines running through a car line and play area. The project was expected to generate up to $50 million in annual tax revenue and create 240 jobs. Full build-out was projected for 2032.
objective: Deny Penzance Development 600MW data center permit in Fredericksburg

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: The Prince William Board of County Supervisors voted 4-3 to approve a 571,000-square-foot data center on a 40-acre site along Prince William Parkway near Hornbaker Road. Simultaneously, the board voted 6-0 to initiate a zoning text amendment that would eliminate by-right data center development in the overlay district going forward.
objective: Approve Prince William data center while ending by-right approvals

summary: The Danville-Pittsylvania Regional Industrial Facilities Authority unanimously approved the sale of 2,990 acres at the 3,528-acre Berry Hill Megasite to SAC III Acquisition Co., an affiliate of Stack Infrastructure, for $238,000 per acre. The project is expected to create 2,050 new jobs (avg salary $80,500) and $73 billion in investment over 30 years — the largest project in Southside Virginia's history. First phase closing is expected by June 2027. In January 2026, Pittsylvania County created a new heavy industry zoning district (M-SVMS) providing by-right zoning for data centers at the megasite. Local officials have urged the state to preserve data center tax incentives, warning that ending them would eliminate rural Virginia's opportunity to benefit from the industry.
objective: Oppose Stack Infrastructure data center over environmental harm

summary: Virginia's Democratic-controlled legislature missed a critical budget deadline as lawmakers remain deadlocked over data center tax exemptions. The sales tax exemption cost the Commonwealth $1.6 billion in forgone revenue in FY2025 — up from the $1.5 million originally estimated in 2008. The Senate proposes accelerating the sunset from 2035 to January 1, 2027. The House wants to preserve the 2035 sunset but attach clean energy requirements. Gov. Spanberger sides with the House position. The IBEW has lobbied to protect data centers. Meanwhile, SB 253 would allow the SCC to shift electricity distribution and capacity auction costs from residential customers to data centers demanding 25+ MW, which would reduce residential bills by about $5.50/month.
objective: Reduce data center tax exemptions via SB 253

company: Valley Link (Dominion Energy / FirstEnergy / Transource Energy)
opposition_groups: Stop MARL Virginia / Stop Valley Link Virginia
summary: Valley Link — a joint venture of Dominion Energy, FirstEnergy Transmission, and Transource Energy — plans a $1 billion, 115-mile, 765-kilovolt transmission line from Campbell County (Joshua Falls substation) to Culpeper County. The SCC filing is planned for approximately September 2026. Community meetings are underway, and opposition groups (Stop MARL VA, Block Valley Link) are organizing, with significant pushback from Fluvanna County in March 2026.
objective: Rally against Valley Link (Dominion Energy data center in Central Virginia

summary: Dominion Energy's proposed 944-MW gas peaker plant — the Chesterfield Energy Reliability Center — would be built on the site of a former coal plant retired in 2018, near the Dutch Gap conservation area. The SCC reaffirmed its approval of the project, declining to reconsider. SELC has pivoted to an air permit appeal in Richmond Circuit Court under the Environmental Justice Act, arguing the facility would disproportionately affect nearby communities.
objective: Oppose Dominion Energy data center as environmental injustice

summary: Virginia's DEQ proposed guidance expanding the definition of 'emergency' to include planned utility outages, allowing data centers to run dirtier Tier II diesel backup generators more frequently. Over 500 public comments were submitted, overwhelmingly opposed. VCU research found data center diesel generator emissions in Northern Virginia increased dramatically from 2015-2023: CO up 196%, NOx up 111%, particulate matter up 139%. In a worst-case scenario, backup generators could release 9,000 tons of NOx annually — equal to half of all typical Northern Virginia emissions. Del. John McAuliff (D-Loudoun) introduced legislation requiring data centers to use battery storage first, limiting Tier IV generators to emergencies, and banning Tier II generators entirely. The 2026 legislative session is also considering bills from Sen. Creigh Deeds and Del. Rip Sullivan to limit diesel generator use at data centers.
objective: Ban diesel backup generators at data centers in VA

summary: Residents of Loudoun Valley Estates in Ashburn fought a proposed 500-kilovolt high-voltage transmission line connecting Golden and Mars substations, part of a reliability loop to bring more power to data centers on Ashburn's eastern side. Over 1,000 written statements were submitted to the SCC and 500+ people registered to speak. The 165-foot lines require wide clearances, raising health concerns for nearby residents. The Loudoun County School Board voted against routes that would affect school zones. The evidentiary hearing ran January 8-15, 2026. On February 19, 2026, the Virginia Supreme Court affirmed the SCC ruling, allowing the project to proceed as planned. The controversy has pushed Loudoun County leaders to look at moving away from further data center growth.
objective: Oppose transmission lines for Dominion Energy data center

bill_name: SB 978 - Large-Scale Energy Facilities: Labor: Electricity Rates
summary: Establishes regulations for large-scale energy users (75+ MW facilities). Requires the CPUC to create special rate structures ensuring these users cover infrastructure upgrade costs and cannot shift expenses to other customers. Mandates prevailing wage construction standards.
objective: Create special rate structure for 75MW+ energy users (SB 978)

summary: Removes CEQA exemptions for data centers. Allows gubernatorial certification as 'environmental leadership' projects only if they pay full interconnection costs, achieve 100% zero-carbon electricity within 5 years, and implement water-efficient cooling.
objective: Regulate data center development in California

bill_name: SB 1168 - Data Centers: Natural Gas and Electricity Surcharges
summary: Imposes surcharges on natural gas and electricity consumed by data centers (20+ MW). Revenue funds low-income rate assistance programs (CARE/FERA).
objective: Impose energy surcharges on data centers to fund rate relief

summary: Comprehensive water reporting requirements with three data center size categories (hyperscale >25 MW, mid-range 2-25 MW, small <2 MW). Requires water consumption disclosure before business license issuance. DWR/CEC must develop best practices by January 2029.
objective: Mandate data center water usage disclosure in CA

summary: The Provo City Council voted unanimously (7-0) on March 11, 2026 to deny a zoning change for a proposed AI data center at 1507 S. 180 East in the East Bay area. Developer B+F Timpanogos Tech had requested to have property rezoned to an industrial commercial data center overlay zone for a 66,000 sq ft, 30 MW facility on 9 acres. Council members said the city needs to complete its economic development study before making long-term land-use decisions of this nature. Lingering concerns about power consumption and community impact drove the unanimous rejection.
objective: Deny B+F Timpanogos Tech rezoning of 9 acres in City of Provo

summary: The Minnesota House Elections Finance and Government Operations Committee approved a bill on March 12, 2026 that would largely prohibit municipalities from entering into non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) with data center developers except where required by law. Testimony centered on data center proposals in Farmington, Hermantown, and Pine Island where county officials signed NDAs preventing them from discussing project details, including a proposed Google data center in Hermantown. The bill addresses transparency concerns raised by communities learning about massive projects only after key decisions were already made behind closed doors. Separately, Sen. Jennifer McEwen (DFL-Duluth) announced she will introduce a statewide moratorium bill (SF 4298) barring permits until one year after the PUC completes a comprehensive data center study.
objective: Ban data center development NDAs via SF 4298

summary: The Alabama Senate passed SB270 on March 13, 2026, sponsored by Sen. Lance Bell (R-Pell City). The bill requires the Public Service Commission to review contracts between utilities and large-scale data centers (150+ MW) to ensure they are 'in the public interest.' Key provisions: (1) incremental infrastructure costs must be recovered from the data center, not existing ratepayers; (2) contracts must provide positive benefits to all other retail electric customers; (3) PSC must evaluate whether pricing terms could lower costs for other utility customers. The bill now advances to Alabama House committees.
objective: Shield ratepayers from data center costs via SB270

summary: Governor Josh Shapiro announced GRID (Governor's Responsible Infrastructure Development) standards for data centers, offering expedited 'Fast Track' permitting in exchange for commitments to water conservation, local hiring, and independent power generation. Two Amazon data centers in Salem Township and Falls Township are the first projects in the program. Separately, the PA House Energy Committee advanced two bills along party lines on March 9: one requiring annual water and energy use reporting, another creating model zoning guidelines for municipalities. Environmental advocates expressed concern about the voluntary nature of GRID and the lack of enforcement mechanisms without legislative cooperation from the GOP-controlled Senate.
objective: Launch GRID fast-track permitting standards in Pennsylvania

summary: A bipartisan bill banning non-disclosure agreements between municipalities and data center developers cleared the Wisconsin Senate committee with bipartisan support. Authored by Sen. Andre Jacque, the bill reflects growing anger over secretive data center deals in communities like Beloit, Port Washington, and other Wisconsin towns where residents learned of massive projects only after NDAs prevented elected officials from discussing them publicly.
objective: Advance data center NDA ban through Wisconsin Senate committee

summary: Independence City Council approved $6.26 billion in tax breaks over 20 years for Nebius on March 3, 2026, including a 98% real property tax abatement, 90% personal property tax abatement, and sales tax exemptions. On March 5, the city rejected a referendum petition citing charter Section 7.2 (excluding referendums on immediately-effective ordinances). On March 12, three residents (Gonzalez, Vaughn, Magers) filed a lawsuit in Jackson County court to force the City Clerk to certify the petition and allow a public vote, potentially by August 2026. The 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' Facebook group has ~3,000 members. Good Jobs First published an open letter criticizing the deal. No court ruling as of March 17.
opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence
objective: Challenge $6.26B in Nebius tax breaks approved by Independence

summary: Prometheus Hyperscale is developing a 1.2-gigawatt data center near Evanston on the Wyoming-Utah border, courting both Meta and Microsoft as potential tenants. Uinta County commissioners approved a letter of intent for a 'workforce hub' providing temporary housing for up to 3,000 construction workers on 997 acres of county-owned property adjacent to Bear River State Park. Environmental groups have opposed the project citing enormous energy and water consumption. Over 200 environmental organizations nationally have cited such projects as needing better regulations before approval.
objective: Oppose Prometheus Hyperscale data center over water in Evanston

summary: Wyoming faces 12.8 GW of announced data center projects that would more than double statewide electrical demand, sparking fears of soaring residential electric rates. While the state has an existing ratepayer protection law (statute 37-3-116 from 2019) preventing new large load customers from obligating existing customers for utility costs, the Wyoming Outdoor Council has published a white paper calling for additional legislation requiring data center developers to pay for grid infrastructure, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and be accountable to the public. Lawmakers are considering additional regulatory measures for the upcoming budget session.
objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in WY

summary: Sarpy County Board of Commissioners formally opposed state data center tax incentive legislation (LB209), testifying that the county loses $5.8 million in personal property tax revenue annually from existing data center tax incentives. Research shows a net fiscal loss of $10.02 million per year during the incentive period. Meta is expanding its Sarpy campus to nine buildings across 4 million sq ft on 500+ acres in Springfield. A statewide tax incentive audit found $1.2 billion in lost revenue across incentive programs over four fiscal years.
objective: Oppose tax incentives for Meta/Google in Sarpy County

summary: Residents near Meta's existing data center on West Ridge Drive in Madison County report ongoing noise issues from generators and alarms, with one resident stating alarms sometimes run for 12 hours. Mayor Tommy Battle has publicly expressed wariness about opening a second data center in Huntsville, noting data centers require seven times more energy than typical facilities but only employ about 180 workers. Battle has not written off a new center but wants to ensure the tradeoff is worthwhile, signaling a shift from the state's previous pro-development stance.
objective: Oppose Meta data center over noise in Huntsville

summary: Fayetteville City Council voted in early March 2026 to remove data centers as a permitted use from ALL zoning categories within city limits. A pending application by CHI Acquisitions LP for a 300,000 sq ft facility on 37 acres was denied by the Planning and Zoning Commission. CHI appealed; the City Council will make a final decision on March 19. The CHI Acquisitions LP appeal hearing was scheduled for March 19 at 6 PM at City Hall. CHI's application predates the zoning ban so it remains eligible for consideration. CHI Acquisitions/Crow Holdings withdrew their appeal on March 18, one day before the council vote. Councilman Emmett Spurlock called it "the power of the people."
objective: Ban data centers in all zones in Fayetteville

summary: Prologis is seeking to rezone 312 acres on 32 Mile Road for a Microsoft data center. The Planning Commission has postponed the rezoning decision four times (Jan -> Feb -> April -> June 11) as the township works on zoning ordinance amendments. Hundreds of residents have packed multiple hearings in opposition, citing water, noise, traffic, and loss of rural character.
objective: Block Prologis rezoning of 312 acres in Washington Township

summary: Residents and the Center for Coalfield Justice protested on March 12 as County Commissioners entered an invite-only meeting with data center developers, chanting 'Our land, our power, our land, our water.' Part of a broader PA movement where 50+ data center plans face growing opposition.
objective: Protest secretive meeting between officials and data center developers

summary: AWS purchased 346 acres for $178M and plans a 2.5M sq ft hyperscale campus with nine two-story data center buildings on Lofty Road. Residents are pushing back at community meetings, demanding more details about environmental and infrastructure impacts. AWS argues the development fits within existing industrial zoning.
objective: Oppose Amazon Web Services (AWS) data center in Kline Township

summary: Olyphant is drafting zoning regulations for data centers in advance of a developer's interest in building 12 data centers at Triboro Industrial Park. A public hearing is scheduled March 31 at the Municipal Building. The draft ordinance requires developers to submit sound, environmental, and community impact analyses before any hearing. Council could vote on the ordinance April 14.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Olyphant Borough

summary: Canadian company Bitfarms Ltd. proposes a 350MW high-performance computing facility near the Panther Creek Power Plant, initially used for Bitcoin mining. Goal to start construction mid-2026. Unlike many PA locations, Nesquehoning (in the economically distressed Panther Valley area) has shown some local support and has already issued permits.
objective: Oppose Bitfarms data center in Nesquehoning Borough

company: QTS Data Centers / Amazon Web Services
summary: QTS has spent $580M acquiring dozens of properties in Salem Township for a data center campus, situated west of Amazon's planned campus near Talen Energy's nuclear plant. Amazon is planning 15+ buildings and signed an $18B deal with Talen Energy for 1.92GW of nuclear power through 2042. A petition with ~3,000 signatures (2x the township population) opposes the projects.
objective: 3000-signature petition opposing QTS Data Centers data center

jurisdiction: Sugarloaf Township / Hazle Twp / Black Creek Twp / Nescopeck Twp
summary: Alliance to Stop the Line opposes PPL's proposed 12-mile 500kV transmission line through four Luzerne County townships to support data center growth. Poles would be 100-240 ft tall. Luzerne County Council voted 10-1 for a nonbinding resolution urging PPL to consider alternatives, but PPL plans to submit to PA Utility Commission regardless. Town hall held Feb 24 at Sugarloaf Fire Company.
objective: Oppose transmission lines for PPL Electric Utilities data center

summary: Londonderry Township tabled a zoning ordinance that would create a data center overlay district after overwhelming resident opposition at a packed public hearing. Proposed limits include 30-acre minimum lots and 150-ft residential buffers, but residents say restrictions don't go far enough. Concerns about noise, environmental impact, and rural character. No formal data center application yet.
objective: Create data center overlay zone in Londonderry Township

summary: Scranton City Council introducing an amendment to the 2023 zoning ordinance to regulate data centers, following advice from Lackawanna County planners that municipalities should address data centers proactively. The amendment was expected to be introduced March 17, 2026.
objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in City of Scranton

summary: Houston-based Cloverleaf Infrastructure is proposing 'Project Cherry Blossom,' a $1B+ data center on roughly 200 acres of the former River Raisin Golf Course near I-75 and North Dixie Highway. Dozens of residents protested in January 2026. Protect Frenchtown launched a petition drive to collect 1,200 signatures to place a zoning challenge on the November ballot.
objective: 1200-signature petition opposing Cloverleaf Infrastructure data center

summary: Southfield City Council voted 5-2 on Dec. 15, 2025 to approve a $500M, 100MW data center by LA-based Metrobloks on 13 acres near Inkster Road and I-696, despite nearly six hours of resident opposition. Rep. Rashida Tlaib issued a statement opposing the project. Developer still needs to secure full funding within 18 months.
objective: Oppose Metrobloks data center in Southfield

summary: Wilmington City Council voted 5-2 on Feb 20 to rezone 545 acres from rural residential to light industrial for a second data center campus by Ardent Technologies (affiliated with TAC Data Centers), a $6 billion project promising 125-150 jobs. This is in addition to the existing 471-acre AWS campus, bringing total data center-zoned land in Wilmington to ~1,000 acres.
objective: Block Ardent Technologies rezoning of 545 acres in Wilmington

summary: Brown County-based group launched a petition for an Ohio constitutional amendment to ban data centers over 25 megawatts statewide, collecting ~1,800 signatures in eight days. Petition submitted to AG Dave Yost's office. Would need ~413,000 signatures by July for November ballot. Originated from backlash over a secretive 1,000-acre mega site in Mt. Orab.
opposition_groups: Mt. Orab Residents for Responsible Development
objective: 1800-signature petition opposing data center in Brown County

summary: Aligned Data Centers is proposing 11 buildings on 300 acres near Reed Road/Carbon Hill Road. An open house was held at Coal City High School in February 2026. Part of the Will/Grundy County data center cluster alongside Joliet, Minooka, and Essex.
objective: Oppose Aligned Data Centers data center in Coal City

summary: Equinix approved for a $2B, 340-acre, 700MW campus requiring 3M gallons/day of water — roughly 30% of the municipality's Lake Michigan pipeline allocation. Already approved but concerns persist about water and energy impacts.
objective: Restrict Equinix 340-acre data center in Minooka

summary: Hut 8 Corp. proposed a $4-5B, 500MW data center on 200 acres near Latham. Public outcry forced a withdrawal of the rezoning request in Dec 2025. The company rescheduled as a conditional permitted use; county board vote scheduled for April 21, 2026.
objective: Block Hut 8 rezoning of 200 acres in Logan County

company: Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, CoreWeave, Digital Realty, Equinix
summary: Sens. Warren, Van Hollen, and Blumenthal opened an investigation into Big Tech data centers driving up consumer electricity costs, sending letters to Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, CoreWeave, Digital Realty, and Equinix. Companies responded but refused to disclose contracts with utility companies or actual rates paid. Senators noted some regions saw electricity costs rise 267% over five years.
objective: Oppose Google data center impact on rates

summary: EPA launched Clean Air Act Resources hub for data centers and redefined 'begin actual construction' under New Source Review, allowing developers to build foundations, structural steel, and walls before receiving air permits. Final rule expected by September 2026. Also prioritized data center chemical reviews under TSCA.
objective: Weaken Clean Air Act construction rules for data centers

summary: Army Corps finalized 2026 Nationwide Permits explicitly expanding NWP 39 to include data centers, creating a fast-track pathway for construction affecting wetlands and waterways without project-specific environmental review. Effective March 15, 2026 through March 15, 2031. NRDC criticized the permits as endangering streams and wetlands.
objective: Expand Army Corps fast-track permits for data center wetlands

summary: Food & Water Watch released 'The Urgent Case Against Data Centers,' the first comprehensive national report making the case for a nationwide moratorium. Key findings: by 2028, data centers could consume 720 billion gallons of water annually and 580 TWh of energy (12% of national demand). Virginia data centers created just 1 permanent job per $54M invested. 230+ organizations support the moratorium call.
objective: National report calling for data center moratorium

summary: Sierra Club released its 2026 state policy framework with four guardrails: ratepayer protections preventing cross-subsidization, clean energy requirements, water efficiency standards, and community engagement mandates. Active campaigns in MI, GA, NJ, MA, CO, TN, WI. Called the White House ratepayer pledge 'baby steps.'
objective: Sierra Club proposes state policy guardrails for data centers

summary: Data Center Watch reported $98B in data center projects blocked or delayed by grassroots opposition in Q2 2025 alone. Identified 142 active advocacy organizations across 28 states. Opposition is bipartisan: Republicans cite tax incentive concerns; Democrats focus on environmental impacts. Total tracked disruptions reached $64B ($18B blocked, $46B delayed).
objective: Oppose data center tax incentives in National

summary: Big Tech is conducting an unprecedented PR and lobbying campaign. Meta running multimillion-dollar TV ads in DC and 9 state capitals. Data Center Coalition nearly tripled lobbying spend. NAIA (National AI Association, est. Jan 2025) organizing Congressional field trips. Only 28% of voters support data centers near their homes (Heatmap poll). At least 16 local governments placed bans in 2026 alone.
objective: Organize coalition against data center in National

summary: Lee County residents packed a commissioners meeting on March 16 demanding a moratorium on data centers. Deep River Data plans to power an AI data center with fracked natural gas from Butler Well #3 — which would be a first for North Carolina. The gas well is approximately 2,700 feet deep with fractures known to travel up to 2,000 feet, raising groundwater contamination concerns. SELC attorney Brooks Rainey urged commissioners to adopt a moratorium.
objective: Rally against Deep River Data data center in Lee County

summary: QTS filed a Developments of Regional Impact application for a 12 million sq ft data center campus on the outskirts of Blakely, the 'Peanut Capital of the World.' Phase 1 is $290M with construction starting July 2026. At a January Rotary Club meeting, QTS could not answer key questions about start dates, job numbers, or water usage. Blakely previously adopted 30+ restrictions on data centers in November 2025.
objective: Oppose QTS Technology Services data center over water in Blakely

summary: Stokes County voters resoundingly rejected both incumbent commissioners in the March 3 Republican primary, less than two months after the board's controversial 3-2 vote to approve rezoning for the Project Delta data center. Commissioner Rick Morris, who voted in favor, came in last place in the eight-way contest. The results signal strong voter backlash against the data center approval.
objective: Stokes County voters oust pro-data-center commissioners in primary

summary: 'Move the Gas Plant' grassroots group fighting a 1,200 MW gas plant by Sandow Lakes Energy that would serve nearby data centers and Bitcoin mining. TCEQ denied their hearing request in approximately 45 seconds and issued the air pollution permit authorizing 460 tons/year of ammonia, 153 tons of soot, 76 tons of sulfuric acid. Texas Energy Fund also denied Sandow Lakes' $295M loan application.
objective: Deny Sandow Lakes Energy Co. 1200MW data center permit in Lee County

summary: A $13 billion plan to build Texas' first 765kV transmission line through the Hill Country to serve Permian Basin data center demand. Coalition of landowners, conservation advocates, and lawmakers mobilizing across Burnet, Llano, and other Hill Country counties. At final stages of administrative review; construction could start 2027.
objective: Oppose new transmission lines for data centers in Permian Basin /

summary: The State Republican Executive Committee passed a near-unanimous resolution calling for 'rigorous independent assessments' of data center impacts on the grid and water supply before approval, and a pause on 'open loop' (high water use) data centers. Significant because it reflects growing intra-GOP opposition to data center development in Texas.
objective: Republican party resolution opposing data center impacts in TX

summary: Amazon Data Services purchased GWU's 122-acre Ashburn campus for $427M. Loudoun County officials were 'blindsided' and are vowing to fight the data center conversion. Under new zoning rules, data centers require special exception review rather than by-right approval.
objective: Oppose Amazon Data Services data center in Loudoun County

summary: Following Goochland's lead, New Kent County is proposing a 1,800-acre Technology Overlay District along the Route 33 corridor. Controversial fast-track process would skip standard public hearings. Public meetings planned for late March/April 2026. No actual data center application has been filed yet — this is a proactive regulatory framework.
objective: Create data center overlay zone in New Kent County

summary: First state law mandating Tier IV emission standards for data center backup generators after July 2026. Passed both chambers of the Virginia General Assembly; awaiting Governor Spanberger's signature. Sponsored by Del. John McAuliff (D-Loudoun).
objective: Mandate Tier IV emission standards for backup generators (HB 507)

bill_name: SB 94 / HB 153 - Data Center Siting Requirements
summary: Requires 100+ MW data center facilities to submit site assessments examining impacts on agriculture, parks, historic sites, and noise near homes/schools within 500 feet. Conference committee deadlocked over Senate's industrial-zone-only requirement.
objective: Require site impact assessments for 100MW+ data centers (SB 94)

summary: First water transparency requirement for Virginia data centers. Requires water utilities to report aggregate data center water usage. Cleared both chambers; in conference committee or awaiting governor's action.
objective: Mandate data center water usage disclosure in VA

summary: Franklin County Planning & Zoning Commission is considering county-wide data center regulations including 1,000-ft setbacks from residential/parks/agriculture, max 800-acre site limit, 10% open space requirement, and sound limits of 60dB daytime/50dB nighttime. On the same agenda as two major rezoning requests (Gateway Digital and Beltline Energy). The March 17 P&Z meeting lasted nearly 11 hours (6 p.m. to 4:39 a.m.) with 850+ attendees and ~174 speakers, overwhelmingly opposed. No votes were taken — all items tabled to April.
objective: Block rezoning of 800 acres in Franklin County

summary: Meade County adopted Ordinance 2025-07 on January 13, 2026, establishing a moratorium on data center development. This followed the Fiscal Court's vote to deny rezoning of 138 acres for a PowerHouse Industries data center in October 2025.
objective: Halt data centers in Meade County after PowerHouse Industries proposal

summary: TVA is reversing coal plant closures and investing in new natural gas generation to meet surging data center electricity demand across its 7-state service territory. Critics warn this threatens TVA's clean energy commitments and could raise rates for the 10 million people it serves. TVA pledged rate fairness but has not detailed how data center costs will be allocated.
objective: Challenge TVA plans to power data centers in Tennessee Valley

bill_name: HB 2631 - Data Center Tax Incentive Repeal
summary: Governor Katie Hobbs called for repealing data center tax incentives which cost Arizona approximately $38.5 million per year. The bill would end the transaction privilege and use tax exemption for large data centers. Part of a broader national trend of states reconsidering data center tax breaks.
objective: Repeal Arizona data center tax incentives via HB 2631

summary: Monterey Park City Council voted unanimously to place a permanent data center ban on the June 2, 2026 ballot. If approved by voters, it would permanently ban all data centers citywide — an escalation from the 45-day moratorium passed in January 2026. The community collected 5,779 petition signatures.
opposition_groups: No Data Center in Monterey Park
objective: Ban data centers in all zones in Monterey Park

summary: Commissioner James R. Kern III distributed an advisory model data center siting ordinance to all Warren County municipalities, providing a planning tool for communities facing data center proposals. Follows Phillipsburg's ban in the same county.
objective: Distribute model data center ordinance in Warren County

summary: Would prohibit developers from entering NDAs with municipalities that conceal data center project details. Part of a wave of NJ transparency legislation alongside S680 (BYOP) and S3379 (reporting).
objective: Ban data center development NDAs in NJ

summary: JRE Strategies withdrew its application to rezone 7 acres on Third Avenue for a 10 MW data center before the March 16 city commission vote. The planning commission had previously voted 7-0 against. Neighbors and the Noble Foundation opposed.
objective: Deny JRE Strategies rezoning of 6.74 acres in Ardmore

summary: Application IC-SE-01-2026 for a data center in Irwinville was withdrawn by the applicant as of the March 2 Irwin County Commission meeting.
objective: Community pressure forces withdrawal in Irwinville

summary: Logan County adopted new data center regulations on Feb 18, lifting its moratorium. Granite Renewables' $15B, 2GW project on 4,198 acres can proceed under new rules with setbacks, noise limits, and water use requirements.
objective: Restrict Granite Renewables 4198-acre data center in Logan County

summary: 20-30 year sales tax exemptions for data centers investing $250M+. Estimated $29M/year revenue loss. Environmental coalition opposed.
objective: Regulate data center development in Colorado

summary: Prevents data centers (20+ MW) from raising utility rates for existing customers. Requires PUC approval before utilities can serve new large loads.
objective: Prevent data center cost pass-through to residential rates in ID

summary: Farmer lawsuit challenging the 620-acre Gemstone Technology Park rezone approved in a 3-2 tie-breaking vote. Plaintiff alleges council member conflict of interest.
objective: Challenge TIF financing for Diode Ventures data center

summary: Eleven data center developers in talks with NorthWestern Energy, projecting 1,400 MW total demand by 2030 — more than double Montana's current peak load. A 7-organization coalition is organizing statewide opposition.
objective: Organize coalition against data center in Montana

summary: Passed both chambers; requires new data centers to report water use with $10K/day fines. Awaiting Governor Cox's signature by March 26.
objective: Mandate data center water use reports with $10K/day fines (HB 76)

summary: $11B, 1,000 MW data center on 505 acres rezoned from agriculture. Lyon County Commission approved 4-1 despite 13-4 public opposition. Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe raised water and cultural concerns.
objective: Block Monarch Data Center rezoning of 505 acres in Lyon County

summary: 5,200+ public comments on Project Jupiter air quality permits. Potential 14M tons CO2/year. Two lawsuits pending. State decision due April 22. Shaping 2026 county commissioner elections.
objective: Voters reject officials who approved data center in Dona Ana County

summary: East Lansing City Council voted unanimously on March 17 for a 6-month moratorium on data center development while evaluating zoning ordinances.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in East Lansing

summary: Detroit City Council approved a resolution on March 18 asking Mayor Sheffield to not issue any data center permits for two years while the city studies impacts.
objective: Pause data centers in Detroit pending regulations

company: Sheridan Data Center LLC (Clean Cloud Energy)
summary: Grant County Quorum Court passed a resolution allowing officials to negotiate a PILOT agreement with Sheridan Data Center LLC (operating under Clean Cloud Energy) for a proposed data and solar center just outside Sheridan. Residents protested at the courthouse, citing water depletion concerns (county relies on underground water only), lack of company transparency, and project scale. A resident sued the county for FOIA violations. The Facebook opposition group has over 1,700 members.
objective: Rally against Sheridan Data Center data center in Grant County

company: Crosland Southeast / Crow Holdings / Engineered Land Solutions
opposition_groups: Save Matthews - NO AI Data Center
summary: Developers proposed a 123-acre data center campus (Project Accelerate) near I-485 and East John Street in Matthews, Mecklenburg County. After a contentious community open house and a Change.org petition gathering over 2,100 signatures, the developer withdrew the rezoning application on October 8, 2025. Under local rules, the developer can resubmit after 6 months (April 2026).
objective: Crosland Southeast withdraws 123-acre data center rezoning in Matthews

opposition_groups: Stop Data Center Project Anthem Fair Oaks
summary: Atmoss is developing Project Anthem, an $800 million data center campus on 340 acres at the Fair Oaks Industrial Park near 11th Street and the Creek Turnpike in east Tulsa. The developer (possibly Meta) also seeks to rezone 375 additional acres west of the site. Nearby residents opposed the project at planning meetings, citing noise, environmental, and property value concerns. Planning staff recommended denial of the expansion rezoning. Construction began in late 2025 despite neighborhood opposition.
objective: Oppose Atmoss data center over noise in Tulsa

opposition_groups: Muskogee County RWD #6 NO WATER FOR DATA CENTERS
summary: Google announced plans to build two data center campuses near Summit and Council Hill in Muskogee County as part of a $9 billion Oklahoma investment. Rural Water District #6 and local residents oppose the projects over water consumption concerns. The Council Hill campus anticipates ~20,000 gallons/day from the local water district. Indigenous environmental advocates warn that tech companies are targeting rural and indigenous communities because of available space and resources, often under NDAs that block elected officials from sharing details.
objective: Oppose Google data center over water in Muskogee County

company: Stream Data Centers / Headwaters Site Development
summary: Stream Data Centers and Headwaters Site Development proposed Project Saltworks, an 867-acre data center campus with 600+ MW capacity between FM 61 and FM 209 in Young County. Concerned Citizens of Young County organized meetings and public outreach, with a petition gathering 900+ signatures in one week. Headwaters has not yet identified an end-user but is in discussions with Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta, expecting to announce by end of 2026 with construction in 2027.
objective: Petition to block Stream Data Centers data center in Young County

opposition_groups: Citizens United for Responsible Growth in Leon County
summary: Belltown Power proposed the Kahla Project, a 1,500 MW data center on private property near Marquez in Leon County. The $15 billion project aims to complete half by 2026 and the rest by 2027. Nearly 200 residents packed a meeting in Jewett to oppose the project, citing health impacts, livestock effects, noise, lighting, public safety, and water use. Leon County has 11 endangered species. Belltown Power has declined invitations to attend public meetings. The grassroots opposition group Citizens United for Responsible Growth (CURG) organized the community response.
objective: Mobilize opposition to Belltown Power data center in Leon County

summary: AVAIO Digital signed an agreement with the Appomattox County EDA for a 452-acre site at the Appomattox Business Center, planning a $3 billion, 300 MW data center campus (Project Hercules). Initial phase delivers 130 MW in H1 2026. Residents organized as 'Appomattox Data Center Watch,' raising concerns about lack of public input, water supply (county purchases water from Campbell County under a contract expiring 2029), and skepticism about promised benefits. The EDA scheduled a public information session for March 23, 2026.
objective: Rally against AVAIO Digital data center in Appomattox County

opposition_groups: Southwest Virginia Data Center Transparency Alliance
summary: Google proposed a data center campus in Botetourt County that could use 2-8 million gallons of drinking water per day from Carvins Cove via the Western Virginia Water Authority. Residents organized through the Southwest Virginia Data Center Transparency Alliance, demanding project cancellation. Google tried to keep water usage figures secret, but a Roanoke Circuit Court judge ruled the information is public, stating 'there are few resources more precious than water.' The WVWA is weighing infrastructure changes to accommodate the project. Residents raised concerns about environmental impact, infrastructure strain, and lack of public input.
objective: Community pressure forces Google to withdraw in Botetourt County

summary: Takanock proposed Project Tallmadge, a 2-million-sq-ft data center on 87 acres in Strasburg's industrial park near I-81 Exit 296, with an $8 billion capital investment. The land is zoned industrial (by-right use). Residents questioned water use, noise, and long-term oversight at a March 2025 public meeting. Shenandoah County is drafting its first data center regulations focusing on water use, noise control, and land protections, with the county's reliance on small-town water systems and private wells making it vulnerable to high-volume industrial users. Construction potentially commencing late 2027.
objective: Rally against Takanock data center in Strasburg

summary: Monroe County residents organized opposition to potential data center development. The Monroe County Commission denied ongoing data center discussions, but acknowledged that recent West Virginia state legislation (2025) removed local authority over certified data center projects, limiting the Commission's ability to regulate or influence them. The Commission encouraged residents to contact state legislators. The state legislative session ending March 14, 2026 further increased data center development powers while reducing local control, despite overwhelming public opposition.
objective: Rally against data center in Monroe County

summary: KBC Advisors, a Seattle-based commercial real estate company with ties to Amazon and Microsoft, signed a letter of intent to purchase 62 acres at SouthPointe Business Enterprise Campus for a 'Class A logistics facility.' Residents suspect the project is actually a data center and organized as 'No Data Center In Sheboygan,' collecting 5,000 signatures opposing any data center in the area. City officials deny data center plans, but residents point to KBC's past work with tech companies as a red flag, noting KBC could assign the property to other businesses. The Common Council approved the land deal despite opposition.
objective: Oppose KBC Advisors data center in Sheboygan

opposition_groups: Raton Data Center Hub MOU Discussion
summary: Raton City Commission voted 3-1 on February 10, 2026 to approve a six-month MOU with Atterix for a feasibility study on an 'Advanced Digital Hub Infrastructure Project' at the former Kmart building, despite citizen pushback. Residents demanded transparency about Atterix and the MOU terms. City officials clarified the MOU is only for a feasibility study, not construction approval, and includes a request not to divulge proprietary information shared during the six-month study period. The Facebook discussion group has a neutral stance, focused on monitoring the MOU process.
objective: Oppose Atterix data center in Raton

summary: Meta purchased 328 acres near the Amazon facility in Davenport for a two-building data center campus. The Iowa Economic Development Authority approved Meta's application and tax incentives. Over a year later, the land remains empty with no construction timeline announced. Environmental activists and residents raised concerns about electricity usage (potentially enough to power a million homes), water consumption, few permanent jobs, and potential utility cost increases. The broader Quad Cities area already hosts two data centers.
objective: Oppose Meta data center over water in Davenport

summary: Watts Township in Perry County drafted an amended zoning ordinance that would include data centers. Residents organized as 'Concerned Citizens of Watts Township' (Facebook group: 'Preserve Watts Township Perry County') to oppose potential data center development, part of the broader wave of Pennsylvania grassroots resistance to the 50+ data centers proposed statewide.
objective: Organize against data center zoning in Watts Township

summary: Google's Project Zodiac data center campus on Adams Center Road in Fort Wayne was proposed in October 2023 and broke ground in April 2024. The campus spans nearly 900 acres (700+ annexed into Fort Wayne). Phase 1 became operational in December 2025. Phase 2 expansion to fill 2.5 acres of regulated wetlands was approved by IDEM despite over 100 residents showing up to oppose it. Opposition centers on wetland destruction, 143 additional diesel generators, rising energy costs, air quality, noise, and lack of public hearings. The 'Stop FW Data Center Campus' Facebook group has organized community opposition.
objective: Oppose Google-backed data center over noise in Fort Wayne

summary: Logistix Realty proposed a data center near Frankfort, first petitioning to rezone land within city limits (withdrawn July 2025), then filing a new request in December 2025 to rezone 715 acres of agricultural land to light industrial in unincorporated Clinton County. The Area Plan Commission sent a 'no recommendation' to commissioners. On January 20, 2026, commissioners voted unanimously 3-0 to deny the rezoning, citing resident opposition and concerns about water, electricity, noise, farmland loss, and lack of an identified end-user. A 12-month waiting period now applies. The 'Clinton County Indiana-NO DATA CENTERS' Facebook group mobilized opposition including yard sign campaigns.
objective: Petition to block Logistix Realty data center in Clinton County

summary: Jumping Bison LLC proposed a 550-acre hyperscale data center campus near Greensburg. The City Council approved a 10-year, 60% real property tax abatement in December 2024 and annexed 340 acres. The Coalition to Protect Decatur County Against AI Data Center formed in response, launching a Change.org petition that gathered over 950 signatures. Opposition centers on lack of transparency about environmental and health risks, farmland loss, and water/power impacts. The fight has attracted statewide support from other anti-data-center groups.
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Decatur County Against AI Data Center
objective: 950-signature petition opposing Jumping Bison data center

summary: Pinnacle Properties, representing landowner Christine Truee Wilhelm, sought to rezone 247 acres of farmland on County Road 23 in Bristol for a data center campus of 800,000 to 1.2 million sq ft with an estimated $1B investment. The Bristol planning commission initially rejected the proposal in January 2024. A second application was submitted and the Town Council voted 5-1 in September 2024 to approve the rezoning despite heated opposition. The 'Bristol United: No Data Center' Facebook group organized residents concerned about noise, light pollution, farmland loss, well water impacts, and rising power costs.
objective: Oppose Pinnacle Properties data center over water in Elkhart County

summary: Microsoft announced a $1B data center for the City of LaPorte, approved in 2024 but not yet broken ground. While the city project has some support (15% of property taxes directed to schools), broader county opposition has grown significantly. Commissioner Joe Haney publicly took a stance against data centers, citing massive electricity demands, water consumption, light pollution, and diesel generators. A Fortune 100 company began purchasing farmland for potential data center development in unincorporated county areas, prompting officials to move to restrict where data centers can be built. The 'Say No to Data Centers in LaPorte County' Facebook group (1,155 members) has organized opposition.
opposition_groups: Say No to Data Centers in LaPorte County
objective: Organize coalition against Microsoft data center in LaPorte County

summary: Prologis proposed annexing and rezoning 429 acres east of the SR 44 and I-74 interchange from agricultural to industrial for a data center campus of up to 13 buildings across three phases. After a five-hour meeting on January 7, 2026, the Shelbyville Plan Commission gave an 'unfavorable' recommendation. Over 6,700 people signed a petition organized by the 'Shelby County citizens against data centers' group (2,400 Facebook members). Residents cited concerns about noise, water usage, and tax incentives. The City Council subsequently tabled the annexation vote. This was at least the third Indiana data center rejection in four months.
opposition_groups: Shelby County citizens against data centers
objective: Petition to block Prologis data center in Shelby County

summary: Sourecolo, a Lehi, Utah-based company, attended the Warrick County Area Plan Commission meeting on December 8, 2025 to present general information about data centers. No specific project has been formally proposed yet, but the presentation sparked immediate opposition from a Facebook group calling for concerned residents to attend the meeting. Opponents warn about electricity consumption driving up rates for residents and small businesses. The 'Warrick County Data Center Discussion' group has 778 members.
objective: Oppose Sourecolo data center impact on rates

summary: Cornerstone Alliance marketed a site in Benton Township, Berrien County for data center development, with a proposed $3 billion project. Benton Township's water plant alone lacks capacity, requiring Benton Harbor's struggling water system. Berrien County is in nonattainment for ozone. The county administrator stated he was not aware of any active developer. The 'Flip the Switch Berrien County MI' Facebook group (316 members) formed to oppose data center development in the county. Environmentalists warn of high costs to water, climate, and community despite the economic development promise.
opposition_groups: Flip the Switch Berrien County MI
objective: Organize coalition against data center in Berrien County

summary: Alterra Development proposes the Marshall Technology & Energy Center in Brooks Industrial Park, including a data center and gas power plant near Stuart Lake and the Brooks Nature Area. The Local Development Finance Authority approved a purchase agreement for 200+ acres in October 2025 (unanimous vote). Residents were blindsided, with at least 30 attending a meeting where the project was not even on the agenda. The 'Stop the CCP Ford Marshall Megasite' Facebook group (1,819 members) and EDRA of Michigan organized opposition. Concerns center on wetland impacts, noise, electricity and water use, and few permanent jobs. The project is in early due diligence (6-12 months).
opposition_groups: Stop the CCP Ford Marshall Megasite
objective: Organize coalition against Alterra Development data center in Calhoun County

summary: Hyperscale Data (parent of Alliance Cloud Services) operates a 30 MW data center in Dowagiac and plans expansion to 70 MW by 2027 and 340 MW by 2029 -- enough energy to power 100,000+ homes. Residents packed City Hall and organized a rally demanding a moratorium on data center expansion. The Dowagiac City Council denied a moratorium petition but unanimously passed a resolution to update industrial zoning ordinances, particularly regarding noise pollution. Cass County commissioners passed a separate resolution urging more transparency in data center development. The 'Dowagiac AI DATA CENTER' Facebook group (309 members) organized opposition.
objective: Petition to block Hyperscale Data data center in Cass County

summary: Rocklocker LLC (headed by Traverse City geologist Matt Rine) proposed a gigawatt-scale hyperscale data center on 1,440 acres of state-owned land near Island Lake and South River roads, with its own natural gas power plant and carbon capture. The project initially had county commission and economic development support but encountered overwhelmingly negative public feedback. Residents cited concerns about impacts to the Boardman River and other cold water streams, use of public land, water and electricity consumption. The 'Kalkaska Citizens Against the Proposed Data Center' Facebook group (487 members) organized opposition. Rine withdrew the proposal in November 2025, citing threats and community opposition.
opposition_groups: Kalkaska Citizens Against the Proposed Data Center
objective: Community pressure forces Rocklocker to withdraw in Kalkaska County

summary: The Piqua Commission approved a $1B data center development agreement with J5 LLC (dba Shaytura LLC) on November 3, 2025 after a 4+ hour meeting, with the developer planning two 350,000 sq ft buildings at the I-75 Exit 78 Business and Industrial Park. Residents expressed strong frustration over the waived three-reading rule and emergency designation. Concerns included temporary construction jobs vs. few permanent ones, air and noise pollution, water supply threats to the Great Miami River, and data centers' massive water consumption. The 'Piqua Citizens Against the Data Centers' Facebook group (171 members) and a Change.org petition ('Save Piqua!') organized opposition.
opposition_groups: Piqua Citizens Against the Data Centers
objective: Petition to block J5 (Shaytura) data center in Miami County

summary: Developers showed interest in approximately 450 acres of industrial land off Township Road 93 in Woodville Township. Danberry National Ltd. served as the realtor, with agents offering landowners $100,000 per acre. Residents from Gibsonburg, Woodville Township, and Madison Township pushed back, but no developer attended a township meeting meant for public discussion. No formal rezoning request or resolution had been filed as of late 2025. Any zoning changes would require public hearings and multiple levels of approval. The 'Woodville & Gibsonburg Data Center' Facebook group (843 members) organized opposition.
objective: Organize coalition against data center in Sandusky County

summary: Panattoni Development (California-based) purchased 100 acres of industrial-zoned land on Faircrest Street SW in Perry Township, Stark County, planning four buildings (two at 218,000 sq ft, two at 160,000 sq ft) with completion targeted for 2027. Microsoft was previously interested but withdrew in October 2024. The project is paused due to outstanding conditions with Ohio EPA, the regional chamber, and utilities. Canton and Perry Township are at an impasse over a JEDD contract with a 50-year anti-annexation clause. Residents voiced opposition at a March 2026 trustees meeting citing noise, air pollution, electric costs, water use, and lack of transparency. The 'Stop the Data Center Construction in Perry Township' Facebook group (471 members) organized opposition.
opposition_groups: Stop the Data Center Construction in Perry Township
objective: Community pressure forces Panattoni Development to withdraw in Perry Township

summary: Washington County commissioners signed an NDA and letter of intent in January 2026 with an unnamed developer for a potential data center in Waterford. Commissioner Schilling read a letter of intent supporting the developer and offering a 100% tax abatement for 15+ years. Residents packed commissioners' meetings demanding transparency, criticizing the NDA as unethical. Concerns include rising electric rates, aquifer depletion, farmland loss, and few jobs. Ohio House Bill 695 was introduced to ban local officials from signing data center NDAs. As of March 2026, no public meeting had been scheduled despite continued pressure. The 'No Waterford Data Center (Ohio)' Facebook group (64 members) organized opposition.
objective: Oppose NDA-shrouded data center proposal in Waterford

summary: Crawford County Board of Commissioners passed a 6-month (180-day) moratorium on data center permits on January 6, 2026, by a unanimous 5-0 vote, on the recommendation of Planning & Zoning. County attorney Joel Sherlock stated data center development is 'way more complicated than anything Crawford County has ever seen' and the county lacks infrastructure, rules, and procedures for processing applications. No data center developer had approached the county -- this is a preemptive moratorium. The 'Crawford County Community in Action' Facebook group (22 members) supported the measure.
objective: Impose 6-month data centers moratorium in Crawford County

summary: Two major data center projects are proposed in Floyd County. Atlas Development purchased 100 acres of the former Battey Complex (NW Georgia Regional Hospital site) from the Rome-Floyd Development Authority for $5.7M, planning a 2.395M sq ft, 7-building campus with rezoning to heavy industrial approved by Floyd County Commission on May 27, 2025 (over a 4-3 planning commission recommendation). Microsoft separately purchased 347 acres on Huffaker Road in 2023 for $1B. The 'Oppose Atlas Data Center Battey' group gathered 440+ Change.org signatures and filed Open Meetings Act complaints with the GA Attorney General. The Floyd County Libertarians also condemned the commission's decision. Concerns include noise, rising power rates, use of taxpayer-purchased land, and lack of permanent jobs.
opposition_groups: Stop Floyd County Hwy 53 Data Center
We say NO! to Atlas Development
objective: Petition to block Atlas Development, Microsoft data center in Floyd County

summary: A 'No Data Center Paulding County Georgia' Facebook group (27 members) formed in response to concerns about potential data center development in the county. Paulding County is part of the broader wave of Georgia communities pushing back against data center expansion, as the state grapples with growing opposition that led to moratoriums in at least 8 municipalities and multiple state bills. Specific project details for Paulding County are limited.
opposition_groups: No Data Center Paulding County Georgia
objective: Organize coalition against data center in Paulding County

summary: San Francisco-based Crane Data Centers is building a two-phase campus at 3975 Heather St. in Forest Grove, on a 35-acre property zoned light industrial since 1980, bordered by residential homes on two sides. Phase 1 (180,000 sq ft, 40 ft tall) began construction July 2025 targeting 2026 completion, starting at 8 MW and capping at 47 MW. Crane will fund all improvements to existing substations and installation of new lines — Forest Grove Light & Power Director Keith Hormann confirmed safeguards are in place to ensure local ratepayers won't experience rate hikes or be on the hook for power system upgrades. Phase 2 would add 363,000 sq ft at 75 feet tall, bringing the site to ~100 MW, but requires additional approvals. Neighbors raised concerns about noise and light pollution, traffic congestion, property values, and disruption to wetlands and migratory bird paths. Forest Grove City Council approved in October 2024. Air-based cooling limits water consumption to typical plumbing needs.
objective: Oppose Crane Data Centers campus adjacent to residential homes

summary: Boone Town Council unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on data center and crypto mining development on March 23, 2026. Watauga County may follow with a public hearing on April 21.
objective: Pause data centers and crypto mining in Town of Boone pending regulations
